Title: digital-strategy-for-schools-to-2027.pdf
Content: digital-strategy-for-schools-to-2027.pdf I am delighted we have this vision, which acknowledges the enormous strides already made and sets out our ambitions for even greater progress, to continue the transformation of our learners’ experiences with technology. I would like to thank sincerely everyone who participated in such numbers and with great energy in the consultation process: students, parents, teachers, principals, education partners, representatives from industry and other interested bodies. Your generosity and enthusiasm in sharing your experiences, views and hopes for the future yielded rich and varied contributions, which have been essential in informing the approaches outlined in this Strategy. The need for digital skills is ever­present in our daily lives. To answer this need, it is vital that our education system supports all of our learners – children, young people and beyond – to gain the knowledge and skills they need in the modern world and to ensure that they have a positive experience of learning with technology. This Strategy aims to ensure this happens effectively for all learners. The Strategy outlines how we will ensure that all elements of our education system are proactively involved in progressing the use of digital technology in education. It takes a three pillar approach, covering embedding digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment; digital technology infrastructure and policy, research and digital leadership. Each of these three pillars is essential to the success of the Strategy, and I am convinced that by delivering it in full we will be equipping our students for the future, with the skills and capacity they need to be effective as global citizens in a digital world, in the way to which we aspire. Learners are central to this Strategy. No one should be left behind and it is critical that all learners are provided with meaningful opportunities to benefit from new technologies to support their education in an environment that supports and delivers modern teaching practices. By combining excellence in teaching practises with technology we can equip or students and young people with the key digital skills required to enhance and enrich their pathway through life. Through continued investment we will ensure that all schools, regardless of location, are provided with appropriate broadband connectivity. We will continue to invest in supporting schools to build their own capacity and digital technology infrastructure. 4 [v5] DS For SCHOOLS 2021 to 2027 (s) P2.qxp_DIGITAL STRATEGY For SCHOOLS 12/04/2022 12:38 Page 5 Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 The effective use of digital technology, integrated as a seamless part of the teaching, learning and assessment practice in every classroom, is a key goal. There is much to learn from the challenges that COVID­19 brought to the school system. It is remarkable how, in the midst of unprecedented challenges, school leaders and teachers during this time demonstrated some amazing innovation and ingenuity, using technology in new ways to support students’ learning. There also grew for many a new appreciation of how digital technology can benefit the teaching and learning process and how it helped everyone to stay engaged and connected during the school closures necessitated by the pandemic. Many studies also found that schools had already successfully engaged with digital technology and were well placed to respond with agility to the changed landscape. Rather than stand still in this ever changing digital world it is important to continue to evolve with technology and this Strategy recognises the need to support schools, school leaders and teachers to build their capacity to adopt new approaches that will benefit all learners, in turn creating the entrepreneurs, engineers and educators of tomorrow.

Title: digital-strategy-for-schools-to-2027.pdf
Content: digital-strategy-for-schools-to-2027.pdf Many studies also found that schools had already successfully engaged with digital technology and were well placed to respond with agility to the changed landscape. Rather than stand still in this ever changing digital world it is important to continue to evolve with technology and this Strategy recognises the need to support schools, school leaders and teachers to build their capacity to adopt new approaches that will benefit all learners, in turn creating the entrepreneurs, engineers and educators of tomorrow. Over the lifetime of this Strategy, progressing the use of digital technology and innovation will be a key enabler to deliver curriculum reform. Empowering school communities and building capacity will support progression of both primary curriculum reform, including the Modern Foreign Language initiative, and Senior Cycle reform. The digital landscape is a dynamic and changing environment. The Strategy indicates the need to build awareness of new and emerging technologies and how we must be prepared to take advantage of the benefits and opportunities they bring, The strategic objectives of the EU Digital Education Action Plan include to develop a ‘digital education ecosystem’. To this end the Strategy will enable us to continue to support school leaders and teachers through effective initial teacher education and ongoing teacher professional learning. This will support them to develop essential skills and empower them to gain the maximum advantage from digital technology in every classroom, for the benefit of all learners. The capacity of digital technology to support differentiated teaching and learning will facilitate stronger personalisation in teaching approaches to benefit all learners but particularly those with additional educational needs. In an ever­changing world, we are increasingly connected and we increasingly collaborate effectively using technology. Delivering on this Strategy for our learners will require ongoing connection and collaboration. I look forward to working in partnership with all education partners and our colleagues across Government to advance the development of digital technology in how we deliver education in our schools and achieving the objectives contained in this Strategy. Norma Foley TD Minister for Education 5 [v5] DS For SCHOOLS 2021 to 2027 (s) P2.qxp_DIGITAL STRATEGY For SCHOOLS 12/04/2022 12:38 Page 6 Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 Foreword by the Secretary General I am delighted to introduce the Department’s Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 which sets out our policy approach to continue the progress made in embedding digital technology across the curriculum in our schools. In the midst of the Covid­19 pandemic the benefits and opportunities afforded by the use of digital technology in education were brought into sharp focus. Digital technologies facilitated the continuity of learning during school closures but also highlighted the challenges that exist for some learners. The work advanced under the previous Digital Strategy for Schools and the associated funding which allowed schools to invest in digital infrastructure was a significant strength in being able to respond when the pandemic impacted directly on schools in March 2020. This called for innovation and agility across the system to continue to deliver a vast range of education services and support learners. It is important that we maintain the momentum gained during Covid­19 in the adoption of digital technology in education and continue to invest in building an effective digital ecosystem in our schools. This Digital Strategy will support the ongoing advancement of digital infrastructure through targeted funding, and working with colleagues across Government to ensure broadband connectivity to all schools, regardless of location, growing their capacity in the use of digital technology.

Title: digital-strategy-for-schools-to-2027.pdf
Content: digital-strategy-for-schools-to-2027.pdf This called for innovation and agility across the system to continue to deliver a vast range of education services and support learners. It is important that we maintain the momentum gained during Covid­19 in the adoption of digital technology in education and continue to invest in building an effective digital ecosystem in our schools. This Digital Strategy will support the ongoing advancement of digital infrastructure through targeted funding, and working with colleagues across Government to ensure broadband connectivity to all schools, regardless of location, growing their capacity in the use of digital technology. We must also continue to support our school leaders to lead innovation and change in adopting digital solutions, embedding the use of digital technology to deliver curriculum in every classroom and for every learner. We know that our teaching workforce responded very effectively to the changed education landscape in recent years and we must now continue to support them with targeted teacher professional learning, building on and progressing the skills gained so as to support all learners to gain key digital skills as part of their educational journey. Digital skills are key to young learners’ future transition to Further and Higher education and into the world of work. They must be afforded the opportunities to acquire those skills as part of their foundational education. Essential to this aspiration is the need for young learners to learn how to make the most of the opportunities available from the use of digital technology in their learning but also to navigate the internet safely. The voice of young learners will be important to inform and advance curricular change for which digital technology will be a key enabler. 6 [v5] DS For SCHOOLS 2021 to 2027 (s) P2.qxp_DIGITAL STRATEGY For SCHOOLS 12/04/2022 12:38 Page 7 Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 I would like to express my sincere thanks to all of those who were part of the development of this Strategy. We engaged with learners, parents/guardians, teachers, principals, initial teacher education providers, industry representatives, non­governmental organisations such as those in the disability and charity sector and the education partners. It is important that all voices were provided with an opportunity to be heard. Contributions from our education partners, colleagues in our agencies and other Governments Departments, the industry sector and learners themselves helped to shape this strategy. We will continue to engage with our many partners and stakeholders to ensure that we deliver on the objectives contained in the Strategy. This will include continuing to develop an appropriate digital infrastructure and digitally skilled workforce, to realise the benefits of digital technology for all our learners to gain the skills they need to navigate the digital world effectively and safely. It sets out the high level objectives under three Pillars. Pillar 1 Supporting the embedding of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment ­ Supporting our teachers to strengthen their confidence and competence in using digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment in schools, to ensure all learners develop the digital skills necessary to navigate a complex digital world. In tandem, supporting school leaders to guide and build capacity and increase teacher confidence through appropriate teacher professional learning (TPL), to ensure appropriate developmental learning experiences for all learners to enable them develop the necessary digital competence. This will be informed by learning from the experience of COVID­19 to better realise the effective use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment across the entire curriculum 16 [v5] DS For SCHOOLS 2021 to 2027 (s) P2.qxp_DIGITAL STRATEGY For SCHOOLS 12/04/2022 12:38 Page 17 Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 Pillar 2 Digital Technology Infrastructure ­ Commitment to build on the progress made to date to ensure that all schools are served with high speed broadband connectivity and appropriate infrastructure to support teachers and learners to realise the benefits from the use of digital technologies in classroom activities including technical support and procurement frameworks. Pillar 3 Looking to the future: policy, research and digital leadership ­ Establish sustainable policies to guide further progress for the use of digital technologies in schools, leveraging work underway in other relevant strategies to ensure that all learners develop their digital competence. Support schools and school leaders in terms of the ethical and safe use of the internet. The number of arbitrators shall be one.  3 Abstract The integration of digital technologies is reshaping education systems, offering both opportunities and challenges. In Ireland, the Department of Education and Youth has recognised the need for action to ensure inclusiveness and responsiveness in the digital age. To support this, the OECD Secretariat and Ireland’s Department of Education and Youth organised an international peer-learning event on 25 November 2024 in Dublin. This work is part of the project Resourcing School Education: Policies for the Digital Transformation of Education and Future-Readiness of Teachers. The event gathered experts from Estonia, France and Sweden to share experiences and strategies with Ireland’s Department of Education and Youth. Discussions focused on measuring the impact of digital tools, ensuring equitable access, and fostering digital competences among students and teachers. This document summarises the discussion and insights, structured in five sections: opportunities and challenges, measuring impact, access and competence, success factors and conclusions.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf Digital education initiatives in Sweden 33 BOXES Box 1. Balancing benefits and distractions, the dual impact of digital technologies on student learning 9 THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 6  Executive summary Digital technologies are transforming education systems, offering new opportunities but also posing challenges. To support its digital strategy, Ireland’s Department of Education and Youth partnered with the OECD Secretariat to host an international peer-learning event in Dublin (25 November 2024). Experts from Estonia, France, and Sweden shared experiences on the impact of digital tools on student learning. Informed by the peer-learning event, OECD evidence and national policy experiences, this document presents key insights across five areas: 1) opportunities and challenges, 2) measuring impact, 3) ensuring quality and equity, 4) key success factors, and 5) conclusions and main considerations. Opportunities and challenges. PISA 2022 shows that moderate use of digital tools for learning is linked to better academic outcomes and a stronger sense of belonging. Irish students perform above average in core subjects and are confident in using digital platforms. Yet, distractions remain a concern, and equity gaps persist. Participants agreed that successful digital integration requires clear pedagogical intent, support for teachers, and targeted policy measures. Measuring impact. Measuring the effects of digital tools on learning is complex. While PISA and ICILS provide useful data, they do not offer conclusions regarding causality. Countries are thus turning to mixed methods, including surveys, classroom observation, longitudinal research, and RCTs. Estonia’s DigiEfekt study highlights that the type of digital use matters more than frequency. France’s EduPilote and Sweden’s national efforts aim to connect technology use with academic outcomes. Ireland’s Digital Learning Framework provides a foundation for progress, and there is potential to strengthen it with more robust monitoring tools and data-driven approaches Ensuring quality, competence and equity. Digital education must address issues related to infrastructure, teacher capacity, and equity. Ireland’s Digital Strategy, DEIS programme, and ICT Grant Scheme target these issues. Teacher training is supported through Oide, and digital skills are included in initial teacher education. France offers digital skills certification via Pix, Estonia promotes coding through ProgeTiger, and Sweden supports local innovation, despite regional disparities. Ireland’s Webwise programme also fosters digital citizenship and safety. Key factors for success. Participants highlighted key enablers: • Equitable access to technology • Purposeful pedagogical integration • Strong teacher training • Stakeholder engagement • Monitoring and evaluation Ireland’s strategy aligns with these principles, but there are opportunities to enhance data use and evaluation of learning outcomes to further strengthen its approach. Final considerations. Ireland is making progress in digital education. To maximise impact, it should continue to improve monitoring systems, ensure equitable access and support teachers. Experiences from other countries might assist Ireland in this endeavour. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  7 1 Opportunities and challenges of digital technologies in education: OECD and Ireland’s overview During the event, participants discussed the integration of digital technologies in teaching and learning worldwide, sparking considerable debate regarding their impact on student learning outcomes. As a basis for this discussion, the OECD team outlined some available evidence. Results from the 2022 edition of the Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA) highlight a complex relationship between digital device use and academic achievement (see Box 1). For example, PISA 2022 findings indicate that a moderate use of digital devices for educational purposes is associated with improved student performance and a stronger sense of belonging at school.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf As a basis for this discussion, the OECD team outlined some available evidence. Results from the 2022 edition of the Programme for International Student Assessment (PISA) highlight a complex relationship between digital device use and academic achievement (see Box 1). For example, PISA 2022 findings indicate that a moderate use of digital devices for educational purposes is associated with improved student performance and a stronger sense of belonging at school. Specifically, as shown in Figure 1, students who spend a moderate amount of time engaging with digital tools for learning activities tend to score better in mathematics compared to those who do not use these tools at all (OECD, 2024 ). This evidence indicates [1] the potential benefits of integrating digital technologies into educational settings, particularly when this is done judiciously, moderately, and with clear pedagogical intent1. Figure 1. Students score better in mathematics when they spend moderate time using devices for learning at school but not when engaging in leisure activities Based on students’ reports (OECD average). Note: Differences between categories are all statistically significant. Source: OECD, PISA 2022 Database, https://webfs.oecd.org/pisa/PIF_124_Figures_Tables.xlsx. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 8  Disaggregating the data by gender, socio-economic status, and immigrant background reveals further insights. Firstly, boys tend to spend more time on digital devices for leisure than girls, which may contribute to differences in academic performance. Secondly, students from lower socio-economic backgrounds often have less access to high-quality digital resources, potentially exacerbating educational inequalities (OECD, 2024 ). Finally, students with an immigrant background report higher levels of distraction from digital [2] devices compared to their native peers. Thus, in line with PISA 2022 data, the OECD team highlighted that it is important to secure a balance as digital tools are introduced in education. While they offer substantial opportunities for enhancing learning experiences, there is a critical need for policies that mitigate potential distractions and promote effective use. This includes implementing guidelines for responsible digital device usage, providing high-quality digital content and materials and ensuring equitable access to technology across different socio-economic groups. Additionally, equipping educators with the necessary skills to integrate technology effectively into their teaching practices is crucial for maximising the benefits of education technologies. The OECD team highlighted that it is important to secure a balance as digital tools are introduced in education. While they offer substantial opportunities for enhancing learning experiences, there is a critical need for policies that mitigate potential distractions and promote effective use. The OECD team also gave an overview of Ireland’s PISA 2022 results, highlighting how they provide relevant insights into the opportunities and challenges associated with the use of digital technologies in education. Ireland’s academic performance in PISA 2022 was above average, with students achieving high reading scores compared to both EU and OECD countries. Irish students also performed significantly above average in science and mathematics. Although Ireland’s performance was above the OECD average, participants in the event pointed out that challenges remain in integrating digital technologies in Irish education. In the context of the COVID-19 pandemic and its related shift to remote learning, Irish students reported high levels of confidence in using digital platforms for remote learning – e.g., navigating learning management systems and finding online resources – which reflects their adaptability during this period (OECD, 2023 ). However, they were less confident about managing their own motivation and focus [3] without external prompts. According to PISA 2022, nearly one in three 15-year-olds across OECD countries are in classes where students get distracted by using digital devices during mathematics lessons.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf In the context of the COVID-19 pandemic and its related shift to remote learning, Irish students reported high levels of confidence in using digital platforms for remote learning – e.g., navigating learning management systems and finding online resources – which reflects their adaptability during this period (OECD, 2023 ). However, they were less confident about managing their own motivation and focus [3] without external prompts. According to PISA 2022, nearly one in three 15-year-olds across OECD countries are in classes where students get distracted by using digital devices during mathematics lessons. In Ireland, 20% of 15-year-olds are in classes where students are distracted by their own device use, and 16% where they are distracted by others’ use. These figures are below the OECD averages (30% and 25% respectively) (OECD, 2023 ). This trend reflects a broader need to develop strategies that promote [4] self-directed learning skills alongside technological competence (OECD, 2023 ; OECD, 2023 ). In [3] [4] addition, during this time, disparities in access to quality educational materials and digital devices between socio-economically advantaged and disadvantaged schools persisted. Addressing these inequities is crucial to ensure all students benefit equally from digital tools (OECD, 2023 ). [4] Further, during the initial discussion, Teacher Professional Learning emerged as a critical factor in Ireland's efforts to integrate digital technologies in education. In this regard, participants in the event stressed that equipping teachers with the skills to effectively use and teach through digital technologies is essential for successful integration into classrooms. In particular, the participants from Ireland reported that significant progress has been made under initiatives such as Ireland’s Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 and its associated Implementation Plan, which includes substantial investments in infrastructure and THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  9 professional development (Department of Education and Youth, 2022 ). However, some challenges – [5] e.g., addressing digital skills and literacy in the curriculum, managing distractions, ensuring equitable access to resources and fostering self-directed learning – remain. Finally, the discussion highlighted that addressing these challenges through targeted policies and investments will be crucial for optimising the potential of digital tools to support student achievement. Box 1. Balancing benefits and distractions, the dual impact of digital technologies on student learning Digital education technologies impact student learning in several areas. Firstly, they can enhance academic performance when integrated into the curriculum with clear educational objectives. Further, students using digital devices for learning can access a wide range of resources, fostering engagement and potentially improving outcomes. However, nearly one in three 15-year-olds are in classes where students get distracted by using digital devices in OECD countries, and the use of digital devices for leisure, especially during school hours, often correlates with distractions and lower academic performance. This is particularly true when students engage in non-educational activities such as social media browsing or gaming. Finally, digital education technologies influence classroom dynamics and student well-being. They provide opportunities for interactive learning and collaboration but also pose challenges like increased distraction and anxiety related to device dependency. Effective management of digital device use in educational settings is crucial to maximise their benefits while minimising potential drawbacks. Source: OECD (2024), “Managing screen time: How to protect and equip students against distraction”, PISA in Focus, No. 124, OECD Publishing, Paris, https://doi.org/10.1787/7c225af4-en. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 10  2 Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education After the introductory session, the second part of the event focused on the measurement of the impact of digital technologies in education.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf Effective management of digital device use in educational settings is crucial to maximise their benefits while minimising potential drawbacks. Source: OECD (2024), “Managing screen time: How to protect and equip students against distraction”, PISA in Focus, No. 124, OECD Publishing, Paris, https://doi.org/10.1787/7c225af4-en. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 10  2 Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education After the introductory session, the second part of the event focused on the measurement of the impact of digital technologies in education. The complexity of this issue was a key point of discussion during the event, as participants highlighted that assessing the impact of digital technologies in education is a complex and ongoing process. As stated by several authors (Golden, 2020 ; Hallie Preskill et al., 2013 ), policy [6] [7] evaluation in education entails understanding complex contexts and relationships within education systems, as well as the specific reforms being implemented. Part of this involves the use of indicator-based frameworks, which allow policymakers to assess the availability, use, and outcomes of, in this case, digital technologies in schools. These frameworks track metrics such as ICT infrastructure (e.g., device-to-student ratios and internet access), usage patterns in classrooms, and learning outcomes like academic performance and digital skills. For instance, international testing frameworks like PISA offer data and overviews of students’ ability to solve complex problems, think critically, and communicate effectively. The same applies for the testing framework of ICILS (International Computer and Information Literacy Study) in relation to students’ computer and information literacy (CIL) and computational thinking (CT)2. However, while these indicators provide valuable insights into usage patterns and correlations, they do not establish causality or fully capture the nuanced effects of digital technologies on educational outcomes (see Box 1). Because of this, some authors (Golden, 2020 ; Hallie Preskill et al., 2013 ; Daniel L. Stufflebeam, 2001 ) stress the need [6] [7] [8] for more comprehensive evaluation strategies that integrate both quantitative and qualitative methods to fully understand the complexities of educational reforms. Qualitative and mixed methods assessments – such as interviews or focus groups with teachers, students, and parents – provide crucial context for understanding how digital technologies are perceived and used in practice. Surveys and classroom observations, for example, can complement self-assessment data by offering additional insights into how digital technologies are used and how their use correlates with student engagement and teacher practices (Joint Research Centre, 2023 ). These methods allow policymakers [9] to consider stakeholder needs, identify implementation barriers, and, ultimately, find ways to address them. Though these data sources can help policymakers track trends and correlations, to assess actual impact and causality mechanisms, additional research approaches – such as experimental designs (e.g., Randomised Control Trials) and longitudinal studies – are required. Randomised Control Trials (RCTs) allow policymakers to establish causal links between technology use and educational improvements by comparing outcomes between treatment and control groups (Cukurova and Luckin, 2019 ). Therefore, this type of study offers robust evidence to inform scalable policy decisions [10] and identify effective practices across diverse educational contexts (Golden, 2020 ). Longitudinal studies [6] allow policymakers to track the long-term effects of digital technologies on students’ learning trajectories, career readiness, and overall educational equity. This methodology allows to assess the sustainability of reforms as well as their impact over time (Golden, 2020 ). [6] THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  11 To assess actual impact and causality mechanisms, […] research approaches – such as longitudinal studies or experimental designs (e.g., Randomised Control Trials) – are required.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf Longitudinal studies [6] allow policymakers to track the long-term effects of digital technologies on students’ learning trajectories, career readiness, and overall educational equity. This methodology allows to assess the sustainability of reforms as well as their impact over time (Golden, 2020 ). [6] THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  11 To assess actual impact and causality mechanisms, […] research approaches – such as longitudinal studies or experimental designs (e.g., Randomised Control Trials) – are required. On the grounds of these strategies and regular evaluations, policymakers can monitor progress and adjust policies based on evidence. By using these methodologies, policymakers can gather robust evidence to support informed decision-making, enabling governments to design policies that maximise the benefits of digital tools while addressing challenges such as inequity and misuse. This evidence-based approach ensures that investments in educational technology contribute meaningfully to achieving broader educational goals. To achieve this, comprehensive digital education strategies are essential in addition to these methods. In fact, strategies can guide policy efforts, as they set clear objectives for the integration of technologies with measurable targets (e.g., for infrastructure development, teacher education, professional learning, and student outcomes) (OECD, 2023 ). [11] The experiences of Estonia, France, Sweden and Ireland Estonia Estonia3 is widely acknowledged as a pioneer in the integration of digital technologies into education, serving as a model for other countries. In 1996, Estonia launched the Tiger Leap programme (Tiigrihüpe), which was critical for the country’s digital policy (Tiger Leap Foundation, 2007 ). This ambitious initiative [12] aimed to digitise schools by providing them with robust technological infrastructure, promoting digital literacy, and enhancing teachers’ ability to effectively integrate technology into their teaching. The goal was to ensure that technology would support the achievement of learning outcomes as outlined in the national curriculum. By the early 2000s, all Estonian schools had internet access and computer labs, creating a strong foundation for the subsequent integration of digital tools (Digital Skills & Jobs Platform, 2022 ). Building on the Tiger Leap programme, Estonia will launch the AI Leap 2025 (TI-Hüpe 2025)4 [13] programme to provide students and teachers with free access to leading AI tools as well as equip them with the skills needed to use them effectively in education (e-Estonia, 2025 ). [14] Further, since 2014, Estonia’s national curriculum has included digital competence as a core objective, further embedding technology into education. This focus has been reinforced by frameworks such as the Lifelong Learning Strategy 2020 and the ongoing Education 2035 strategy, which stresses the role of technology in improving the quality and inclusiveness of education (Figure 2). Additionally, according to the expert from Estonia, the country has made efforts to combine its infrastructure investments with initiatives to boost digital skills. For instance, the ProgeTiger programme, launched in 2012, promotes coding, robotics, and other technological competencies among students and educators. The programme also supports continuous teacher training to ensure educators can effectively integrate digital tools into their teaching practices (Education Estonia, 2020 ). [15] Despite these advancements, Estonia faces challenges in measuring the direct impact of digital technologies on educational outcomes. As current metrics primarily capture access to digital technologies and digital competencies, there is a recognised need for longitudinal research and comprehensive metrics that link digital device usage to academic performance and critical thinking development (Education Estonia, 2024 ). [16] THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 12  Figure 2.

Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf The programme also supports continuous teacher training to ensure educators can effectively integrate digital tools into their teaching practices (Education Estonia, 2020 ). [15] Despite these advancements, Estonia faces challenges in measuring the direct impact of digital technologies on educational outcomes. As current metrics primarily capture access to digital technologies and digital competencies, there is a recognised need for longitudinal research and comprehensive metrics that link digital device usage to academic performance and critical thinking development (Education Estonia, 2024 ). [16] THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 12  Figure 2. Underlying principles and values of Estonia’s Education 2035 Strategy Source: Ministry of Education and Research (Estonia) (2021[17]), Education Strategy 2021–2035, https://www.hm.ee/sites/default/files/documents/2022-10/haridusvaldkonna_arengukava_2035_kinnittaud_vv_eng_0.pdf To address this gap, Estonia has initiated studies such as the DigiEfekt Study (2020–2023). Conducted by the University of Tartu, this research examines how digital technologies influence student outcomes in mathematics, science, and digital literacy (Education Estonia, 2024 ). Early findings suggest that lessons [16] leveraging interactive digital tools to connect prior knowledge with new concepts yield positive outcomes, whereas passive substitution of traditional tools with digital ones has limited effects (Education Estonia, 2020 ). Crucially, this study found that the nature of technology use is more influential than frequency [15] alone. Interactive or constructive uses of technology demonstrated positive effects on learning outcomes, while passive uses resulted in no or even negative effects depending on the specific learning outcomes measured. These findings underscore the importance of considering not just how often, but how effectively technology is used in educational settings to maximise its positive impact on student learning. Future research and assessments may, thus, need to focus more explicitly on the quality and effectiveness of technology integration to provide a more comprehensive picture of its impact on education. France In his intervention, the expert from France5 highlighted that his country has adopted a comprehensive, data-driven approach to integrating digital technologies in education. According to the expert, central to this strategy is EduPilote, a platform aiming to collect, aggregate, and analyse data on the use of digital tools and resources across public, lower secondary (11 to 15 years old) and upper secondary (15 to 18 years old) schools nationwide. As of April 2025, a functional prototype of the platform is being used at the Direction du Numérique pour l'Éducation (DNE)6. A first version shall be released to the public by the end of 2025. By leveraging features such as dynamic navigation and data visualisation tools, EduPilote will enable policymakers, local authorities, and educators to track and evaluate the impact of digital technologies on teaching and learning outcomes. This platform will, hence, provide detailed insights into device usage and engagement levels, facilitating informed decision-making tailored to the specific needs of individual schools and districts. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  13 Complementary to this upcoming platform, France’s “Territoires Numériques Éducatifs” (TNE) programme supports the pedagogical use of digital resources across 12 selected regions through four main goals: 1) train teachers; 2) equip classrooms; 3) make digital resources and tools available; and 4) support parents. The programme offers webinars, training opportunities, digital resources across a variety of digital related topics, as well as a variety of “educational digital solutions” (e.g., online libraries, learning language tools, etc.) for all educational levels (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale, 2024 ). [18] The French expert also mentioned that the 2015 “Digital Plan for Education” was pivotal in supporting the adoption of digital technologies at every educational level in France.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf The programme offers webinars, training opportunities, digital resources across a variety of digital related topics, as well as a variety of “educational digital solutions” (e.g., online libraries, learning language tools, etc.) for all educational levels (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale, 2024 ). [18] The French expert also mentioned that the 2015 “Digital Plan for Education” was pivotal in supporting the adoption of digital technologies at every educational level in France. According to the expert, this plan prioritised teacher training, the development of high-quality digital content, and the integration of technology into curricula to enhance teaching in primary and lower secondary schools. Complementing this effort, the ongoing “École Numérique Rurale (ENR)” programme provides funding for schools to acquire digital tools and resources. This programme has played a critical role in addressing disparities by ensuring equitable access to technology across both urban and rural areas (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale et de la Jeunesse, 2024 ). [19] To assess the effects of the 2015 Digital Plan for Education, the Direction de l’Évaluation de la Prospective et de la performance (DEPP) conducted the Élaine (i.e. the Longitudinal Evaluation of Activities related to Digital Education) study in 2017. The study tracked over 5,000 students in 7th and 8th grade and focused on the Plan’s deployment of mobile digital equipment in classrooms. Specifically, it assessed two initiatives: the “1-to-1 Tablets” initiative (for both in-school and at-home use) and the “Mobile Classrooms” (MC) initiative (for individual and shared use in the classroom only). Findings showed increased tablet access and usage in schools, alongside improvements in students’ digital, mathematical, and language (reading and oral comprehension) skills. The 1-to-1 Tablets programme yielded stronger educational benefits than the MC initiative. However, impacts varied by subject, grade level, and were most significant among students from low socio-economic status and in disadvantaged schools (Direction de L’Évaluation de la Prospective et de la performance (DEPP), 2017 ). [20] Currently, France’s “Digital Strategy for Education 2023–2027” – i.e. the official digital strategy developed by the then ministère de l'Éducation nationale et de la Jeunesse (now ministère de l’Éducation nationale, de l’Enseignement supérieur et de la Recherche) – provides a roadmap for integrating digital technology into the French education system for the 2023 to 2027 period. The strategy outlines the vision, priorities, and key actions set out to support digital transformation in French schools and to ensure that students and educators develop the digital skills needed in today’s world. Further, the strategy relies on four main axes: 1. A committed ecosystem in support of a shared public policy; 2. Teaching digital literacy to build citizenship and digital competence; 3. An educational community supported by a thoughtful, sustainable, and inclusive digital offering; 4. A renewed framework for a (ministerial) information system designed to serve its users (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale et de la Jeunesse, 2023 ). [21] Despite significant progress in France, challenges remain. Disparities in digital literacy levels among teachers and students persist, prompting targeted professional development initiatives and resource- sharing platforms to bridge these gaps. Additionally, systemic issues such as inconsistent internet connectivity in rural areas and growing cybersecurity risks continue to hinder full-scale implementation. Programmes like “Numérique Inclusif” aim to address these inequities by promoting broader access to digital tools and skills (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale et de la Jeunesse, 2024 ). [19] Sweden Sweden7 has long been recognised for its high level of digital inclusion in education, supported by widespread access to devices and robust digital infrastructure.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf Disparities in digital literacy levels among teachers and students persist, prompting targeted professional development initiatives and resource- sharing platforms to bridge these gaps. Additionally, systemic issues such as inconsistent internet connectivity in rural areas and growing cybersecurity risks continue to hinder full-scale implementation. Programmes like “Numérique Inclusif” aim to address these inequities by promoting broader access to digital tools and skills (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale et de la Jeunesse, 2024 ). [19] Sweden Sweden7 has long been recognised for its high level of digital inclusion in education, supported by widespread access to devices and robust digital infrastructure. In his intervention, the expert from Sweden brought forward that schools in the country began introducing computers into classrooms as early as the 1980s, and that authorities (local government or municipalities) have consistently invested in digital tools and platforms to support learning. Since 2017, the National Digitalisation Strategy for the School System THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 14  shapes the integration of digital resources in education with the aim of enhancing teaching quality and equipping students with critical digital competencies. The strategy focuses on three key areas: ensuring equal access to digital tools, providing adequate infrastructure, and promoting research on digitalisation's impact (Andric, 2023 ). [22] One of the key characteristics of Sweden’s digital education is its effort to emphasise critical thinking and media literacy. Recognising the prevalence of misinformation online, Swedish schools have incorporated media literacy into their curricula since 2018. This includes teaching students how to critically evaluate digital content and understand programming concepts in technology subjects. These efforts align with Sweden’s broader goal of preparing students to navigate an increasingly complex digital world (European Commission, 2024 ). [23] To build on its successes, Sweden is exploring ways to enhance data collection and analysis related to digital education. Initiatives aimed at linking device usage with academic performance metrics have been piloted under the National Digitalisation Strategy. These efforts seek to develop a more comprehensive understanding of how technology impacts classroom learning outcomes (Skolverket, 2022 ). This [24] experience underscores the importance of balancing innovation with rigorous evaluation to ensure that digital tools contribute meaningfully to student learning. Despite these efforts, Sweden has faced challenges in the systematically measuring the direct impact of digital technologies on learning outcomes. For example, it has been difficult to examine how digital competencies – assessed by studies as ICILS – translate to improved results in subjects like mathematics or science (Fraillon et al., 2020 ). Qualitative evidence from educators suggests that digital tools promote [25] student engagement, creativity, and collaboration. According to the expert, many teachers report using digital technologies to facilitate group projects, enable real-time feedback, and customise lessons to meet individual learning needs. However, systematic data collection linking these practices to measurable improvements in academic outcomes remains limited. This gap is also highlighted in reports published by the Swedish National Agency for Education (Skolverket). The reports also emphasise the need for enhanced data collection mechanisms (Skolverket, 2022 ). [24] According to the expert, Sweden also faces equity challenges in its digital education landscape. While most students have access to devices at school, disparities in digital literacy and home internet access persist, particularly among socio-economically disadvantaged groups. To address these gaps, the government has launched initiatives such as the provision of funding for schools to purchase devices and implement teacher training programmes on effective digital pedagogy.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf This gap is also highlighted in reports published by the Swedish National Agency for Education (Skolverket). The reports also emphasise the need for enhanced data collection mechanisms (Skolverket, 2022 ). [24] According to the expert, Sweden also faces equity challenges in its digital education landscape. While most students have access to devices at school, disparities in digital literacy and home internet access persist, particularly among socio-economically disadvantaged groups. To address these gaps, the government has launched initiatives such as the provision of funding for schools to purchase devices and implement teacher training programmes on effective digital pedagogy. Additionally, the "Skoldigistöd" initiative organised by the SALAR (Swedish Association of Local Authorities and Regions) supports equitable access to technology by helping schools procure and implement digital tools effectively (OECD, 2023, p. 299 ). [26] Based on the interventions from the different experts, several challenges are common when it comes to measuring the impact of digital technologies in education across Estonia, France, and Sweden. One significant issue is the lack of standardised metrics. There is a notable gap in measurements that go beyond access to digital technologies. While international assessments like ICILS (in which Ireland has not participated to date) measure digital competencies, there is a lack of comprehensive metrics assessing how the use of technology directly influences academic performance, critical thinking, and other learning outcomes, as discussed in the relevant section of this document (OECD, 2023 ). These countries also [27] lack comprehensive longitudinal studies that directly link digital device usage to learning outcomes, something that is missing in many other OECD countries as well (OECD, 2023 ). [27] Ireland In their turn, representatives from Ireland8 offered a presentation of their own situation. Ireland’s approach to measuring the impact of digital technologies in education reflects a structured and stakeholder-driven THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  15 strategy, with room to enhance its evaluation mechanisms. A key element for this is the Digital Learning Framework (DLF), a tool which assists schools and teachers to plan and monitor their progress in embedding digital technologies into teaching and learning. The tool provides schools with clear descriptors of relevant digital competencies for teachers and school leaders, emphasising the use of technology to improve teaching, learning, and assessment outcomes. The DLF also provides a Digital Learning Planning Cycle teachers and schools can engage with as develop their own digital plan. The Cycle is composed of six steps (see Figure 3): the first three (i.e. “identify”, “gather evidence”, and “analyse and make judgements”) form the investigation phase, the fourth (“write and share reports”) represent the creation phase, the fifth (i.e. “put improvement and plan into action) represents the implementation phase, and the last (“monitor actions and evaluate impact”) represents the evaluations stage (Oide Technology in Education, 2024 ). By focusing on the alignment of digital practices with pedagogical goals, the DLF [28] ensures that digital tools contribute meaningfully to student achievement. Figure 3. The six steps of the Digital Learning Planning Cycle Source: Oide Technology in Education (2024[29]), Digital Learning Framework - Planning Resources, https://www.dlplanning.ie/ Further, the Irish Primary Curriculum Framework outlines “being a digital learner” as one of seven key competencies to embed across all curriculum areas and subjects (see Figure 4). This aims to make children curious, creative, confident, and critical users of digital technology while also fostering a responsible, safe and ethical use of technology. The framework also proposes that digital technology use would become part of the curriculum area of Science, Technology, and Mathematics with its own subject specification for third to sixth class.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf This aims to make children curious, creative, confident, and critical users of digital technology while also fostering a responsible, safe and ethical use of technology. The framework also proposes that digital technology use would become part of the curriculum area of Science, Technology, and Mathematics with its own subject specification for third to sixth class. This approach highlights Ireland’s aim to integrate digital competencies into everyday teaching practices across different educational levels, especially with strategies aligned to the technological advancements shaping the future. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 16  Figure 4. The Primary Curriculum Framework’s core competencies Source: National Council for Curriculum and Assessment (2023[30]). Primary Curriculum Framework for Primary and Special Schools. https://curriculumonline.ie/getmedia/84747851-0581-431b-b4d7-dc6ee850883e/2023-Primary-Framework-ENG-screen.pdf At the post-primary level (i.e. junior and senior cycle levels), students’ competencies are further built on this foundation. The junior cycle English curriculum supports students in using literacy skills to manage information, and to find, evaluate, synthesise, and communicate information across various media. The curriculum emphasises the use of non-literary texts – such as advertisements, media broadcasts, and opinion pieces (e.g., articles, speeches, social media posts) – to help students interpret meaning, assess effectiveness, and respond critically to digital media. The aim is for students to analyse key ideas, style, tone, content, and overall impact of these texts in a systematic way. At the senior cycle, the Key Competencies Framework informs the development of subjects, modules, and programmes. Ireland’s National Council for Curriculum and Assessment (NCCA) has published a schedule for the redevelopment of all Senior Cycle subjects. The Framework emphasises the interconnectedness of key competencies, literacy, and numeracy, and how the development of one supports the others. This framework also underscores how key competencies are enhanced when students use digital tools to aid their learning. Additionally, in line with the Digital Strategy for Schools (DFL), there are ongoing efforts to explore how the use of digital technologies can be incorporated into the curriculum and assessment of revised Leaving Certificate subjects, as appropriate. Moreover, a revised specification for the Leaving Certificate Computer Science will be introduced in September 2027 as part of the third phase of subject revisions. The development group for this revision convened on September 2024 under NCCA structures, drawing on a brief and research from other jurisdictions to inform its work. Finally, online safety remains a central focus of Ireland’s strategy to ensure that the benefits of digital tools are not undermined by risks like cyberbullying or distractions. To this purpose, Ireland has developed resources like the Webwise programme, which supports schools in promoting a safe and responsible use THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  17 of digital technologies (Department of Education and Youth, 2024 ). Further, in terms of funding and [31] infrastructure, Ireland’s ICT Grant Scheme and the Schools Broadband Programme support schools in acquiring the necessary resources to enhance digital learning environments. These initiatives provide schools with the autonomy to tailor their digital leaning plans while ensuring alignment with national standards and guidelines. Lastly, recent advances in artificial intelligence, particularly generative AI, have prompted discussions about their potential impact on assessment. The State Examinations Commission (SEC) has been tasked with commissioning research on the role of generative AI in teacher-based assessment, exploring both the opportunities and challenges these technologies present. Key considerations for Ireland At the end of this session, several key considerations emerged. To begin with, Ireland has made notable progress in supporting the embedding of digital technologies in education.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf These initiatives provide schools with the autonomy to tailor their digital leaning plans while ensuring alignment with national standards and guidelines. Lastly, recent advances in artificial intelligence, particularly generative AI, have prompted discussions about their potential impact on assessment. The State Examinations Commission (SEC) has been tasked with commissioning research on the role of generative AI in teacher-based assessment, exploring both the opportunities and challenges these technologies present. Key considerations for Ireland At the end of this session, several key considerations emerged. To begin with, Ireland has made notable progress in supporting the embedding of digital technologies in education. This was achieved through initiatives as the Digital Strategy for Schools, the Digital Learning Framework (DLF), the curricular reforms, and the provision of a wide range of professional learning opportunities for teachers which all support a structured and pedagogical approach to the integration of technology into teaching and learning. Additionally, the ICT Grant Scheme and Schools Broadband Programme have bolstered infrastructure, supporting schools in enhancing their digital learning environments. However, Ireland could learn from the experiences of Estonia, France, and Sweden on how to address some of its measurement gaps. Estonia’s concrete effort to provide rigorous data on the effects of digital technologies on student learning, could offer insights that Ireland might adopt to strengthen its long-term monitoring of digital tools’ effectiveness. France’s EduPilote platform (prototype) exemplifies a data-driven initiative that links technology use to learning outcomes, a strategy Ireland could explore to improve evidence-based policy decisions. On this, Sweden has also identified the need to address the challenge of linking digital tools usage directly to academic performance; a challenge that can be tackled by developing more robust, standardised metrics and experimental studies (like RCTs) to assess the full impact of digital technologies. By building on these international experiences, Ireland could refine its approach to measuring the real- world outcomes of its digital education strategies, ensuring that technology adoption translates into tangible improvements in student outcomes. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 18  3 Ensuring quality, competence and equitable access in digital education Discussions during the peer-learning event emphasised that achieving equitable and effective digital education systems requires addressing interconnected challenges of infrastructure, teacher preparedness, and resource quality through system level policies. According to the framework of the OECD project Resourcing School Education: Policies for the Digital Transformation of Education and Future-Readiness of Teachers (OECD, 2025 ), considering these elements is fundamental to address barriers such as [32] disparities in access to quality digital infrastructure, lack of necessary skills for the pedagogical use of digital resources in and out of the classroom, assessment of digital resources, and sustainable financing among others. Doing so through a systemic policy approach can ensure coherence across policy areas, hence establishing a comprehensive and integrated policy environment that enables effective uses of digital resources. As highlighted at the start of the event, teacher professional learning remains a critical enabler of educational success. However, only 43% of educators in OECD countries report confidence in using digital tools pedagogically, despite widespread technical training (OECD, 2023 ). To address this gap, some [11] accredited professional development programmes have focused on AI-driven lesson differentiation and digital assessment literacy. These programmes demonstrate the value of grounding training in the Technological, Pedagogical, Content Knowledge (TPACK) model, which integrates technological, pedagogical, and subject-specific expertise (Koehler, 2013 ). This approach aligns with broader OECD [33] recommendations for competency frameworks that prioritise continuous, practice-oriented learning ecosystems – such as peer coaching networks and micro-credentialling systems tied to career progression – over one-off workshops (OECD, 2023 ).

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf To address this gap, some [11] accredited professional development programmes have focused on AI-driven lesson differentiation and digital assessment literacy. These programmes demonstrate the value of grounding training in the Technological, Pedagogical, Content Knowledge (TPACK) model, which integrates technological, pedagogical, and subject-specific expertise (Koehler, 2013 ). This approach aligns with broader OECD [33] recommendations for competency frameworks that prioritise continuous, practice-oriented learning ecosystems – such as peer coaching networks and micro-credentialling systems tied to career progression – over one-off workshops (OECD, 2023 ). [11] Further, fostering student self-direction requires deliberate policy architectures rather than assuming innate digital competence. To this end, systemic models that embed self-regulated learning objectives into curriculum standards show promise, particularly when supported by learning analytics dashboards that provide real-time feedback. Evaluations of such approaches point to measurable improvements in problem-solving competencies (OECD, 2023 ). Nonetheless, the OECD stresses the need for parallel [27] investments in teacher training to interpret analytics effectively (2023 ). [27] Underpinning all these elements are governance frameworks that coordinate cross-sector priorities. An example of the needed systemic coordination would be coupling inter-ministerial collaborations that align infrastructure investments and pedagogical goals with procurement standards that ensure edtech accessibility and data ethics. As highlighted in the OECD’s Shaping Digital Education: Enabling Factors for Quality, Equity and Efficiency report (2023 ), if monitoring focuses on classroom integration rather [11] than only on device distribution metrics, such architectures might transform the usage of digital tools from isolated interventions into drivers of pedagogical renewal. THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  19 The experiences of France, Sweden, Estonia and Ireland France In terms of quality, competence, and equitable access in digital education, France has adopted a centralised and structured approach to integrating digital technologies in education, aiming to address quality, competence, and equitable access. The Pix platform plays a central role in this, by offering tailored, skill-oriented assessments that promote high-quality digital literacy across all educational levels. With a comprehensive curriculum mapped to real-world applications, Pix ensures that students and educators develop both technical and critical thinking skills, essential for navigating a rapidly evolving digital landscape. The platform is a key driver of competence development, as it enables educators to evaluate and improve their own digital skills through self-paced learning modules. To cater to the specific needs of different user groups, Pix offers two distinct versions: one for students and another one for teachers. The student version, simply called Pix, is designed to assess, develop, and certify digital skills throughout their education, from lower secondary to upper secondary schools. It includes mandatory certifications at key stages and helps prepare students for the digital demands of further education and the workforce. For teachers and education staff, a specialised version called Pix+ Édu has been developed. This version focuses on enhancing educators’ professional digital competencies, aligning with the European DigCompEdu framework and the French Cadre de Référence des Compétences Numériques pour l'Éducation (CRCN-Édu). Pix+ Édu offers self-assessment, thematic training pathways, and the opportunity for teachers to validate their digital skills through certification. This dual approach ensures that both students and educators are equipped with the necessary digital competencies to thrive in the modern educational environment. The expert from France also mentioned that to address equity, support for the provision of digital devices is provided to schools, especially to rural and economically disadvantaged ones. For instance, the 2015 Digital School Plan provided schools with financial support for purchasing devices, training teachers, and modernising IT infrastructure.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf Pix+ Édu offers self-assessment, thematic training pathways, and the opportunity for teachers to validate their digital skills through certification. This dual approach ensures that both students and educators are equipped with the necessary digital competencies to thrive in the modern educational environment. The expert from France also mentioned that to address equity, support for the provision of digital devices is provided to schools, especially to rural and economically disadvantaged ones. For instance, the 2015 Digital School Plan provided schools with financial support for purchasing devices, training teachers, and modernising IT infrastructure. Further, since 1981, the Territories of Educational Priority (REP) initiative foresees additional funding and training to ensure students located in disadvantaged areas have equal opportunities to access digital resources (Éduscol, 2024 ). Finally, the expert referred once again to the [34] EduPilote platform prototype (also mentioned in section Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education), which monitors the usage and impact of digital tools in schools. By collecting real-time data on digital education practices, the platform allows policymakers to identify disparities and inefficiencies, enabling adaptive and evidence-based interventions. However, despite these measures, challenges persist in fully bridging the rural-urban divide and ensuring universal access to high-speed broadband, which remains uneven in some regions (Ministère de l’Éducation nationale et de la Jeunesse, 2024 ). [19] Sweden In contrast, the expert from Sweden explained how their decentralised model reflects the country’s broader commitment to autonomy and innovation and objective of ensuring quality, competence, and equitable access by empowering local stakeholders. Municipalities and schools have the flexibility to design tailored digital education programmes, ensuring that strategies are locally relevant and responsive to specific needs. This decentralisation aims to give more autonomy to local agents, so that schools may experiment with innovative approaches (e.g., gamified learning, digital storytelling, or virtual reality-based teaching). Accordingly, to promote teacher competence equitably, Sweden provides professional development programmes funded through municipal budgets and supported by national guidelines. Initiatives like the National Digitalisation Strategy for the School System outline competency goals for teachers and encourage the integration of digital tools into core subjects. However, the decentralised system has resulted in disparities in training quality and access. For instance, rural municipalities often struggle to THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 20  allocate resources for advanced teacher training or state-of-the-art digital tools, creating inconsistencies in digital education quality across the country. In this perspective, equitable access remains a significant challenge in Sweden. Schools in wealthier municipalities benefit from better funding, leading to more advanced infrastructure and technology adoption compared to less affluent areas. Efforts to address these disparities without targeted national subsidies for schools in underserved regions is difficult due to the lack of uniform standards, which can hinder broader equity goals. Sweden’s experience thus highlights the trade-offs of a decentralised approach: while it fosters innovation, it requires robust mechanisms to ensure consistency and fairness (Skolverket, 2022 ). [24] Estonia The expert from Estonia explained that the country’s holistic and technology-driven approach to digital education is designed to comprehensively tackle quality, competence and equitable access. Central to Estonia’s strategy is the Digital Competency Framework (already mentioned in section “Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education”), which ensures a consistent and high-quality digital education experience across the country by outlining clear standards for students and teachers. This framework stresses critical digital skills, including cybersecurity, data literacy, and creative problem-solving, ensuring that students are not just passive users of technology but active and informed participants in the digital economy.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf [24] Estonia The expert from Estonia explained that the country’s holistic and technology-driven approach to digital education is designed to comprehensively tackle quality, competence and equitable access. Central to Estonia’s strategy is the Digital Competency Framework (already mentioned in section “Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education”), which ensures a consistent and high-quality digital education experience across the country by outlining clear standards for students and teachers. This framework stresses critical digital skills, including cybersecurity, data literacy, and creative problem-solving, ensuring that students are not just passive users of technology but active and informed participants in the digital economy. To address equity issues, Estonia has implemented several nationwide initiatives, such as the ProgeTiger programme (also mentioned in section “Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education”) which provides free coding and robotics education for all students, regardless of their socio-economic background. The Estonian government also prioritises rural and disadvantaged communities through targeted funding for devices, infrastructure, and teacher training. Estonia’s eKool platform exemplifies the country’s commitment to equity by providing a free, universal digital interface for students, teachers, and parents, ensuring that all stakeholders have access to real-time information about learning progress. Despite these achievements, Estonia continues to face challenges in ensuring universal access to high- quality broadband in rural areas, as well as in addressing socio-economic disparities in device ownership. Nevertheless, its comprehensive, forward-thinking policies and commitment to data-driven decision- making position it as a global reference in digital education innovation. Achieving equitable and effective digital education systems requires addressing interconnected challenges of infrastructure, teacher preparedness, and resource quality through systemic policy design. Ireland During this session, the Irish expert highlighted that Ireland has taken important steps to address issues of equity, competence, and quality in digital education, while continuing to work on bridging gaps and improving outcomes. The Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 emphasises inclusion as a key priority. Programmes like Delivering Equality of Opportunity in Schools (DEIS) provide additional funding and resources to schools who have high concentrations of students from socio-economically disadvantaged areas, helping to close the digital divide. DEIS schools and special schools/special classes in schools also benefit from supplementary funding under the ICT Grant Scheme. In relation to digital competencies, teachers’ digital skills have been a key focus, with Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes incorporating digital skills training since 2020. Additionally, the Oide (the Irish Department of Education’s professional development service for teachers) organises professional THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025  21 development initiatives, ensuring that educators have access to workshops, resources, and school-based supports to enhance their digital teaching practices. This approach aligns with Ireland's Digital Learning Framework (DLF) (already mentioned in section “Measuring the impact of digital technologies in education”) which promotes innovative and reflective teaching methods that integrate technology effectively. In more details, the Oide Technology in Education (TiE) division develops and delivers professional learning opportunities such as online and face-to-face courses, workshops, seminars, webinars and whole-school support. The Oide TiE also provides advice and support to schools on broadband, ICT Infrastructure matters, and Internet safety. In particular, the latter is fostered through the Webwise program, which promotes the autonomous, effective, and safe use of the internet by young people and has a wide range of training materials and resources available to guide teachers, students and parents on online safety. Webwise develops and disseminates resources that help teachers integrate digital citizenship and online safety into teaching and learning in their schools.

Title: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf
Content: OECD_Education_Policy_Perspectives_125.pdf The Oide TiE also provides advice and support to schools on broadband, ICT Infrastructure matters, and Internet safety. In particular, the latter is fostered through the Webwise program, which promotes the autonomous, effective, and safe use of the internet by young people and has a wide range of training materials and resources available to guide teachers, students and parents on online safety. Webwise develops and disseminates resources that help teachers integrate digital citizenship and online safety into teaching and learning in their schools. With a growing repository of over 25,000 resources and 100 good practice videos tailored to different age groups, the platform supports schools and teachers to create safe digital learning environments for students. Moreover, Oide TiE manages Scoilnet – Ireland’s official digital education content portal – which offers a wealth of educational materials, including videos, lesson plans, and good practice guides for educators. Equity of access is further enhanced by Ireland’s investment in broadband provision under the Schools Broadband Programme which provides high-speed connectivity to all recognised schools. However, like other countries, Ireland still faces challenges in fully bridging the digital divide, particularly in ensuring universal access to high-quality devices and resources across all socio-economic strata. For example, while DEIS schools receive additional support, ensuring consistency in access and outcomes remains an ongoing priority. In this regard, participants highlighted that Ireland has demonstrated a commitment to reducing the digital divide and is working to ensure equitable access to digital resources across all schools. As part of the National Recovery and Resilience Plan, the Irish government issued €50 million in funding to support schools and students at risk due to socio-economic factors. This funding supported schools in addressing the digital divide and aimed to ensure that disadvantaged learners have access to the necessary tools for digital learning. The country is also making efforts to improve digital infrastructure, with many schools benefitting from high-quality broadband and filtered content. Schools then have autonomy in their digital learning plan to determine how to deploy digital technologies including devices, in their schools. In doing so, it is important that schools are supported so that all students experience the same level of access and opportunity. Key considerations for Ireland In conclusion, Ireland has made significant improvements in addressing equity, competence, and quality in the embedding of digital technologies in teaching and learning. Key assets include its focus on equitable access through initiatives like DEIS and the ICT Grant Scheme, which provide targeted support to socio- economically disadvantaged schools. Ireland's commitment to teacher competence is also evident, with a range of opportunities for digital skills training for teachers and ongoing professional development through the Oide TiE platform at all stages throughout their careers. Furthermore, the country's investment in broadband infrastructure means that all recognised schools are provided with high-speed connectivity through the Schools Broadband Programme operated by the Department of Education. However, there are areas where Ireland could benefit from the experiences of other countries. Sweden's decentralised model offers a valuable experience in promoting innovation and tailoring digital education to local needs, although it also highlights the challenge of ensuring consistency across localities. France's structured approach, including the Pix platform and targeted rural initiatives, could help Ireland ensure universal access to digital tools and high-speed broadband. Estonia’s holistic, data-driven strategy, which THE IMPACT OF DIGITAL TECHNOLOGIES ON STUDENTS’ LEARNING IN IRELAND © OECD 2025 22  includes a clear Digital Competency Framework and free nationwide coding programmes, exemplifies how comprehensive and inclusive policies can address both equity and quality on a national scale.
