........................................................................................................ 135 www.h2.ie 1 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Executive summary Introduction The Digital Strategy for Schools (2015-2020): Enhancing Teaching, Learning and Assessment contained an action to encourage a culture of innovation in schools in relation to digital learning. The Department of Education and Skills (now the Department of Education and Youth)1, subsequently launched the Schools Excellence Fund Digital Programme (SEF Digital) which was later expanded to the SEF Digital & STEM Programme. In April 2018, Minister Richard Bruton announced funding for 265 schools through the Programme. The purpose of the Programme was to demonstrate the innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment through clusters of schools collaborating on specific projects. The schools applied for inclusion in the Programme via an open call to all schools which was launched in December 2017 by the Department2. The Programme sought to create collaborative environments in school clusters where teachers could work jointly on innovative projects that integrated digital tools into their teaching practices. The participating primary and post-primary schools were encouraged to design projects that addressed specific curricular needs, including STEM education and curricular reform in primary languages and maths. The projects were expected to demonstrate how digital technologies could improve learning outcomes, strengthen collaboration among teachers, and trial innovative approaches to assessment. The Programme aimed to build links between schools, higher education institutions, and industry partners, to broaden the focus of cluster activities and provide students meaningful, real-world contexts for their digital learning. Evaluation approach The evaluation process commenced in early 2019 and was finalised in 2024. The work of the Programme, like many other school activities, was severely disrupted by the COVID-19 pandemic.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf The projects were expected to demonstrate how digital technologies could improve learning outcomes, strengthen collaboration among teachers, and trial innovative approaches to assessment. The Programme aimed to build links between schools, higher education institutions, and industry partners, to broaden the focus of cluster activities and provide students meaningful, real-world contexts for their digital learning. Evaluation approach The evaluation process commenced in early 2019 and was finalised in 2024. The work of the Programme, like many other school activities, was severely disrupted by the COVID-19 pandemic. The schools commenced their projects in 2018 and were very active up to March 2020 when schools were closed on a rolling basis until June 2021. Schools gradually reopened in September 2021, and the Programme held a key event in April 2022 where the clusters reconvened with the Department and other key stakeholders in Croke Park. As a result of the disruptions, the evaluation was carried out in two phases: Phase 1: September 2018 to June 2020 and Phase 2: April 2022 to June 2023. In 2020 the evaluation team, anticipating further disruption to school activities decided to produce an Interim Evaluation Report based on a range of data including documentation provided by the clusters, and on two surveys, one for Cluster Coordinators and one for Oide TiE Advisors3. This report was shared with the Department in September 2020. This document was subsequently revised and updated in May 2022, and key findings are summarised in Section 4. 1 The Department was known as the Department of Education and Skills (to October 2020) and subsequently the Department of Education (to May 2025). It is currently the Department of Education and Youth. Throughout this report, the Department of Education and Youth is referred to as “the Department”. 2 https://www.dcu.ie/stemeducationinnovationglobalstudies/news/2017/12/minister-bruton-launches-school- excellence-fund 3 Clusters were supported by Oide Technology in Education (TiE). www.h2.ie 2 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Phase 2 of the evaluation commenced with the Croke Park event held in April 2022 where the clusters shared their reflections on the Programme and lessons learned. This was an extremely successful event, and it also provided useful insights to inform the development of a Post- Project Survey for Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors that was sent out during the 2023 school year, when schooling was returning to normal. The final survey captured data on three areas: 1. Experience of the project 2. Views on lessons learned (related to good classroom practice, infrastructural models, support models and embedding digital education), and 3. Views in relation to possible future digital initiatives. The findings are presented in Section 5, 6 and 7. The evaluation also captured a series of case studies and teacher stories that illustrate how clusters engaged with the Programme and document how they used a range of digital technologies with their learners. These appear in Appendices 3 and 4. Key findings The Programme had a significant positive influence on both teaching and learning practices in the participating schools. Teachers reported increased confidence in using digital tools, greater collaboration with colleagues, and more effective engagement with students. Learners exhibited increased levels of motivation and more developed key skills, particularly in STEM- related subjects, while they also benefited from the interactive and creative use of digital tools for activities such as coding, robotics, and multimedia storytelling. The collaborative structure of the SEF Digital & STEM Programme proved to be one of its strongest elements. Schools worked together within their clusters to share resources, strategies, and experiences. This collaboration allowed educators to exchange knowledge and expertise, fostering a culture of support and innovation.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Learners exhibited increased levels of motivation and more developed key skills, particularly in STEM- related subjects, while they also benefited from the interactive and creative use of digital tools for activities such as coding, robotics, and multimedia storytelling. The collaborative structure of the SEF Digital & STEM Programme proved to be one of its strongest elements. Schools worked together within their clusters to share resources, strategies, and experiences. This collaboration allowed educators to exchange knowledge and expertise, fostering a culture of support and innovation. Schools that had previously been isolated, or lacked access to resources, found value in connecting with their peers, strengthening their overall capacity to engage in digital education. The Interim Evaluation Report at the end of Phase 1 found that: • Most of the teachers involved in the project found the experience very positive, fostered good links between teachers across different schools, facilitating knowledge and expertise sharing, and advanced students' digital skills. • The SEF Digital and STEM Programme experience was helpful in the move to online learning during lockdown, as learners and staff had developed their skills in the use of online learning tools through project activities. • The clusters involved in the Programme had been successful in disseminating their activities to the wider community, through the use of websites (60%) and social media (mainly using Twitter/X). Some clusters had also participated in (or initiated) large-scale public events. This evaluation captured data from 35 of the 41 clusters and found a wide range of innovative practices supported by digital technologies across six categories: www.h2.ie 3 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 1. Use of sophisticated technology (e.g., the use of equipment/infrastructure allotted for the project e.g., use of iPads, Lego WeDo kits, VR headsets etc.) 2. Inter-cluster collaboration 3. Teacher collaboration 4. Upskilling of learners (e.g., increase in learners’ skills e.g. project management, problem-solving, 21st-century skills) 5. Curriculum planning 6. Engagement of young women in STEM careers. These findings are discussed further in Section 4, with Phase 2 data presented in Sections 5, 6 and 7. The responses to closed questions in the Post-Project Survey in Phase 2 are captured in Section 5, showing a high-level of satisfaction with the projects and that learners responded positively to being involved in the Programme. Some issues were raised in relation to Programme supports (funding, advice, guidelines etc.) and in terms of support for Cluster Coordinators. An analysis of the open questions in the Post-Project Survey reinforced these findings and it highlighted a range of successes and challenges faced by the clusters, which are summarised below. Table 1: Successes and challenges identified in the Post-Project Survey. Most participants had positive experiences of the project, noting high student engagement, collaboration between schools, and the development of innovative teaching approaches. Overall Experience Coordinators and teachers identified challenges that came up in some clusters, which included high staff turnover, problems around project communications, and some technical and infrastructure issues. All clusters were badly disrupted by the pandemic and school closures, Impact of COVID-19 though some were more able to adapt than others. Collaboration between schools was a major element of the success of the clusters. It is notable that a third of the school clusters continued to Collaboration collaborate after the end of the project, this was most often in STEM or computing related areas. The Advisors noted the positive impact of the projects on digital skills, on the integration of technology into teaching and learning processes, and on Long-Term Impact the collaborative working that had developed with other schools.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Collaboration between schools was a major element of the success of the clusters. It is notable that a third of the school clusters continued to Collaboration collaborate after the end of the project, this was most often in STEM or computing related areas. The Advisors noted the positive impact of the projects on digital skills, on the integration of technology into teaching and learning processes, and on Long-Term Impact the collaborative working that had developed with other schools. They also noted that where staffing was consistent and project goals were clear, digital practices were sustained after the project. Some clusters reported issues over funding, and in particular the impact Funding lack of funding had on the sustainability of their projects, especially in terms of ongoing procurement and repair of equipment. The survey did not ask specifically about regulation, ethics or data privacy, Regulation and Data but it was perhaps surprising that only one respondent raised issues here, Privacy this was in relation to “support for managing data and compliance with data protection regulations”. Section 6 of the Final Evaluation Report captures the responses to open questions related to lessons learned in the Programme and these are summarised below. www.h2.ie 4 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Table 2: Lessons learned as identified in the evaluation. Effective strategies included student-led projects, collaborative learning Good Classroom and personalised learning. In such cases teachers often moved from Practice structured to more open approaches over time. Need for: • Strong broadband. • Physical space for housing and securing technology. • Hardware and devices, for both technologies for general use, but Infrastructural Models also specialist equipment for specific areas. • Software and platforms (i.e. Google, Microsoft and learning management systems). • Standardisation and clear policy. • Importance of continuing professional development and training and for peer-to-peer collaboration. • Participants wanted practical, up-to-date training tailored to their needs. In-school and peer-led models were preferred. Some Support Models questioned the value of private providers (i.e. those who developed certain technologies or software products providing training). • Technical support. • Administrative and institutional support. • The sustained Oide TiE support model was widely praised. • Embedding required long-term funding, training, time, collaboration, Embedding Digital curriculum alignment. Technologies • Relation to assessment needs consideration. • Evaluation of effectiveness of technology integration is required. Recommendations for future projects The evaluation suggests that there is a need for similar research projects in the future and recommends that if such projects are undertaken, then the Department might make some adjustments to further enhance the experience for the participating schools, and to further inform wider policy and practice in digital education. The report recommends that any future programme or projects should: • Establish clear goals for the use of digital technologies in schools. While this Programme sought to “demonstrate the innovative use of digital technologies”, it was not always clear what this might look like. • Revise the application process and provide feedback to clusters on their applications, so that proposals are more realistic in light of the timeframe and the resources available to schools. • Build in greater flexibility at the design phase to enable projects to change direction if their activities are not progressing according to their original plans. In particular, such an approach might adopt a phased funding release model, as is sometimes the case in other jurisdictions. • Revisit future monitoring and evaluation requirements.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf • Revise the application process and provide feedback to clusters on their applications, so that proposals are more realistic in light of the timeframe and the resources available to schools. • Build in greater flexibility at the design phase to enable projects to change direction if their activities are not progressing according to their original plans. In particular, such an approach might adopt a phased funding release model, as is sometimes the case in other jurisdictions. • Revisit future monitoring and evaluation requirements. While a project of this nature will require participating schools to complete regular reports, particularly in relation to their www.h2.ie 5 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 project expenditure, it is recommended that this element is revised in the future. The clusters did receive guidance and advice from the Department, but this was deemed to be insufficient by some Cluster Coordinators who expressed a need for additional supports. This element of reporting also resulted in an increased workload for the Department, and they too need adequate resourcing to deal with such intensive projects going forward. • Reconsider how funding is distributed to clusters of schools. While schools were readily able to draw down funding to procure digital technologies at the outset of the Programme, there were issues as time went on. The evaluation suggests revisiting the kinds of financial supports that might be made available to schools and how these could be drawn down over a prolonged period of time. • Further strengthen the supports made available to clusters. While many of the supports, such as the role played by the Oide TiE Advisors, were successful the report identifies the need for a range of additional supports that might include technical support and critical feedback on project activities over time. • Provide greater clarity on how projects are expected to disseminate the activities they engage with using digital technologies, so that the wider school community can learn from their activities. Final word The SEF Digital and STEM Programme was deemed an overwhelming success by those who remained active through to the end. The Programme enabled schools to work together to use digital technologies in a supported way across the curriculum. The majority of teachers who participated continue to use these technologies beyond the Programme and believe this will have a lasting impact in embedding digital practices in their schools. The Programme has gathered a range of valuable practices, which are captured on project websites and in the case- studies and teacher stories. These could be further developed by the Oide TiE team to inform all schools in the future. New and emerging technologies, such as Artificial Intelligence, are now impacting on a range of school practices and there is an ongoing need to explore what impact, good and not so good, they can have on educational practices now and in the future. Thus, there is a need to engage in further such innovative projects and to continually evolve the model so that it works for all stakeholders, the Department of Education and Youth, support services and, most importantly, for schools. www.h2.ie 6 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 1: Introduction H2 Learning was contracted by the Department of Education to carry out an evaluation of the Schools Excellence Fund Digital and STEM Programme (SEF Digital & STEM) and began work on the evaluation in March 2019. H2 Learning shared the Interim Evaluation Report in September 2020, with the expectation at that time of producing a final evaluation report by late 2021.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf www.h2.ie 6 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 1: Introduction H2 Learning was contracted by the Department of Education to carry out an evaluation of the Schools Excellence Fund Digital and STEM Programme (SEF Digital & STEM) and began work on the evaluation in March 2019. H2 Learning shared the Interim Evaluation Report in September 2020, with the expectation at that time of producing a final evaluation report by late 2021. The purpose of the Interim Evaluation Report was to test the evaluation approach, to identify emerging trends, to flag any issues that might need additional attention during the final phase of the project, and to give the Department an opportunity to comment on the emerging report. However, the rolling school closures that occurred between March 2020 and Spring 2022, a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, severely disrupted the project and hence the production of the Final Evaluation Report. This Final Evaluation Report builds on the Interim Evaluation Report, providing further insights into the effectiveness of the SEF Digital & STEM Programme. It describes some of the issues that arose during the Programme and provides recommendations for consideration in developing possible future initiatives in this area. The Final Evaluation Report includes elements of the Interim Evaluation Report, particularly those elements which illustrate the richness of the activities that took place in schools, and data gathered from Cluster Coordinators and PDST Advisors4 (later renamed Oide TiE Advisors), at the end of the project in June 2022. The Final Evaluation Report also includes the three case studies created for the Interim Evaluation Report, as these illustrate the innovative nature of the selected projects. These case studies were selected to capture the range of innovative practice across the Programme under the criteria outlined in the European Commission report, entitled Study on supporting school innovation across Europe (2018): Students, Teachers, Schools Leaders, Shared Vision and Wider Partnerships5. Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Publications Office of the European Union. https://doi.org/10.2766/466312 www.h2.ie 7 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 2: SEF Digital & STEM – Programme overview The Digital Strategy for Schools (2015-2020): Enhancing Teaching, Learning and Assessment contained an action, 3.4, to encourage a culture of innovation in schools in relation to digital learning. The Department launched the Schools Excellence Fund Digital (SEF Digital) in December 2017 and subsequently announced in April 2018 that 265 schools were to be funded through the programme. Subsequently the Department took the decision to expand the programme to the SEF Digital and STEM programme. The Department document, Description of Scheme – Schools Excellence Fund – Digital, set out the purpose of the scheme as follows6: “The purpose of the scheme is to demonstrate the innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment, through clusters of schools collaborating on specific projects”. The Programme also included 10 STEM clusters, who used a range of digital technologies to support STEM education practices. The document then set out more detailed objectives organised in several categories (i.e. scope, purpose, outcomes and expectations), and these are set out below. The evidence related to how each of these specific objectives were met is embedded within the report. In the Interim Evaluation Report an explicit mapping of evidence to the specific objectives was provided, but this has not been reproduced here. Programme objectives Scope Schools were initially invited to apply for inclusion in the Programme, where they could collaborate on the design and delivery of specific projects that utilised digital technologies in an innovative way in the context of: a) Post-primary – curricular reform and innovation at Junior and Senior Cycles. b) Primary – curricular reform of Primary Languages Curriculum, Primary Maths Curriculum and innovative delivery of existing subject areas. c) Projects involving cross-sectoral topics. d) Projects relating to key policy initiatives (e.g. STEM report). e) Projects that reflect the diversity of the educational environment. Purpose The projects were to be of three-year duration (i.e. commencing September 2018 and concluding in June 2021) and this included the evaluation and dissemination phases, and they were to demonstrate how digital technologies can be used to enhance teaching, learning and assessment practices and, particularly, how the outcomes from the projects can inform: 6 https://www.oidetechnologyineducation.ie/projects-initiatives/digital-clusters/ www.h2.ie 8 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 1. Classroom practice in relation to the embedding of digital technologies and constructivist approaches to teaching, learning and assessment at primary and post- primary levels. 2. The infrastructural models that could best support innovation in relation to teaching, learning and assessment, communication, and collaborative planning. 3. The most appropriate support models that could be put in place to facilitate innovation in schools. 4. How the embedding of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment within and across all schools could be achieved. It was considered that a three-year period would be the minimum time period necessary to assess local implementation and Programme impact at a national level and to inform longer- term decision making by the Department. Expected outcomes It was expected that the work of the clusters of schools would provide outcomes including some or all of the following: a) Evidence of use of digital technologies to enable sharing of information and collaborative practice between teachers in the cluster/professional network, such as development of resources, blog posts, articles, and best practice videos.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf It was considered that a three-year period would be the minimum time period necessary to assess local implementation and Programme impact at a national level and to inform longer- term decision making by the Department. Expected outcomes It was expected that the work of the clusters of schools would provide outcomes including some or all of the following: a) Evidence of use of digital technologies to enable sharing of information and collaborative practice between teachers in the cluster/professional network, such as development of resources, blog posts, articles, and best practice videos. b) Evidence of enhanced learning by students through teachers’ engagement in action research, collaborative and independent learning, including discovery and problem- based learning by students/pupils in each school in the cluster, mediated through the use of a range of digital technologies. Outcomes of the various projects will include evidence of the students’ own work. c) Evidence of shared learning/teaching experiences between the schools in each cluster. For example, this may include joint videos produced, collaboration on how particular aspects of new curriculum might best be delivered especially through the effective use of technology. d) Evidence of use of technologies (hardware, software, or mechanisms to share the use of technology between the schools in the cluster) to demonstrate how such technologies can be used effectively. Report output from each cluster (which may take the form of an evaluation of their chosen technology, or video demonstrations on the effective use of those technologies). e) Evidence of how linkages with third level institutions enhance the design, development, delivery and the evaluation of the projects. f) Evidence of best practice in relation to forming effective links with business and industry. Programme expectations In rolling out the Programme, the Department provided a range of advice and support to schools, through the Professional Development Service for Teachers (PDST) in the area of Technology in Education (PDST TiE), which is now Oide Technology in Education (Oide TiE)7. For 7 https://www.oidetechnologyineducation.ie/ www.h2.ie 9 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 consistency, this report uses the term Oide TiE when referring to this team of advisors and their role in the Programme. The clusters were expected to: a) Develop clear project implementation plans, with timelines, identified targets, and estimates of associated costs. b) Establish links with local businesses, industries, and third-level institutions where possible. c) Maintain a focus on how the use of digital technologies can enhance teaching, learning and assessment. d) Develop and disseminate a range of project outcomes. e) Disseminate the project outcomes. f) Engage with ongoing evaluation. g) Report on progress to an agreed schedule. Programme funding In 2018, €1m was allocated for the SEF Digital & STEM Programme, comprising €750k to issue to schools via grants for relevant infrastructure and associated training required, support for events etc. Schools were required to submit budget and project plans, outlining how the funding would be utilised. The budgets were reviewed and approved, with the approval and agreement by schools to the terms of the programme, including progress reporting etc, confirmed via a letter signed by a department official and a representative from the lead school in each cluster. The funding varied by project, based on the number of schools involved and the nature of the infrastructure needed, with the average grant being €31k per cluster. As the allocation for the programme fell into 2018, with no guarantee of ring-fenced funding for years 2 and 3, the bulk of funding issued in year 1, and this was advised to schools during the budget submission process.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf The funding varied by project, based on the number of schools involved and the nature of the infrastructure needed, with the average grant being €31k per cluster. As the allocation for the programme fell into 2018, with no guarantee of ring-fenced funding for years 2 and 3, the bulk of funding issued in year 1, and this was advised to schools during the budget submission process. The balance of €250k was allocated within the Department to provide substitution cover so that participating teachers could be released for related activities. Cluster Coordinators could take up to nine days annually, with up to five days available for one teacher in each of the other schools in the cluster. www.h2.ie 10 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Programme timeline The actual timeline of the project is presented below with the key milestones in terms of reporting and project data gathering that took place between December 2017 and September 2024. The original planned timeline was severely disrupted by the number of rolling school closures due to COVID-19. Year 0: 2017-18 • Call for proposals released by Department of Education - Dec 2017 • Schools selected to commence their projects in September - April 2018 Year 1: 2018-19 • Project commencement - Sept 2018 • H2 Learning appointed as evaluator - March 2019 • SEF Digital & STEM Event Athlone - May 2019 Year 2: 2019-20 • School closures due to COVID-19 - March 2020 • Survey (Cluster Coordinators and Oide TiE) - June 2020 • Collection of online data use – Summer 2020 • Internal Interim Evaluation Report - Sept 2020 Year 3: 2020-21 • Further school disruption due to COVID-19 Year 4: 2021-22 • Gradual reopening of schools during the school year - Sept 2021 • Croke Park SEF Digital & STEM Event - April 2022 Year 5: 2022-23 • Post-Project Survey administered to Cluster Coordinators and Oide TiE staff - June 2023 • Initial draft of Final Evaluation Report shared with the DE – Sept 2024 Figure 1: SEF Digital & STEM Project Timeline. www.h2.ie 11 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 3: Evaluation approach COVID-19 and the associated school closures had a major impact on the formulation of this report, resulting in the production of an extensive Interim Report in September 2020. The Interim Report recognised that there could be additional school closures and that this might impact negatively on further data collection and on some clusters completing the project. Thus, the extensive data collected during this initial phase was very important and captures where clusters were at this key juncture in their projects. As expected, COVID-19 continued to disrupt the Programme, and a further round of data collection took place in 2022 and 2023. Evaluators had not initially intended to conduct two distinct phases of data collection; this was necessitated by pandemic related disruptions. While many clusters continued to remain active and completed many aspects of their projects, some clusters did not remain active. The approaches adopted in collecting data from the clusters during these two phases is outlined below. Phase 1 The first stage of the evaluation collected and reviewed data from a wide range of sources as outlined below. The DE shared the original cluster proposals and project plans8 and all the completed progress reports and expenditure reports that had been submitted. This was supplemented by reviewing school dissemination material, interviews, focus groups and the collection of survey data. The dataset therefore consisted of: • The proposals (40 out of the 41 were received and reviewed), and three-year plans (40/41).

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf The approaches adopted in collecting data from the clusters during these two phases is outlined below. Phase 1 The first stage of the evaluation collected and reviewed data from a wide range of sources as outlined below. The DE shared the original cluster proposals and project plans8 and all the completed progress reports and expenditure reports that had been submitted. This was supplemented by reviewing school dissemination material, interviews, focus groups and the collection of survey data. The dataset therefore consisted of: • The proposals (40 out of the 41 were received and reviewed), and three-year plans (40/41). • Twice-yearly Progress Reports (57/82) and Expenditure reports (32/82). • Websites, Twitter accounts and other social media accounts associated with the clusters (as identified in documentation, surveys, or online searches). • Discussions with Department officials. • Discussions and focus group with Oide TiE Advisors (6-11-2019, 13-12-2019). • Attendance and data collection at SEF Digital & STEM Event in Athlone Institute of Technology (now Technological University of the Shannon (TUS) Athlone) 29-5-2019. • Cluster Coordinator Survey April 2020 (35 respondents from 41 clusters - 85% response rate). • Oide TiE Advisor Survey June 2020 (9 respondents). The steps in our analysis of this data were as follows: • Initial systematisation and preliminary review of the proposals, reports and online data. • Identification of important themes through discussion with the Department and the Oide TiE Advisors, and discussions and observations at the Athlone event. • Use of identified themes to inform the development of a survey for Cluster Coordinators. 8 Note the evaluation reviewed 40 of the 41 proposals and project plans. www.h2.ie 12 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 • Selection of three clusters judged particularly active and with diverse data, to develop case studies. This enabled testing and refinement of the themes, examining progress in terms of potential for supporting innovation. • Detailed analysis of the Cluster Coordinator survey, with coding developed both top- down (thematic) and bottom-up (textual) and amalgamated into larger categories. Frequencies quoted in this report refer to numbers of clusters (one coordinator per cluster). • Identification of data gaps relating to support received, leading to an additional survey with Oide TiE Advisors. The Key findings identified in the Interim Evaluation Report are summarised in Section 4 of this report. Phase 2 The second stage of the evaluation took place after the period of school closures because of the COVID-19 pandemic, and the subsequent impacts on schooling post-pandemic. The period of the Programme was extended for a year because of the challenges schools had faced, and most clusters resumed work on their projects once disruption passed. Few further progress reports were received in this period, and projects were not able to follow up fully on the main Interim Report recommendations (notably those related to dissemination and project evaluation). Two further data collection exercises were undertaken, firstly at a Croke Park event in 2022, and a Post-Project Survey in 2023. Croke Park event In April 2022 an event was held at Croke Park bringing the clusters together with a focus on supporting projects in dissemination, and a beginning was also made in sharing reflections on the progress of the projects and lessons learned. These discussions informed the thinking of the evaluation team in constructing a post-project survey. The event provided an opportunity to bring projects together in a face-to-face setting, and for them to share their experiences over the course of the Programme.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Croke Park event In April 2022 an event was held at Croke Park bringing the clusters together with a focus on supporting projects in dissemination, and a beginning was also made in sharing reflections on the progress of the projects and lessons learned. These discussions informed the thinking of the evaluation team in constructing a post-project survey. The event provided an opportunity to bring projects together in a face-to-face setting, and for them to share their experiences over the course of the Programme. The event was extremely successful, and it showcased the progress that clusters made in relation to using digital technologies, despite the challenges they encountered during the COVID-19 pandemic. The event also provided a forum for the DE and H2 Learning to outline their plans for the final phase of the project, where they planned to survey all project clusters and to gather a selection of teacher stories from the clusters which were designed to showcase the work schools engaged in over the lifetime of the Programme. The event also provided reassurance to the participants in relation to what they achieved during the lifetime of the project, as many felt that COVID-19 had disrupted their plans and their activities. Despite this disruption, the majority of clusters adapted their plans and continued to engage with the support services to complete their projects. Following the event, H2 Learning developed the post-project survey to capture sample teacher stories. This work then informed the final data collection phase which was carried out during the 2022-2023 school year. www.h2.ie 13 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Post-project survey The post-project survey (see Appendix 4) was sent to Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors, with tailored questions for each group. The survey consisted of both closed (multiple choice) questions and open questions. The open questions explored three themes: 1. Experience of the project 2. Views on lessons learned (classroom practice, infrastructural models, support models and embedding digital education) 3. Views on possible future digital initiatives. In total 61 responses were received, 36 from Cluster Coordinators, 16 from other teachers and 9 from Oide TiE Advisors. The responses related to 34 clusters from the original 41 clusters. The results of the quantitative data from the closed questions are summarised as bar charts and are presented in Section 5. Responses to the open questions were grouped under the three main themes and examined using thematic analysis to identify sub-themes. Representative responses are included in this report to illustrate key findings. The results related to experience of the project are presented in Section 5, those related to lessons learned are presented in Section 6, and those related to possible future digital initiatives in Section 7. Frequencies quoted usually refer to numbers of responses – which includes those from Cluster Coordinators (sometimes more than one per cluster), teachers and Oide TiE Advisors, unless otherwise indicated. Teachers’ stories At the same time as the post-project survey, Cluster Coordinators were also asked by Oide TiE to create short project descriptions as ‘Teacher Stories’ for publication on the Oide TiE website. Twelve of these were received and reviewed to provide additional background information on specific projects. They are presented in Appendix 3. www.h2.ie 14 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 4: Interim evaluation report – Key findings The Interim Evaluation Report captured a wide range of data from the Cluster Coordinators on the makeup of the clusters and the activities they engaged in. This data was gathered from 35 of the 41 Cluster Coordinators, and Table 1 below captures the distribution of the responses.

They are presented in Appendix 3. www.h2.ie 14 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 4: Interim evaluation report – Key findings The Interim Evaluation Report captured a wide range of data from the Cluster Coordinators on the makeup of the clusters and the activities they engaged in. This data was gathered from 35 of the 41 Cluster Coordinators, and Table 1 below captures the distribution of the responses. The Interim Evaluation Report described the features of the cluster projects: the use of sophisticated technology (such as iPads, Lego WeDo kits, VR headsets), improved learner engagement and problem-solving skills; engagement of young women in STEM; examples of teacher collaboration; developments in curriculum planning; and also provided detailed case studies of the work of three of the clusters. The report also collected evidence that: • Most of the teachers involved in the project found the experience very positive, that it fostered good links between teachers across different schools, facilitating knowledge and expertise sharing, and that it advanced students' digital skills. • The SEF Digital & STEM Programme experience helped the move to online learning during lockdown, as learners and staff had developed skills in the use of online learning tools through project activities. • The clusters involved in the Programme were successful in disseminating their activities to the wider community, using a mix of websites (60%) and social media (mainly using Twitter)9. Some clusters also participated in or initiated large-scale public events. Table 3: Response to Cluster Coordinator Survey. All Post- All Mixed Mixed TOTAL primary primary post-primary led primary-led All 14 12 8 7 41 Responses 11 11 6 7 35 Percentage 79% 92% 75% 100% 85% Of the STEM projects 8 out of 9 responded, and of the Digital projects 27 out of 33 responded. Clusters and schools A total of 41 Clusters were funded, 33 Digital projects and 8 STEM projects. Of these, 35 responded to the Cluster Coordinator Survey in April 2020, which was reported as part of the Interim Evaluation Report. While some schools struggled to complete their End of Year Progress Reports for 2019, they were still active in the project, while two clusters withdrew from the project by the end of Year one. 9 Sixteen Clusters used Twitter, though many of these only tweeted a few times, or tweeted occasionally using the general school Twitter account. Some, however, were very active, nine Clusters tweeted more than 70 times in the period from the project start to 1 July 2020, and four of these tweeted more than 200 times. www.h2.ie 15 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 There were 222 schools in the 41 clusters that were funded. Of these 86 were post-primary (including 1 Further Education and Training College) and 136 were primary10. As to the composition of the 41 Clusters, 11 consisted of all post-primary schools, 13 of all primary schools, and 17 were mixed (post-primary led – 10, primary led – 7). The total number of schools in Ireland in 2019 was: 723 post-primary schools, 3,106 primary Mainstream Schools and 134 Special Schools. These figures indicate that 12% of post-primary schools were involved in the SEF Digital & STEM project, 4% of Primary Schools and 1% of Special Schools. Across Ireland, 890 schools were included in the DEIS programme11 in the 2019/20 school year. These compromised of 692 primary schools (334 urban and 358 rural) and 198 post-primary schools12. The numbers of DEIS schools in the project are shown in Table 4.

Figure 5: Word cloud of keywords used by Cluster Coordinators to describe their projects. www.h2.ie 18 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The frequency of occurrence of the five topmost keywords used is given in Table 5. Table 5: Frequency of five topmost keywords Keyword Frequency Collaboration 23 times Innovative 17 times Creative 11 times Fun 9 times Student-centred 7 times Thus, the majority of Cluster Coordinators from the outset highlighted the collaborative, innovative, creative, fun and student-centred nature of their projects. These elements continued to dominate the active projects over the lifetime of the Programme. Most innovative elements of the project The Cluster Coordinators were asked in the Interim Evaluation Report to identify the most innovative elements of their projects. Their responses could be classified in six categories: 1. Use of sophisticated technology (e.g., the use of equipment/infrastructure allotted for the project e.g., use of iPads, Lego WeDo kits, VR headsets etc.) 2. Inter-cluster collaboration 3. Teacher collaboration 4. Upskilling of learners (e.g., increase in learners’ skills e.g. project management, problem-solving, 21st-century skills) 5. Curriculum planning 6. Engagement of young women in STEM careers. The following graph indicates the number of responses in each of these categories. Increasing the engagement of young 1 women towards STEM careers Curriculum planning 2 Upskilling of learners 5 Teacher collaboration 7 Inter-cluster collaboration 2 Use of sophisticated technology 18 0 5 10 15 20 Figure 6: Most innovative element of the project. www.h2.ie 19 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Interestingly, the use of specific technologies ranked first, closely followed by the inter-cluster collaboration and teacher collaboration. Other benefits cited included support for curriculum planning and in increasing the engagement of young women in STEM careers possibilities. Collaboration featured strongly in the early stages of the Programme. Project achievements and issues Despite the challenging climate in which the project took place, the Cluster Coordinators reported positively on their achievements, while also identifying a range of issues that emerged in the initial phase of the project. Gains All 35 clusters reported important gains from taking part in the project. 22 out of 35 clusters (63%) described their experience as very positive. While schools may have overreached in terms of their project activities, the project provided a focus and opportunity for schools to work together and developing teacher knowledge and associated practices. "So far the SEF has given all schools a very positive experience. Despite the fact that we possibly overreached with content all schools report very positive benefits in terms of student advancement in ICT. Strong personal and professional bonds have been established between staff members in all schools. Significant spin off benefits in terms of staff sharing knowledge and expertise in areas well beyond the scope of the project." "It has been inspirational to see how teachers and pupils from 4 quite different schools have come together into what is now a movement in our local educational community. We were already doing some of these activities in a very occasional and ad-hoc way. SEF gave us an opportunity to do this in a properly planned and structured way, gave us the funding to put the basic resources in place and supported us along the way, in particular with the much- appreciated assistance of our Oide TiE mentor."

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "It has been inspirational to see how teachers and pupils from 4 quite different schools have come together into what is now a movement in our local educational community. We were already doing some of these activities in a very occasional and ad-hoc way. SEF gave us an opportunity to do this in a properly planned and structured way, gave us the funding to put the basic resources in place and supported us along the way, in particular with the much- appreciated assistance of our Oide TiE mentor." The gains in terms of teacher collaboration and professional networking were mentioned frequently (21 respondents - 60%) and probably constituted the clearest gain for many of the projects. "I have personally enjoyed the professional challenge that coordinating this project has afforded me. Taking on a leadership role has given me great confidence in my abilities and I look forward to embracing similar opportunities in the future. Working with other schools as part of a cluster has been hugely beneficial, sharing knowledge, resources, experience and best practice. We have formed a strong professional network, and I have learned so much from this process. I know that I can call on my STEM Champion colleagues for advice and support at any time." Eighteen Coordinators (51%) commented on the innovations in teaching they had made. "Our digital cluster are working together to create a bank of practical and engaging STEM lessons that can be used in Primary Schools. We have analysed the impact of this innovative pilot project on both teachers’ and students’ learning using qualitative (interviews) and quantitative (surveys) research methods to assess how and if their skills and attitudes have changed as a result of their participation with a view to spreading our project to other digital clusters, schools nationwide and beyond to Europe through involvement in Erasmus+ projects." www.h2.ie 20 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The creation and/or sharing of resources was mentioned by 16 Coordinators (46%). The creation of learning materials is typically undertaken by teachers in their own time and often on their own, but it is interesting to see that more collaborative approaches can work effectively. "Our project is a simple one, but the process has allowed all 6 schools to collaborate in such a way so as to create something of vast importance to all of the schools in our network." "We have discovered a great source of resources together and really branched out." "Digital citizenship and the development of digital literacy is very much at the fore of our project so to date participating teachers sourced/created learning resources and shared with the teachers in the other cluster schools by uploading to the cluster email." Other gains mentioned by a small number of clusters included development of leadership capacity, increased confidence in use of technology in teaching and learning, and contacts and links made that would continue into future collaborations. "3 out of 4 of the lead teachers participating in this project are not post holders. This group thus improves and strengthens middle management structures in all schools. Training and mentoring empower school leadership." "Teachers who never really used technology in their pedagogy are now using it more and more. This is an iPad project, and teachers are using the iPads for other areas of learning." "Looking to the future I hope that I can share the outcomes of this project (with other schools and parents). We plan to continue our learning cluster into the future (after the initial three years) and work on other projects together. This is just the beginning."

Training and mentoring empower school leadership." "Teachers who never really used technology in their pedagogy are now using it more and more. This is an iPad project, and teachers are using the iPads for other areas of learning." "Looking to the future I hope that I can share the outcomes of this project (with other schools and parents). We plan to continue our learning cluster into the future (after the initial three years) and work on other projects together. This is just the beginning." However, not all Cluster Coordinators had a positive experience, one for example stated: "My experience to date has been mixed, to be honest!" The next section describes the main issues that were described by the Cluster Coordinators. Reported issues Just over half of the clusters (N=18, 51%) reported a range of issues in the running of the Programme, with many reporting more than one issue. A summary of the reported issues is presented in Table 6, followed by a detailed description of each. Table 6: Issues reported by Cluster Coordinators. Issues Number of clusters reporting COVID-19 8 Time constraints 7 Project management 7 Finance 2 Lack of support 4 School level 5 Technology related 3 As was to be expected, COVID-19 had impacted negatively on many clusters (N=8, 23%). www.h2.ie 21 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "The project was somewhat stunted this year (2020) with uncertainty and lack of drive, staffing changes and now the extended school closure." Time was a big issue for some Cluster Coordinators (N=7, 20%): "On the flip side, I've found the workload as coordinator extremely demanding, given the huge range of tasks involved ranging from equipment research and procurement, through coordinating across four different schools - with different needs and priorities - to facilitating the students with their various projects. I found the hours allocated for the project to be wholly insufficient for the workload involved." "Perhaps hours should be pro rata, as clusters attempting a whole school role out should be given more time than if just one teacher per school is participating." "The substitute days for training are paramount to the future success of the project." Project management, including organisation of the clusters, management of goals (which were sometimes recognised as having been over ambitious), and uncertainty was an issue for some (N=7, 20%). "It has been challenging to gain and maintain momentum in a distributed cluster and in a SEF process that seems to be evolving." "At times I feel that perhaps we have not achieved some of the goals as originally set out in our application, but it has been a learning experience for everybody, and we have had to change our goals as time has passed." Finance was mentioned as an issue by two clusters (6%). "In our own case there was much uncertainty surrounding funding right through until January 2020 - 5 months into the school year." Some clusters felt that they sometimes did not have sufficient support (N=4, 11%). The types of support mentioned included: the frequency and content of professional development on offer from the Oide TiE Advisors, the unforeseen issues related to substitute cover, the possibility of engaging more deeply with other schools within their cluster. "The Oide TiE Advisor can only provide 1-day CPD per school. This appears to be the same number of days regardless of the scale of the role out. For the high number of teachers (almost 100) participating in the project, not all teachers and classes will avail of this service.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf The types of support mentioned included: the frequency and content of professional development on offer from the Oide TiE Advisors, the unforeseen issues related to substitute cover, the possibility of engaging more deeply with other schools within their cluster. "The Oide TiE Advisor can only provide 1-day CPD per school. This appears to be the same number of days regardless of the scale of the role out. For the high number of teachers (almost 100) participating in the project, not all teachers and classes will avail of this service. Perhaps hours should be pro rata, as clusters attempting a whole school role out should be given more time than if just one teacher per school is participating." "The Oide TiE need to be providing clusters with the latest training available (on par with our European counterparts) and when schools request training in a particular area related to their project it should be available by Oide TiE team or facilitators sourced free of charge." "In order to have meaningful collaboration in a school, teachers need to be able to bounce off each other rather than train each other. True collaboration then is not simply to work with someone but to 'think' with someone; to build idea on top of idea with others so you all get to a point you couldn't have reached alone." School level issues included lack of support from school management (N=2, 6%), and staff turnover (N=3, 9%). www.h2.ie 22 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Some members of staff were more enthusiastic than their school management." "Most participating schools have experienced a high number of new staff and also staff changing class level. A significant amount of time at the start of the year was spent with staff familiarising themselves with the project and developing the necessary skills." Technology inevitably had its problems (N=3, 9%) including software changes and delays, and weak technical infrastructure. Many of these issues were outside the control of the DE and the school clusters and involved issues with commercial software and learning platforms, in addition to ongoing challenges around technical support for schools and access to reliable broadband. "Although it was intended to introduce students to the … app earlier in the project, it is still in development with only intermittent access." "This project now has undertaken another substantial change since December 2019 when news of the Learner Management Software platform being used … announced its intention to cease trading with limited functionality until June 2020. This obviously frustrated many of the teachers involved in this project since." "To deliver a progressive project successfully we found that the necessary infrastructure needed to be in place for it to work throughout school buildings. A strong and stable broadband connection, managed Wi-Fi throughout schools with up-to-date devices." While these issues are beyond the control of the project, it is important to note that they did impact negatively on the experiences in schools. Critical reflection and evaluation Only four clusters (11%) made a specific reference to evaluation. This issue of evaluation or critical reflection was often ignored or interpreted narrowly by schools, and they seemed to put greater emphasis on project implementation than on systematic review. "We have analysed the impact of this innovative pilot project on both teachers’ and students’ learning using qualitative (interviews) and quantitative (surveys) research methods to assess how and if their skills and attitudes have changed as a result of their participation with a view to spreading our project to other digital clusters, schools nationwide and beyond to Europe through involvement in Erasmus+ projects."

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf This issue of evaluation or critical reflection was often ignored or interpreted narrowly by schools, and they seemed to put greater emphasis on project implementation than on systematic review. "We have analysed the impact of this innovative pilot project on both teachers’ and students’ learning using qualitative (interviews) and quantitative (surveys) research methods to assess how and if their skills and attitudes have changed as a result of their participation with a view to spreading our project to other digital clusters, schools nationwide and beyond to Europe through involvement in Erasmus+ projects." "These four schools are using the School Self Evaluation process to develop, implement and track oral language, writing and reading in various class groupings." Project communication Six (17%) of the clusters described their use of cluster meetings and training meetings to support the project: "We meet three times each term as a cluster. The first meeting discussed specific dates, outcomes and events for the term/year ahead. The second meeting is usually a training day to upskill teachers or pupils in different digital tools, technology or approaches." "Teachers continuously engage with each other via the cluster email. Feedback is gathered through this format and also through discussion at staff meetings and reverted to me by the co-ordinators in each school." www.h2.ie 23 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Thirteen (37%) described the organisation of showcase events to share work within the school, with other schools or with the wider community. This is particularly noteworthy as it provided schools with a chance to come together and showcase the work of their learners to the wider community. "At our first annual showcase, each primary school was able to demonstrate BeeBots in action, working WeDo engineering models, working programs written in Scratch on Raspberry Pi computers as well as some additional IT projects e.g. VEX robots, Music programs, Lego Mindstorm Robotics and Python programming on Raspberry Pi computers." "End of Year Showcase Event - June 2019 - An Róbó Seó. As a showcase event to highlight the work done by the cluster all of the participating cluster teachers and their classes set up in the auditorium of DCU (St. Patrick’s Campus) to display their Mission Moon projects. An Róbó Seó was the culmination of 9 months of collaboration between the Cluster Group, Prof. Deirdre Butler, Learnit and the Oide TiE. Over 320 children from the 6 participating schools, divided into 75 groups, were present. It was the largest ever First Lego League Junior event in Ireland or the UK and the largest STEM event to take place almost entirely through the medium of the Irish Language. Reviewers from Gael-Linn, COGG, Foras na Gaeilge and Gaeloideachas were in attendance on the day." Seventeen (49%) described their use of websites and blogs to disseminate their activities to the wider community. This figure is broadly consistent with searches at the time, which identified 61% of clusters overall with a public website. Where such websites and blogs existed, they provided staff and learners with multiple learning opportunities, while also enabling the schools to connect to a global audience. "The primary teachers conduct, record and document any practical activities undertaken, and the work is recorded currently on the virtual learning platform ‘Edmodo’ with the intention being to populate our cluster website with all materials." "We are also using websites to communicate to the parents/students." "The cluster website has gone live, and teachers can upload resources/links along with samples of the children’s projects."

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Where such websites and blogs existed, they provided staff and learners with multiple learning opportunities, while also enabling the schools to connect to a global audience. "The primary teachers conduct, record and document any practical activities undertaken, and the work is recorded currently on the virtual learning platform ‘Edmodo’ with the intention being to populate our cluster website with all materials." "We are also using websites to communicate to the parents/students." "The cluster website has gone live, and teachers can upload resources/links along with samples of the children’s projects." The creation of the website itself was also viewed by others as being a useful learning experience for teachers and students: "Creating a website has been a huge learning experience for all involved." Supporting online learning Whilst COVID-19 had a negative impact on many of the projects in June 2021, the work of the projects also supported schools’ transition to online learning during the initial phase of school closures from March 2020 to June 2021. Most respondents (N=26, 74%) said that the SEF Digital & STEM Programme experience helped with the online learning approaches used during lockdown. "Yes, the collaborative features employed in our different platforms have been used extensively. The project has aided remote learning hugely as the students became quite accustomed to using the online learning tools through project activities." www.h2.ie 24 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "It has been most beneficial to have digital portfolios set up and children/parents familiar with them. Seesaw has been the main means for communication used between primary school teachers and their students/parents in our cluster. Microsoft teams is being used by Secondary school staff and students who set up this platform with the support of our cluster Advisor during our project. These digital portfolios are being used across the curriculum not just PE however some of our schools have been busy with on twitter using #ActiveHomeWeek for primary schools and GAA Future leaders’ programme with St Nathy’s Secondary School. The digital portfolios have allowed teachers to correct & grade schoolwork returned digitally and give encouragement to students with comments. However not all students have the internet and devices to use these." "Primary Level - Yes, we had been using Seesaw as an e-portfolio for the pupils for use in school but had not extended it to give access outside the school. We have found this an invaluable tool when thrown in the deep end of distance learning and teaching with very little notice on March 12th. The pupils were already familiar and proficient in using this app, so it was less pressure on parents trying to access the information from the school. Teachers can assign work, and pupils can return work through the app allowing the teacher to correct and comment on each pupil’s individual work. The secondary school is using TEAMS and PowerPoint screen record which is great for making videos." However, four of the clusters (11%) commented that they were unable to continue work on the projects because of issues around access to technology: "No, as the iPads are within the schools which are out of bounds at the moment." "There is an issue around access to internet devices for many of the pupils in our schools." "No. Children don't have access to the Lego WeDo kits. In September (if school days are staggered) we hope to work out a rota where children can bring the materials home to create." Overall, participation in the SEF Digital & STEM Programme enabled schools to transfer many existing practices online during COVID-19.

Curriculum and goals Curriculum areas Table 7 shows the curriculum areas which clusters mentioned in their accounts. The total number of mentions is greater than the number of clusters because some clusters indicated more than one curriculum area. The largest grouping was that associated with Science, Mathematics and the related areas of STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics), STEAM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics) and SESE (Social, Environmental and Scientific Education). The focus on science and mathematics was in part explained by the fact that 8 of the projects were designated as STEM projects. One of the STEM projects did not mention STEM in their response, choosing to focus on problem-solving, but their initial proposal had identified maths and science as important areas for development of problem-solving skills. www.h2.ie 25 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Table 7: Curriculum areas mentioned by Clusters % of Curriculum area N Clusters Science, Mathematics, STEM, STEAM, SESE 19 54% English, Irish 6 17% Music 5 14% Art 4 11% Computer Science (including AI and User Experience Design) 4 11% Geography 2 6% History 2 6% Physical Education 1 3% Modern Languages 1 3% Cross-curricula 4 11% Curriculum planning 2 6% Amongst the Digital projects 12 out of 27 (44%) focused on Science, Mathematics, STEM, STEAM, or SESE. The following quotations capture the range of activities that schools undertook from using BBC micro:bit technology13, to embedding digital technology across the primary curriculum, to enhancing PE classes in post-primary to enhancing oral language and reimagining the Junior Cycle experience for students. "Students are exploring STEM activities using a range of technology: 1. Scratch with Computer Science (CS) first. 2. Micro:bit programming with inventor kits. 3. Sphero programming 4. 3D Design and Printing. The project sees second level students using their skills to help STEM grow at primary level." The narration is recorded, paying attention to voice pitch and clarity. A Digital Story (DS) is created using programmes such as iMovie which is no longer than 3 minutes. The story provides evidence of learning. The DS assessment has a rubric that includes traffic lights for keywords, the main message of learning, and what it means to the creators. All work is original, can be symbolic, metaphorical, or literal." "We have enhanced the Physical Education Programme at both Primary and Secondary School level in our cluster through the use of the video app on iPads by students and teachers to give effective peer assessment and feedback in their PE lessons." "Our project is based on the use of iPads to implement and enhance the oral language curriculum in both English and Irish. In term one in each school, we focus on E-portfolios, namely SeeSaw. Students record themselves using the voice message for both English and Irish. In term two we focus on digital storytelling using apps such as iMovie, Stop Motion Studio, Chatter Pix, Doink Greenscreen and Toontastic. Classes in each school create movies in both English and Irish. In term three the focus is on Coding using Scratch. Students code animations and games and record their own voice within the animation."

The Framework for JC has required a mind shift change in educational thinking over the past number of years and as teachers grapple with this the 13 https://microbit.org/ www.h2.ie 26 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 fruits of their labour are reflected in the high quality of work presented to engage learners in a blended learning approach which is very fitting in the times, we now find ourselves in." Goals Outside specific curriculum goals, 22 (63%) clusters mentioned teaching goals related to digital literacies in a broad sense. In this category the analysis included a variety of terms and counted clusters using one or more of the following closely related terms: digital literacy (5 Clusters), digital skills (9), Key Skills (2), 21st Century Skills (6). Related terms were also noted, including: safe and ethical use of technology (3), digital citizenship (2), problem-solving (11), computational thinking (8), critical thinking (3). "These lifelong skills of investigating, communicating, teamwork, reflecting etc. are explored and developed alongside the development of digital skills." "Digital Citizenship and the development of digital literacy is very much at the fore of our project so to date participating teachers sourced/created learning resources and shared with the teachers in the other cluster schools by uploading to the cluster email." Some clusters referred to wider and broader educational goals: gender equality (2), student engagement and motivation (6), student autonomy (3), insight into career paths (1), development of leadership capacity by students (1), involving students in their own learning (1). The following statements capture how the introduction of digital technologies acted as a trigger to develop new skills and insights for students. "Students of … are trained to become STEM role models for younger female students in primary schools. The STEM role models have visited all three primary schools twice this year." "Through the project we are building leadership capacity amongst the students by training students as iHelpers and giving visibility to their role as peer supports for their fellow students." "The opportunity for students to lead projects and have real and meaningful autonomy over their work both in terms of content covered but also choice around what subjects to attend (Choose your Own Subject -TY)." "We set out to improve senior cycle students' access to real insight into college and career paths shared by college students and professionals in a variety of fields, so that they can make more informed and confident choices." www.h2.ie 27 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Pedagogy Constructivism The definition of constructivism in the Digital Strategy for Schools, 2015-202014 is described as follows: "A constructivist pedagogical orientation supports teachers in effectively using ICT with their students i.e., learners are actively involved in a process of determining meaning and knowledge for themselves."
