Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "For sustainable practice & Long-term impact, the main reasons are Funding, Staffing & Time / Timetabling...Teachers need to be properly trained & resourced... I propose a post-grad similar to the one that up-skilled existing teachers to become Maths Teachers." "Time constraints. Our main area of focus was coding. As this is not yet on the curriculum, making time for coding in a 4 class multi- class and an overloaded curriculum is our biggest challenge." "Time needs to be given specifically to development of projects in order for them to be sustainable. There is a need for sustained support from outside agencies in order to drive projects and coordinate projects and not just an email at the start and end of the year or when there is a change in personnel in Oide TiE." "The pandemic shelved our ability to interact with the technology for two full years. The high cost of the technology also provides a barrier to allowing the students to fully integrate with its practices." "Over time new robotic kits suitable for primary school pupils will become available or the kits we currently use will become obsolete. Funding replacement equipment may become a problem in the long term." "In our project, we (the secondary school) ran STEM workshops for primary school students... The cost of transport has increased hugely, and so, we have not been able to continue running these workshops since the funding and the programme finished." "Shockingly bad Internet. And we are in a town!!!" The issue of time appeared several times in the comments from teachers and Cluster Coordinators, as does the issue of curriculum relevance and ongoing support. Respondents noted that there was a need for ongoing support in keeping programmes such as SEF Digital & STEM alive and evolving. Other issues such as the cost of technology and lack of broadband were also a reality for some schools. Funding The survey also asked teachers and Cluster Coordinators “has funding (for replacement/ updating of equipment, training and support) been an issue for sustainability and long-term impact of the project?” In response 57% said Yes and 33% said No. Respondents highlighted www.h2.ie 49 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 the following issues in relation to project financing: funding for replacement or updating of equipment and infrastructure, ongoing maintenance and repairs. "Expensive equipment that requires updating like any other piece of technology. It still works but at some point, it will become obsolete or unable to function due to continued updates, much like an iPad." "The initial funding bought a limited number of resources that could be used among the vast number of teachers (close to 100) and students (almost 1400) taking part in the Cluster Project." "Funding for ICT is always a challenge when considering the maintenance costs of equipment." "Lack of spend for music now that SEF fund no longer available. As music departments grow or are established in schools, we do not have the capacity to scale the equipment needs." "Although we are a town school, our internet is shockingly bad. Due to the fact that we purchased extra devices, more internet was required. We are prevented from embracing Digital Technology to its fullest due to poor internet." These comments capture a spectrum of the issues schools faced in relation to funding and they highlight the costs associated with procuring and updating a range of digital technologies.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "Lack of spend for music now that SEF fund no longer available. As music departments grow or are established in schools, we do not have the capacity to scale the equipment needs." "Although we are a town school, our internet is shockingly bad. Due to the fact that we purchased extra devices, more internet was required. We are prevented from embracing Digital Technology to its fullest due to poor internet." These comments capture a spectrum of the issues schools faced in relation to funding and they highlight the costs associated with procuring and updating a range of digital technologies. While the cost of some digital technologies, such as computers, has come down over time, there is still a significant outlay for schools in terms of procuring sufficient equipment for staff and learners and ensuring it is regularly maintained. A number of these issues pertain to the sustainability of certain technologies and their suitability for schools, an issue that could be explored further in the future. Finally, the issue of access to quality internet provision again was also raised, as more and more technology requires access to reliable fast internet services. While most of the comments related to the additional funding to procure digital technologies or related services, it is interesting that no one commented on the need for additional discretionary funding to release staff from teaching duties or to attend additional professional learning activities. Regulation and data privacy Whilst the survey did not directly ask for comments related to ethics and regulation, it is nevertheless surprising that there were almost no comments made in relation to ethics, data privacy, GDPR or other data regulations, neither in relation to wider societal concerns with respect to the use of technologies, nor in terms of the duties of teachers. One Cluster Coordinator noted the need for “support for managing data and compliance with data protection regulations.” This is an issue that could be further explored in the future, as more and more digital platforms, tools and applications are capturing and using teacher and student data. Experience of the project – concluding comments Participants’ experience of the SEF Digital & STEM Programme was overwhelmingly positive, though around 10% of Cluster Coordinators and teachers had quite negative experiences. The Programme led to a range of positive outcomes, it contributed to better student engagement, innovative teaching methods, and collaboration among schools. The Programme was impacted by several factors which were outside of its control, particularly the COVID-19 pandemic and www.h2.ie 50 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 changes in personnel (an issue made worse by the epidemic and by the overall length of the extended Programme). The disruptions caused by COVID-19 appeared to have a major impact on project activities, as was the case in many other areas of education. The pandemic impacted negatively on face-to-face collaborative events, school project plans and seriously disrupted momentum. Other specific issues of concern that were raised most frequently in the post-project survey related to communication, coordination, and administration of the Programme; technical support, infrastructural issues; and funding. www.h2.ie 51 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 6: Post-Project Survey - Lessons learned This section summarises the responses of the Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors when asked to comment on lessons learned in relation to the four themes that the SEF Digital & STEM Programme sought to address: • Classroom practice in relation to the embedding of digital technologies and constructivist approaches to teaching, learning and assessment at primary and post primary levels.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf www.h2.ie 51 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 6: Post-Project Survey - Lessons learned This section summarises the responses of the Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors when asked to comment on lessons learned in relation to the four themes that the SEF Digital & STEM Programme sought to address: • Classroom practice in relation to the embedding of digital technologies and constructivist approaches to teaching, learning and assessment at primary and post primary levels. • The infrastructural models that can best support innovation in relation to teaching, learning and assessment, communication, and collaborative planning. • The most appropriate support models that can be put in place to facilitate innovation in schools. • How the embedding of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment within and across all schools can be achieved. In this section the report presents a high-level synopsis of the key findings from the qualitative data analysis on this topic in Table 9 below. This is then followed by a detailed account of the supporting evidence for these findings including quotations from the respondents. Table 9: High-level synopsis of the key findings. • Effective strategies included student-led projects, collaborative Good Classroom learning and personalised learning. In such cases teachers often Practice moved from structured to more open approaches over time. Need for: • Strong broadband. • Physical space for housing and securing technology. • Hardware and devices, for both technologies for general use, but Infrastructural Models also specialist equipment for specific areas. • Software and platforms (i.e. Google, Microsoft and learning management systems). • Standardisation and clear policy. • Importance of continuing professional development and training and peer-to-peer collaboration. • Participants wanted practical, up-to-date training tailored to their needs. In-school and peer-led models were preferred. Some Support Models questioned the value of private providers (i.e. those who developed certain technologies or software products providing training). • Technical support. • Administrative and institutional support. • The sustained Oide TiE support model was widely praised. • Embedding required long-term funding, training, time, Embedding Digital collaboration, curriculum alignment, Technologies • Relation to assessment needs consideration. • Evaluation of effectiveness of technology integration is required. www.h2.ie 52 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Good classroom practice Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors were asked to describe what they saw as effective strategies and techniques for creating an engaging and effective learning environment for students when employing digital technologies. Many (50% of responses) stressed the importance of student-led projects, with students working collaboratively in pairs or groups. "Student-led and engaging practices worked very well for us. We trained secondary school students to become STEM role models who then ran workshops for the younger primary school students. This student-led model ensured engagement at both primary and post- primary level." "Project based learning. The new leaving certificate computer science syllabus uses applied learning tasks. We wanted to give the students a set of foundational skills and have them develop projects in teams. Students were given access to online platforms that would facilitate them developing their own solutions to the challenges given." "Allowing children to be creative in their learning so that they have an input into what and how they learn. Having children working in pairs or small groups to help each other and learn from each other is hugely beneficial. Having children sharing their expertise with other children ensures much more effective learning." Respondents highlighted a range of innovative approaches they engaged in with their learners, from students leading learning to actively collaborating with their peers and teachers in their learning activities.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "Allowing children to be creative in their learning so that they have an input into what and how they learn. Having children working in pairs or small groups to help each other and learn from each other is hugely beneficial. Having children sharing their expertise with other children ensures much more effective learning." Respondents highlighted a range of innovative approaches they engaged in with their learners, from students leading learning to actively collaborating with their peers and teachers in their learning activities. Learners, both in primary and post-primary settings, used digital technologies to engage more actively with their own learning, something teachers noted led to more effective learning. Some respondents described specific ways in which digital technologies could be integrated into classroom practice. "Clearly defining where technology can be of benefit in the classroom and not including it for the sake of using technology. Teachers have to be smart in how it is used and incorporated into lessons to ensure the maximum benefit is achieved. technology is a tool to be used but is not the be all and end all of the educational experience." "We started out with very structured and well-defined lessons for the children to follow. When the children became familiar with the resources, we broadened out the scope of the challenges that we set them, allowing them to start with the basics and then encouraging them to develop and extend their knowledge through experimentation and investigation. Finally, we were able to integrate the use of the resources with the topics that were being followed in class at the time and gave the children the freedom to follow their own initiative when using the resources." "Digital technologies can provide opportunities for personalised learning experiences, where students can work at their own pace and level. Teachers can use digital tools to differentiate instruction, allowing students to engage with content at their own level and pace." "There needs to be a clear framework where children develop and progress their skills on a year-on-year basis." www.h2.ie 53 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 These comments capture the critical reflections of teachers in relation to how they and learners used technology. Teachers critically reflected on how they were using technology and noted that it should never be used just for the sake of using it. Technology should be used for specific purposes, and it is important to capture and share these with colleagues. The comments also capture the journey that staff undertook with digital technologies, and their role and that of their learners evolved over time. Specifically, teachers gave students more freedom to engage in self- regulated learning over time and moved from highly structured to less structured activities, while others used the technology to support more differentiated learning. Finally, some teachers noted that there was a need for more supports around developing student digital skills over time and this was addressed within the Literacy, Numeracy and Digital Literacy Strategy developed by the Department of Education in 202418. These lessons could be captured and shared more widely with all teachers in the future. Several respondents stressed the importance of reflection and self-assessment in the learning process. "Digital technologies can be used to enhance the learning experience by incorporating them into the curriculum. Robotics can be used in the classroom to promote active learning - this can help students develop problem-solving skills and critical thinking skills. Coding skills introduces coding concepts and principles through games and puzzles.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf These lessons could be captured and shared more widely with all teachers in the future. Several respondents stressed the importance of reflection and self-assessment in the learning process. "Digital technologies can be used to enhance the learning experience by incorporating them into the curriculum. Robotics can be used in the classroom to promote active learning - this can help students develop problem-solving skills and critical thinking skills. Coding skills introduces coding concepts and principles through games and puzzles. A constructivist approach to teaching and learning encourages student-centred learning, this can be facilitated by providing opportunities for students to collaborate, problem-solve, and share their ideas." "Formative assessment strategies allow teachers to assess student progress throughout the learning process. This can involve providing feedback on student work, using self- assessment tools, and providing opportunities for peer assessment. Pupil reflection is an important part of the learning process pupils are encouraged to write reflections, share their thoughts with peers, and engage in class discussions." "Students were engaged in the process and technology played a big part in assessment of learning." The first comment above is particularly interesting and highlights the opportunities for digital technologies to enhance existing learning experiences. It also highlights a range of approaches and activities that can promote student-centred learning by encouraging learners to be more reflective. The comments also emphasise the potential role of digital technology in assessment, specifically formative assessment, where students can use data to reflect on their progress with their peers. Infrastructural models When asked about the most appropriate infrastructural models to support innovation in relation to teaching, learning and assessment, a few respondents provided quite comprehensive overviews: "The best infrastructure and technological framework for supporting innovation in education will depend on the specific needs, goals, available resources and budget of the individual school. Cloud based infrastructure provides an efficient and flexible way to store, manage, 18 https://www.gov.ie/en/department-of-education/publications/literacy-numeracy-and-digital-literacy-strategy/ www.h2.ie 54 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 and access educational content and services. This allows for scalability as resources can be scaled up or down as needed. Learning analytics can provide insights into student progress, engagement and preferences and can support personalised and adaptive learning. Good wireless network infrastructure provides reliable and secure connectivity for pupils and teachers to access educational content and services. One-to-one or one-to-two device ratio, iPad or laptop, supports personalised and self-directed learning as well as collaborative learning and assessment. Mobile device management allows schools to manage and monitor mobile devices. It provides features such as app management, content filtering and remote device management." "The following is essential in a digitally emerging school: Robust speedy internet connection with adequate bandwidth Safe internet monitoring for pupil and wider school community Adequate internal network points with up to date switching managed Wi-Fi of APs is a must in larger school areas where there is a steadily increasing number of mobile devices in use from year to year There needs to be a stronger more collaborative link between the Schools Broadband Service and schools internal network provision. Dual provision of internet providers so if one provider fails a second is available to provide continuity. Data storage and cloud providers are an essential part of digitally enabled schools. A dedicated portal for all government, local authority, 3rd level colleges, 2nd level schools, 1st level primary schools, libraries and preschools would be a very aspirational wish." These comments highlight the need for robust internet connectivity to the door of the school and corresponding technologies within the school to enable teachers and learners to access digital tools safely and efficiently.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Dual provision of internet providers so if one provider fails a second is available to provide continuity. Data storage and cloud providers are an essential part of digitally enabled schools. A dedicated portal for all government, local authority, 3rd level colleges, 2nd level schools, 1st level primary schools, libraries and preschools would be a very aspirational wish." These comments highlight the need for robust internet connectivity to the door of the school and corresponding technologies within the school to enable teachers and learners to access digital tools safely and efficiently. They capture the complexity of these evolving digital technologies and how these tools can enhance student learning. Schools indicated a desire for more advice on these technologies and more connected platforms across multiple education levels and stakeholders. Broadband Broadband, Wi-Fi or network connectivity were mentioned as issues by 48% of those responding to this question, highlighting the need for high-speed, reliable broadband connections to enable digital learning and support the use of technology in schools. "One of the biggest restrictions there is at the minute is the speed of internet coming in to the schools. The fact that schools are being supplied with only 300mb when the area has up to 1GB capacity is laughable." The issue of robust broadband provision has been highlighted by schools for many years and is also a concern at EU level, where it is regarded as a key enabling factor in the provision of digital education19. There is an increased need for reliable fast broadband in schools with the growing reliance on cloud computing services and an ever-increasing number of digital devices being deployed in schools. Physical space The survey captured the need for greater investment in locating and using digital technologies. 19 See the Digital Education Action Plan, https://education.ec.europa.eu/focus-topics/digital-education/action- plan/action-1?/focus-topics/digital-education/action-plan/action-1=#Proposal www.h2.ie 55 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Space is vital for any project. I think if schools are to deliver anything they have to consider security of tech, housing of tech and so on." Hardware and devices Many teachers indicated the need for technologies for general use such as iPads, Chromebooks, or laptops, whereas others pointed to specific technologies for specialist use such as micro:bits and LEGO kits for STEM projects. "There needs to be sufficient hardware and software (ideally 1 per pupil)." Software and platforms Many teachers mentioned the value of specific software tools and platforms such as Google Workspace (including Google Drive, Docs, Slides, Classroom), the Microsoft suite, Learning Management Systems, and various educational apps. "Cloud-based infrastructure provides an efficient and flexible way to store, manage, and access educational content and services." "The use of GSuite has really benefited our school with the input of everything from Gmail to Drive and constant use of Docs and Slides." These comments highlight once more the use of cloud-based solutions that rely on robust internet provision. Standardisation and policy Several responses advocated for a standardised model of digital infrastructure. "The department should provide a one for all model of infrastructure as at the moment it differs greatly between schools. Some schools with no IT experienced teachers can struggle. Some schools rely on very expensive IT companies to help them. Having one model for all schools would help to train teachers nationally and would help collaboration between schools. The main issue with this now is that most schools will be reluctant to change to a new system having put a lot of effort and money into developing their current model." This comment suggests that some schools in the Programme have struggled with procuring and implementing digital infrastructural models.

Having one model for all schools would help to train teachers nationally and would help collaboration between schools. The main issue with this now is that most schools will be reluctant to change to a new system having put a lot of effort and money into developing their current model." This comment suggests that some schools in the Programme have struggled with procuring and implementing digital infrastructural models. However, the respondent noted that this may not be feasible in the future as schools have already invested heavily in their existing systems. Once again it highlights the issues schools face in procuring, installing and providing staff with adequate supports to use these expensive technologies effectively. This issue might be further explored in any future programmes. Support models One of the Cluster Coordinators provided a comprehensive overview of support models (presented in the table below for ease of reading). The Coordinator had given this issue considerable thought and provided a detailed response paraphrased below. www.h2.ie 56 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Table 10: Support model suggestions. • Provide ongoing professional development for educators on Professional innovation, emerging technologies, and new pedagogies. This can Development be done through workshops, seminars, online courses, and other approaches. • Provide technical support to ensure that the necessary infrastructure and hardware are in place and working efficiently. Technical Support This includes providing access to software and applications that support innovative teaching and learning. • Provide administrative support to teachers and school leaders to Administrative Support facilitate innovation. This includes streamlining administrative processes and providing access to funding and resources. • Support the development of innovative curricula and lesson plans Curriculum Design that promote creativity, critical thinking, and problem-solving. • Encourage peer collaboration and networking opportunities to Peer Collaboration share best practices, innovative ideas, and successful implementation strategies. • Involve parents and families in the innovation process by creating Parental Involvement opportunities for them to learn and contribute. • Establish partnerships with external agencies, such as Partnerships with universities, businesses, and community organizations, to support External Agencies innovation in schools. This can include providing access to experts and resources, as well as funding opportunities. Such a comprehensive support model, that integrates all these components, could be more effective in facilitating digital innovation in any future schools' projects. "A technical support team could provide assistance with hardware and software issues and maintenance. This team could also provide guidance on the appropriate use of technology in the classroom and ensure that devices and software are up to date. Professional development can support teachers in developing the necessary skills and knowledge to effectively use technology in the classroom. This could include training on new technologies and pedagogical approaches as well as ongoing support and mentorship. Administrative support could include guidance on procurement, budgeting and policy development related to technology in the classroom. This can also include support for managing data and compliance with data protection regulations. Technology suppliers provide customer service and support for the devices and software used in the classroom. This can include support for setting up and configuring devices, troubleshooting issues and providing maintenance and updates. Pedagogical support could provide guidance on how to integrate technology into the curriculum in a meaningful and effective way. This could include support for developing lesson plans and assessments that incorporate technology as well as support for evaluating the impact of technology on student learning." "The success of the 'support model' does have a very high dependency on the 'human resource'.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Technology suppliers provide customer service and support for the devices and software used in the classroom. This can include support for setting up and configuring devices, troubleshooting issues and providing maintenance and updates. Pedagogical support could provide guidance on how to integrate technology into the curriculum in a meaningful and effective way. This could include support for developing lesson plans and assessments that incorporate technology as well as support for evaluating the impact of technology on student learning." "The success of the 'support model' does have a very high dependency on the 'human resource'. A lot has to be carefully put in place so that the area of successful digital technology development can take place in schools. These are different from school to school. Different countries use different models, working at government level, local authority, school districts or municipalities and even schools operating in an insular way providing their own support. There is no doubt but there are certain support structures that www.h2.ie 57 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 must be considered by schools so they can successfully engage and develop their own digital development." The above comments are particularly comprehensive and identify a range of supports or enabling factors that ideally should be in place to support innovative digital practices. Respondents felt that these resources should span a range of areas, such as professional development, curriculum design, technical support and administrative support. The suggestions around administrative support are notable, as they include data compliance and protection. They suggest providing support on establishing partnerships with industry and third level institutions. There is also a suggestion that any innovation support model should have sufficient human resources, to enable full engagementwith digital education in a meaningful way. Most of the Oide TiE Advisors referenced the importance of a sustained support model. "In my opinion the sustained support model is an ideal support model to facilitate innovation in schools. This model allows for multiple support sessions building on targets and achievements over the course of an agreed timeframe." "The sustained support model provides ongoing professional development opportunities for teachers, which is critical for supporting the embedding of digital technology in schools. Teachers need to develop their knowledge and skills to effectively integrate technology into teaching and learning practices. Ongoing professional development ensures that teachers are continuously improving their skills and staying up to date with the latest digital technologies." Here again the advisors highlighted the need for ongoing regular support interactions with schools over time. The embedding of digital educational practices takes time and commitment from schools, and this view was shared regularly by respondents. They noted that the nature of the support depends on the school context. Professional development and support Many respondents to this question (50%) highlighted the need for continuous professional development and the provision of practical, manageable, and regularly updated training materials. "Training and professional development for teachers needs to be free and training provided needs to be in areas of new digital technology and skills – rather than lagging behind the rest of Europe we need to be on par or ahead of other European countries using new up-to-date materials." "During this programme, I became familiar with the Bridge 21 model through Trinity College Dublin. I would recommend teachers to complete similar training for CPD." "Profession development courses need to change on a regular basis rather than the same courses being delivered year after year and very little access to new material." "Training with tech experts. Tech companies to visit schools and give practical solutions to each building i.e.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "During this programme, I became familiar with the Bridge 21 model through Trinity College Dublin. I would recommend teachers to complete similar training for CPD." "Profession development courses need to change on a regular basis rather than the same courses being delivered year after year and very little access to new material." "Training with tech experts. Tech companies to visit schools and give practical solutions to each building i.e. broadband/Wi-Fi still a big issue in most schools as well as solutions for tech i.e. iPad and app management systems etc." www.h2.ie 58 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "I have found that the simplest support networks work best. We have used a WhatsApp group to work with other teachers in the local schools. I have found that a training day works well but if you have a group that has a wide level of abilities when it comes to using technology then a training day will not get the benefits that you want. Support after an initial meeting is a very effective way of seeing improvements." They expressed a desire for a wide range of courses that would constantly evolve to include new or emerging technologies. Where possible they would prefer these courses/events to be free of charge. The reference to Bridge 2120 is interesting, as this is a project in Trinity College and highlights that there is a wide range of providers in this space that schools can avail of as well as the supports available from Oide TiE, the education centre network and others. Ultimately schools indicated a need for quality professional learning experiences that would help them utilise and maintain their existing digital technologies. There were differing views on the value of ‘external’ training. "For example, we purchased our iPads from Wriggle and so received training on app management through JamSchool and whole school training on device and app use." "Private companies have a mixed success rate in our experience." "We rarely use the Oide TiE. At times we consult the company who supplied out iPad/laptops." Again, these comments capture the complexity and variety of professional development options schools can avail themselves of depending on the topic and issues they require support with. Some wanted more explicit professional learning opportunities at post-primary level, rather than generalised ‘support’. "A subject-by-subject teaching plan with online webinars on how to implement the teaching plan. Just like the book publishers do with their teaching plans for teachers. Or equally, add to the book publishers’ plans, by showing what and how you can use digital technology with their books/scheme. Keep it small, practical and manageable with the heavy teacher workload taken out of it, speaking as a teacher with 4 classes." "In-school support through modelling in the classroom." Some respondents expressed a preference for very practical hands-on suggestions on how to use digital technology in specific curriculum contexts, while others wanted to see digital practices modelled in real classroom settings. 20 https://tft-project.scss.tcd.ie/index.php/the-bridge21-model/ www.h2.ie 59 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Peer-to-peer collaboration and support Many (20% of respondents) emphasised the importance of networking, sharing knowledge, observing and showcasing the work of other educators or schools. Suggestions included local and regional collaborations, communities of practice, teach-meets, and the use of digital platforms for communication. "Facilitated collaborative models like communities of practice. Opportunities to share/ observe the work of other schools/ digital leaders." Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf 20 https://tft-project.scss.tcd.ie/index.php/the-bridge21-model/ www.h2.ie 59 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Peer-to-peer collaboration and support Many (20% of respondents) emphasised the importance of networking, sharing knowledge, observing and showcasing the work of other educators or schools. Suggestions included local and regional collaborations, communities of practice, teach-meets, and the use of digital platforms for communication. "Facilitated collaborative models like communities of practice. Opportunities to share/ observe the work of other schools/ digital leaders." "There is a need for a more local support person in an area for schools to work with schools on integrating technology into learning. Having a person in a school that can work with other schools in a geographical area would be of huge benefit. building a relationship of trust takes time and needs to be worked on over a number of years rather than changing from year to year." The idea of a ‘local support person’ for digital education existed in the past, as the 1998 digital education plan, Schools IT200021, made provision for ICT Advisors to perform such a role and they were located in education centres. Ultimately the idea of capturing and sharing digital practices with colleagues was strongly valued by those responding to the survey, particularly during the project phase when they had time and resources to sustain such activity. Technical support The issue of technical support was mentioned frequently (23% of teachers and cluster coordinator respondents), specifically in relation to maintaining and deploying technology hardware. "Redirect the funding from people with the ideas towards qualified technicians. Employ a company that will visit a cluster of schools on a rotational basis. They can then fix the computers, laptops, iPad, Wi-Fi, boards etc. The teachers can then focus on using the technologies and the technicians can make sure that they work." "Expecting teachers to keep a technology infrastructure up and running is every shade of crazy. It is infuriating, pointless and dispiriting. Teachers teach. Managers in the school try to manage. There is no space for keeping hardware working." "A school or any institution that relies on hundreds of devices, a complex network as well as hundreds of other ancillary hardware needs a full-time technician to be effective in its management. At present, teachers maintain these complex systems in their few non- teaching hours." Respondents also highlighted well known issues related to technical support and broadband infrastructure. "A formal, structured and possibly centralised technical support system is essential." "Ongoing repairs. Get this part correct and everything else will follow." The issue of technical support emerged a number of times in this report. There was a strong message that teachers teach, and administrators manage, and that there needs to be additional support to schools to ensure that devices and networks were working effectively. The 21 https://www.gov.ie/en/department-of-education/publications/schools-it-2000/ www.h2.ie 60 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 comments suggest a range of possible solutions, and this is an area that could be further explored in future programmes. Administrative and institutional support Several responses reported that they felt they lacked access to sufficiently detailed guidance on how to approach the management of their project finances. Others reported a need for more guidance on their projects in general. "Administrative support could include guidance on procurement, budgeting, and policy development related to technology in the classroom." "Oversight in all areas. The whole process was very loose, with respect, infrequent check- ins, left to our own devices (excuse the pun)." These comments suggest some people wanted more contact with the Department, other clusters and Oide TiE to ensure they were on the right track.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Others reported a need for more guidance on their projects in general. "Administrative support could include guidance on procurement, budgeting, and policy development related to technology in the classroom." "Oversight in all areas. The whole process was very loose, with respect, infrequent check- ins, left to our own devices (excuse the pun)." These comments suggest some people wanted more contact with the Department, other clusters and Oide TiE to ensure they were on the right track. On a different issue related to institutional support, several responses reported difficulty in obtaining substitute cover, even though funding was available for it. Embedding of digital technologies The Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors were asked for their opinions on how best to achieve the embedding of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment within and across all schools. Funding Many respondents (29%) highlighted the need for adequate funding for hardware, software, internet connectivity and maintenance. "Through appropriate funding to schools so that all pupils have access to digital technology. At present schools may have to share a set of 16 tablets in classes of 30. Funding is necessary to provide more hardware and software to embed digital technology into the classroom." "The Digital Strategy for Schools to 2027 provides a framework for the integration of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment across all schools in Ireland. Adequate funding and resources will be necessary to support the implementation of the national strategy." "Completion, sharing and familiarisation of the agreed digital framework for schools will greatly help to embedding digital technologies coupled with the correct funding to aid development and creative skills." "A department-led, broadscale funded model that includes technicians, and a central procuring system would be the only system that can ensure this." The provision, upkeep and replacement of digital technologies are costly and requires ongoing funding. Schools are acutely aware of the costs involved in procuring and maintaining these technologies and called for adequate funding. www.h2.ie 61 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Professional development and time Many respondents (49%) stressed the need for appropriate teacher professional development and time. This was referenced in relation to professional development and in providing more time for Cluster Coordinators. "Teacher training is key to embedding digital tech in reading, learning and assessment. Continuous Funding is also required as technology is constantly requiring updating." "Professional development for teachers: Teachers need to be equipped with the necessary knowledge and skills to effectively integrate digital technologies into teaching and learning practices." "More time (with sub cover) for IT co coordinators in the schools and clusters to meet, plan and implement change." Though some thought that compulsion was necessary: "By making it compulsory to include such practice within your teaching and learning." There was a recognition that staff, at all levels of education, require ongoing professional development supports and that adequate time and funding should be made available to enable teachers to meet and share their practices. Collaboration and consistency Some (15%) respondents noted the need for collaboration across schools and with other institutions. "The most important thing in embedding technology across a school is a whole school approach and teachers working together and sharing their knowledge." "Consistency. Two opposing schools running oppositional technologies doesn't benefit the educational landscape. It should be collaborative within geographical areas also." "Common sharing platform (e.g. Teams/OneDrive) common and shared outcomes which are measured." "The model of colleges/ universities providing support to secondary schools and secondary schools supporting primary schools is definitely a model that needs to be built upon. It is a model that gives huge collaborative support to teachers."

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "The most important thing in embedding technology across a school is a whole school approach and teachers working together and sharing their knowledge." "Consistency. Two opposing schools running oppositional technologies doesn't benefit the educational landscape. It should be collaborative within geographical areas also." "Common sharing platform (e.g. Teams/OneDrive) common and shared outcomes which are measured." "The model of colleges/ universities providing support to secondary schools and secondary schools supporting primary schools is definitely a model that needs to be built upon. It is a model that gives huge collaborative support to teachers." The comments suggested that it is better if schools adopt a whole school approach and that clusters, where possible, use common platforms, devices and applications. Such approaches helped promote a collaborative culture within and between schools. The final comment suggests that the involvement of higher education institutions (HEIs) could provide collaborative support to teachers. However, it is not clear how this might occur, as there was limited evidence of such approaches in the project. Curriculum redesign Some respondents stressed the need for curriculum change. "Curriculum integration: Digital technologies should be integrated into the curriculum across all subjects and grade levels." www.h2.ie 62 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Align curriculum objectives with digital technology (DT) best practice - teachers have a curriculum to deliver and must see the value in DT in helping achieve this." "Focus on student centred learning, fostering problem-solving, creativity and differentiation for all learners. Technology used as an effective tool for learning across a range of subject areas." These comments highlight that teachers are primarily tasked with implementing the curriculum in their school and thus digital education should be embedded within the curriculum and not seen as something additional. While this is increasingly the case, some respondents felt that it needed further strengthening, through more explicit guidance on where and how to use digital technology across the curriculum. Assessment and evaluation Respondents highlighted both the need for involving technology in assessment of student learning, and the need for evaluation of the effectiveness of technology integration. "Schools should explore the potential of using technology in assessment to a great extent." "Ongoing evaluation and assessment of the impact of the integration of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment will be necessary to determine the effectiveness of the national strategy and to identify areas for improvement." The first comment reflected earlier themes regarding curriculum integration and the use of formative assessment. The issue of ongoing evaluation of the national digital strategy is an important issue that many other countries are also facing. www.h2.ie 63 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 7: Post-Project Survey - Possible future digital education initiatives In this set of questions, the survey asked the Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors to reflect on the ways in which future Department of Education and Youth initiatives in the area of digital technology might be organised. This is then followed by a detailed account of the supporting evidence for these findings including quotations from the respondents. This should include support for replacing obsolete equipment and Programme funding resources for repair and upkeep of the technology equipment, as well funding for hosting face to face events or workshops to share what they have learned with their peers. • Respondents suggested continued support in the form of Oide TiE Ongoing training and support for digital skills development, and short-term support for support a few years after the project. • There was strong interest in future programmes, especially in AI, maths, and digital literacy. It was also suggested that it would be Future Initiatives good to propose a mixture of future initiatives which offered either high autonomy for those who wanted it or more structured options for those who felt they needed the additional guidance. www.h2.ie 64 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Application, funding, support, and monitoring processes The Oide TiE Advisors felt that the process worked quite well, and that clusters had adequate funding and were well supported by them. "I feel the (application) form provided schools ample opportunity to outline what they hoped to achieve in the three years. I feel clusters were adequately funded and the model of releasing funding annually worked well. Clusters had to outline their budget in the application form which worked well to monitor how much they needed annually. Having Advisors assigned to support the clusters was a very good model and allowed us to develop a relationship with clusters. The secondment model led to changes to the Advisor assigned to the cluster which negatively impacted upon the support provided. Project management training would have worked in favour of these clusters if it was provided at the beginning of the project. It would have worked well if the lead coordinators had an opportunity to meet throughout the project as a way to support each other through the project." The issue of project management was highlighted by an Advisor and was something that should be revisited and built in from the outset in any future programmes. Furthermore, it was recommended to bring project/Cluster Coordinators together more frequently, either online or face-to-face, to further support each other and to learn from one another. "We were not involved in monitoring."

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf It would have worked well if the lead coordinators had an opportunity to meet throughout the project as a way to support each other through the project." The issue of project management was highlighted by an Advisor and was something that should be revisited and built in from the outset in any future programmes. Furthermore, it was recommended to bring project/Cluster Coordinators together more frequently, either online or face-to-face, to further support each other and to learn from one another. "We were not involved in monitoring." "Monitoring and reporting was difficult in my role as an advisor as it wasn't part of my remit." "As I wasn't involved in monitoring perhaps an element of project management or support in this regard for teachers might have been helpful." "As monitoring was not part of my role, it is difficult to comment on this area." Several Advisors stated that they had no role in the monitoring and reporting activities of the Programme. This is an important finding, as the role of the Advisors was primarily to support the clusters, and they performed well in this role. However, the role of monitoring or providing critical feedback to the projects did not appear to be assigned to any organisation or individual in the Programme. Some Cluster Coordinators and teachers were satisfied with the existing processes. "I have to admit very little change. I believe the scheme ran very well and the open-ended approach of the scheme meant schools had the opportunity to explore how the funding could be allocated." "The current model worked really well. Even though we were geographically challenged, IT afforded us the opportunity to keep connected." However, others pointed to a number of problems and made suggestions for improvement. Process of application and setting up projects Some respondents found the application process overly time-consuming and suggested a less formal application process, while others suggested the need for clearer guidelines and expectations. "Give direct examples and a working plan on how these digital initiatives can be directly implemented into the curriculum, and everyday teaching and learning." www.h2.ie 65 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "A step-by-step plan: 1. What to teach. 2. What to use. 3. How to teach it. For example: To use Book Creator in English. The options/ scope of what schools can do is way too broad, in primary school anyway, and there is so much time wasted trying to figure it out ourselves. I really felt as if I were scrambling in the dark for a long, long time before we got up and running." "Previous application process extremely time consuming!" "I would like to see a less formal approach to applications for funding." "Pick initiatives from a menu. Pick a max number of maybe 3 from 12. Run for an initial 3 years with funding to purchase anything that’s required. Maintenance funding yearly after that. After the first three years pick 3 more initiatives from the list of 12 and repeat the same process. funding based around how many schools; colleges are involved in the cluster. The most expensive part of these clusters is setting them up. They could run very cost effectively once setup." "The tech should be small scale. Even our project was too high end." There are some particularly interesting suggestions here in relation to how future innovative digital projects might be organised. Some schools expressed a preference for less choice and more direction in the future so that cluster plans would be more manageable in the future.
