The most expensive part of these clusters is setting them up. They could run very cost effectively once setup." "The tech should be small scale. Even our project was too high end." There are some particularly interesting suggestions here in relation to how future innovative digital projects might be organised. Some schools expressed a preference for less choice and more direction in the future so that cluster plans would be more manageable in the future. This suggests that the Department might initiate specific calls in the future in areas such as Generative AI or in digital literacy and provide ongoing guidance and support to the successful clusters. Management of funding Some felt that there was a need for more advice on managing funding issues, and some argued that there should have been more attention to contingency planning. Others suggested a phased funding approach and potentially funding to extend some projects into the future. "More advice regarding how to split funding. We were a school of 430 students and as there was no advice re how funds were to be split, it was split evenly with the other schools in our cluster. Both much smaller schools." "Drawing down of funds should be a lot easier and the amount of reporting and accounting should be handled by school management." "Obviously, with COVID, the project went on for an extra year. This of course was unprecedented; however, we budgeted for a three-year programme." "I think that the funding should be released on a phased basis so the school can better assess what technologies are needed." "Funding needs to be available over a much longer time period at least 5 years possibly 10 to fully embed in the initiative in schools." "Making sure that those involved are being reimbursed for expenses they incur." The issue of project administration has featured earlier, and it is clear that funding and budgeting issues presented challenges for some clusters. Cluster Coordinators would have benefited from greater clarity on how to manage this process and this aspect might have been outsourced, in some cases to an education centre. Clusters often struggled with how to budget www.h2.ie 66 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 and manage their funding, as the Coordinator was often a classroom teacher and this aspect may be revisited in any future projects. Project communication and support Whilst Cluster Coordinators and teachers expressed appreciation for the support provided by Oide TiE, concerns were raised in relation to general Programme administration communication activities and the level of support provided to Coordinators to manage their clusters. The following quotations capture these two issues from the perspective of the Cluster Coordinators and teachers. “Luckily, we had a super cluster coordinator who appeared to manage these processes efficiently. Personally, I would have found the role of coordinator very daunting, challenging and time-consuming - it would put me off taking on the role. I feel the process would need to be simplified considerably." "Training for co-ordinators would have been a great support & improved the monitoring piece also." "Very poor communication throughout the project. End of year reports were sent in and never acknowledged. Emails were ignored. But for the support of the Oide TiE Advisors, we would have been lost." "Overall communication from the project leader was poor. We regularly sent in reports which were not acknowledged. Emails were not answered. This was very disheartening." "There was very poor communication from the top down.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf I feel the process would need to be simplified considerably." "Training for co-ordinators would have been a great support & improved the monitoring piece also." "Very poor communication throughout the project. End of year reports were sent in and never acknowledged. Emails were ignored. But for the support of the Oide TiE Advisors, we would have been lost." "Overall communication from the project leader was poor. We regularly sent in reports which were not acknowledged. Emails were not answered. This was very disheartening." "There was very poor communication from the top down. (I do not include the Oide TiE advisors in this, they were fantastic) Reports were sent in and not acknowledged. This was very disheartening especially given the significant amount of time given to the project and the reports." These comments highlight a number of issues in relation to how project reporting and requests for ongoing support might be handled in any future project. It may be worth revisiting the kinds of reports that clusters were asked to submit and determine whether the information requested was essential, while also considering how best to acknowledge such reports and determine if additional information was needed. Secondly, it may be worth exploring how best to respond to cluster email correspondence in any future potential projects. Ultimately, a more simplified reporting approach could work better in the future, and communication with clusters could be outsourced. Monitoring and deliverables Monitoring was recognised as important, and some respondents felt it had generally worked well, but some felt that the definition of deliverables was unclear, and that greater clarity in this area would have helped the monitoring process. "Implement regular monitoring and evaluation of the project with clear objectives and deliverables." "Everything was great except the lack of a clear list of deliverables from the beginning. This would greatly help teachers not to be demotivated every time a new ask is being made. This lack of clarity likely contributed to a lack of whole staff buy-in." www.h2.ie 67 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Monitoring/review completed immediately on completion of the initiative." "No need to have to make a website for the project, this was a waste of time and money when a free twitter page was much more useful." "I think a more localised approach to showcasing projects would be beneficial. Provide an opportunity for schools to meet and view each other's work." These comments suggest that future programmes should clearly outline what deliverables are required from the start, what supports are available and what is expected from projects. In future, other organisations, such as the education centre network, could play a role in this process in hosting regional showcasing events for clusters in a particular region. Ensuring sustained impact In this set of questions, the survey asked the Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE Advisors to reflect on how the Department might ensure that any such future initiatives have a sustained impact beyond the project's funding period. It should be acknowledged that several projects were already able to evidence the sustained impact of their projects. Existing good practice "We have decided to continue with our project. It is important that resources that are initially invested in are sustainable and can be reused. I think that it is a good idea to stagger the funding over a period of time. This gave us the opportunity to see what the resources were like within a classroom setting and allowed us the chance to monitor and evaluate the next phase of our project." "Allow for projects to be extended if there is room to do so.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Existing good practice "We have decided to continue with our project. It is important that resources that are initially invested in are sustainable and can be reused. I think that it is a good idea to stagger the funding over a period of time. This gave us the opportunity to see what the resources were like within a classroom setting and allowed us the chance to monitor and evaluate the next phase of our project." "Allow for projects to be extended if there is room to do so. We loved our project so much we extended it into an ERASMUS project called STEAM teams in order to go deeper and wider." These comments suggest that future programmes should include options for extension and that funding should be released in stages over time, rather than dispersing significant funding at the outset. It is notable to see that some projects found ways to extend their activities through programmes such as Erasmus+ and this option could be included in future programmes. Furthermore, some of the structures that exist within Erasmus+ projects, such as clarity on project deliverables could be considered for inclusion in future programmes. Suggestions for improved sustainability included sustainability planning, communities of practice, continued funding, ongoing support and training, and the need for attention to teachers’ existing workloads. Sustainability planning Some responses focused on the need for clear sustainability planning from the beginning of the project. "Consider a whole department approached around open-source technology to ensure that the initiative is sustainable and can be maintained beyond the project's funding period." This suggestion, though a post-primary example, suggests that where possible clusters should use open-source technologies, so that they can continue to use these technologies after the www.h2.ie 68 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 project has ended. This is a reference to sustainable funding and the cost of digital technologies, an issue raised earlier in the report. Communities of practice and local involvement There were numerous calls to build in opportunities for clusters to meet and share their practices and issues with a range of stakeholders including project teachers, NGOs and local companies, and coordinating teacher meetups. Events such as local competitions, school showcases, and teachmeet-style events were suggested to build local community engagement. "Local Education Centres are ideally suited to encourage sustained impact. School showcases, local competitions and similar activities would be very beneficial.” "Ensuring that a central element of the project is the development of a sustainable community of practice for each cluster that can be kept running after the funding period has ended.” "Give schools power to build local capacity by training and empowering local communities to take ownership of the initiative. (b) Give schools the opportunity to foster partnerships and collaborations: industry, NGOs. This can be done by having a framework already in place at the beginning of the project." "Create digital communities of practice in local ed centres or in local schools whereby schools can meet and work on projects or shared initiatives and help each other to implement digital strategies in their schools. Assisted by the Oide TiE Advisors for support/training." "Establishing strong communities of practice amongst cluster teachers could ensure sustainability by providing a strong foundation for learning amongst school leaders and teachers. Facilitating coordinating teachers to meet up and collaborate face-to-face during the project could also prove beneficial in this regard to support one another." These comments suggest that local education centres could play a role in organising and facilitating future communities of practice associated with digital education projects.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Assisted by the Oide TiE Advisors for support/training." "Establishing strong communities of practice amongst cluster teachers could ensure sustainability by providing a strong foundation for learning amongst school leaders and teachers. Facilitating coordinating teachers to meet up and collaborate face-to-face during the project could also prove beneficial in this regard to support one another." These comments suggest that local education centres could play a role in organising and facilitating future communities of practice associated with digital education projects. These communities of practice could also include the Advisor working with the cluster and other organisations. Furthermore, the creation of such local networks could enable projects to disseminate what worked well and potentially lead to the creation of teacher professional learning courses and possibly new projects in the future. Programme funding Many respondents (52%) emphasised the need for sustained, additional, or maintenance funding after the initial project funding period. This included support for replacing obsolete equipment, funding for hosting events or workshops, as well as resources for repair and upkeep of technology infrastructure. "Access to funding to replenish obsolete equipment in time." "If face-to face workshops are accepted in the future as part of the project - to sustain these, it would be great to receive small additional funding after the programme finishes to allow the continuation of these workshops. Even once a year to cover travel." www.h2.ie 69 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Arrange technology repairs people to visit schools regularly so that they can keep the hardware working from day to day. There is not a single day in this school when some device isn't working. There is no teacher qualified to fix the computers, interactive panels, Wi-Fi, iPads, laptops etc. They then lie idle. This is shocking and awful. Ongoing repairs is essential. It must form part of the reality of schools." "One of the best ways to ensure sustained involvement in a project is that in a few years it would be checked back in on and if it is still running successfully that a further allocation of funds would be available to build upon that work. Incentives continued involvement." Many of the comments address the issue of ongoing equipment maintenance and repair, and this is typically funded via the Digital Strategy ICT infrastructure grant22, which is provided on an annual or biannual basis to schools. In addition, there were calls for funding to enable clusters to share their learnings, via face-to-face learning events, allowing them to share experiences with peers. Such events could take place in an education centre. The final quote raises the question of how to ensure future projects are incentivised to continue innovating and building on their initial findings. Ongoing professional development and support Respondents suggested continued support in the form of Oide TiE support for digital skills development, short-term support for a few years after the project, and ongoing training from established frameworks. "Provide short term support for a few years after." "Continuing Oide TiE support for digital skills development for teachers and creating a suggested equipment list for schools." It seems clusters wished to continue learning and therefore sought to retain the connections they had built with the Oide TiE team. This also raises the issue of identifying projects that had the potential to be further developed over time and the possibility of providing them with extra supports. Consideration of teacher workload and time A few respondents stressed the importance of greater consideration of the existing workload of teachers when planning future initiatives. "Lighten the workload in "figuring it all out".

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "Continuing Oide TiE support for digital skills development for teachers and creating a suggested equipment list for schools." It seems clusters wished to continue learning and therefore sought to retain the connections they had built with the Oide TiE team. This also raises the issue of identifying projects that had the potential to be further developed over time and the possibility of providing them with extra supports. Consideration of teacher workload and time A few respondents stressed the importance of greater consideration of the existing workload of teachers when planning future initiatives. "Lighten the workload in "figuring it all out". Give the teachers a plan/ options of plans per subject give a certain amount of funding for upgrades replacements each year." "A time allocation for the teachers involved." "I believe it comes down to time and funding. As coordinator, the release days with sub cover were essential to making the project a worthwhile experience. Without that it is hard to keep the original initiative going beyond the 3 years." "The release days for the Cluster Coordinator allowed excellent transfer of skills and ideas between schools. A small number of release days on an ongoing basis would improve the sustained impact of the project." 22 https://www.oidetechnologyineducation.ie/technology-infrastructure/ict-grants/ www.h2.ie 70 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Some sub relief for school coordinator to meet annually." There are some particularly interesting suggestions here in relation to how Cluster Coordinators and teachers can be better supported in the future. While time and funding are mentioned, there is also a suggestion to disperse project management duties among a number of individuals or organisations. These suggestions should be considered in any future programme rollout, as issues around teacher time and workload23 have been increasingly highlighted in public discourse. Possible further initiatives All respondents were asked to provide suggestions related to similar future initiatives that they would see as valuable. A wide range of suggestions were made. Some were happy to take part in similar initiatives in the future. "Keep them coming, for me it was one of the best initiatives I have ever been involved with. The personal and professional growth was hugely beneficial. The schools involved are now passionate about enhancing STEM education." "We really enjoyed the project that we undertook, and we would love to revisit this area and expand where possible." Schools clearly recognise the benefits of collaborating with other schools, the Oide TiE team, and other partners in implementing innovative digital and STEM practices. These schools want to be part of such collective experiences where they can explore the potential and challenges associated with embedding new or existing digital technologies into their classroom/school practices. However, some wanted more autonomy. "Allow schools to have greater autonomy, flexibility and freedom when trying out any new initiatives." And some wanted less autonomy. "I think it should be planned by you and delivered to schools en masse. As an example I've been exploring LEGO coding elements and robotics. It's so simple but can be upscaled for higher years so easily. If the Dept/Oide TiE offered a set of technology criteria and set amount of it there wouldn't be such disparity between the breadth of projects. You define the project, then ask schools to sign up. This will allow for more rigid deadlines rather than each project having its own unique set of criteria and goals. Ours were too lofty, happy to admit that now." Future programmes could offer both flexible options for schools with well-developed proposals and more structured, guided projects for schools seeking additional support at the design stage.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf If the Dept/Oide TiE offered a set of technology criteria and set amount of it there wouldn't be such disparity between the breadth of projects. You define the project, then ask schools to sign up. This will allow for more rigid deadlines rather than each project having its own unique set of criteria and goals. Ours were too lofty, happy to admit that now." Future programmes could offer both flexible options for schools with well-developed proposals and more structured, guided projects for schools seeking additional support at the design stage. There appears to be value in both approaches, depending on what the Department wishes to achieve in any future digital innovation projects. Some specific suggestions were provided. 23 https://www.independent.ie/irish-news/education/study-reveals-teachers-stretched-to-the-limit-with-workload- demands-and-lack-of- resources/a1957320861.html#:~:text=Some%2083pc%20of%20teachers%20%2D%20when,hours%20or%20more %20each%20week www.h2.ie 71 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Coding and Online pupil profiling are two excellent digital programmes that could be funded by the dept." "I would like to see something similar around the new Digital Strategy for Schools. A shared vision of where we are going nationally is important to both set clear goals for the future and to ensure a minimum standard is achieved by both students and teachers." "Something creative could be done with the Teaching, Learning & Assessment of MATHS to improve the Classroom Based Assessments & promote Higher Level up-take. As enjoyable as the initiative was it was niche. Something creative in a core subject as important as Maths should aim to encourage students to keep on Maths to HL in LC and to purse course & careers in STEM." "STEM hubs of excellence at regional level where schools could opt in to participate and receive funding to help showcase innovation in their school." While much has been achieved in the current project it is clear that many more areas could be developed using a similar approach. Over time, emerging technologies, such as AI and immersive technologies are likely to become more widespread and new curriculum areas may require further exploration. Approaches such as SEF Digital & STEM could provide spaces for the Department and its support services to work with clusters of schools to explore how these technologies might be implemented in schools within a supported environment. www.h2.ie 72 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Section 8: Programme recommendations In this section a number of recommendations are outlined that could enhance any future digital education action research projects that take place in schools. At the outset it should be noted that this Programme was an extremely positive experience for most of the Cluster Coordinators, teachers and Oide TiE advisors who responded to the interim and final report surveys. They reported that the Programme impacted positively on student learning experiences and that, in most cases, they continue to embed digital technologies into their classroom practices. While this positivity must be balanced against the challenges that were outlined in previous sections (i.e. funding, advice, timely communications and project implementation guidance), the Programme had a long-term impact in many schools. This has taken place despite the significant disruption the Programme experienced due to rolling school closures during the COVID-19 pandemic. The education system should continue to assess how emerging technologies affect administration, teaching, learning, and assessment, so that policy and practice adapt to these developments. Many technologies are not specifically developed for education but have the potential to enhance existing teaching practices and student learning experiences. These technologies require further exploration of how they might be used and their positive and negative impacts evaluated.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf This has taken place despite the significant disruption the Programme experienced due to rolling school closures during the COVID-19 pandemic. The education system should continue to assess how emerging technologies affect administration, teaching, learning, and assessment, so that policy and practice adapt to these developments. Many technologies are not specifically developed for education but have the potential to enhance existing teaching practices and student learning experiences. These technologies require further exploration of how they might be used and their positive and negative impacts evaluated. Dedicated ‘safe spaces’ and structures should be established to allow schools, support services, and other stakeholderscan engage in experimentation to see how these technologies can be used in schools. The European Commission24 has been actively encouraging ministries of education to engage in such experimentation in recent years, as part of their Digital Education Action Plan25, through the Erasmus+ Key Action 2 policy experimentation instrument26. Ministries of education, particularly those involved in the Digital Education Learning Teaching and Assessment Working Group (DELTA WG)27 are keen to learn how schools are using these new technologies and what lessons have been learned. There are always new or emerging technologies that have the potential to impact on the work of schools, and today these include Artificial Intelligence, digital education content, immersive learning environments and much more. Programmes such as SEF Digital & STEM can complement larger EU funded programmes and help inform future policy creation and implementation practices. This is an on-going activity that should be taking place in all countries. The following specific recommendations are based on the evidence presented in the previous sections. 24 https://commission.europa.eu/about/departments-and-executive-agencies/education-youth-sport-and- culture_en 25 https://education.ec.europa.eu/focus-topics/digital-education/action-plan 26 https://erasmus-plus.ec.europa.eu/programme-guide/part-b/key-action-2 27 https://wikis.ec.europa.eu/spaces/EAC/pages/23461992/DELTA www.h2.ie 73 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Establish clear programme goals In the document Description of Scheme – Schools Excellence Fund – Digital, the purpose of the Programme was set out as follows. The purpose of the scheme is to demonstrate the innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment, through clusters of schools collaborating on specific projects. The call was seeking projects that would demonstrate the innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment in the first instance and secondly looking for clusters of schools to come together to achieve this. The focus on innovation had an influence on the application process, in that projects framed their proposals as innovative, when on occasion replication of existing practices might have been a better approach. Many proposals came from teachers with strong experience using technology, supported by both their schools and partner organisations. These applicants were well positioned to innovate. However, some proposals appeared more ambitious than appropriate, given the applicants’ own level of experience with technology and project management, the readiness of their schools, the support available within their institutions, and the strength of their partnerships. This is reflected in the expressed desire from a number of the survey respondents for a much more structured process, with the Department proposing detailed implementation and teaching plans. Some specifically stated that their projects were too ambitious and thus should have been scaled back over the course of the programme. Whilst ‘innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment’ was a reasonable goal for this initiative in 2018, other goals may be more appropriate in the future. An obvious future goal might be: The implementation of a wider uptake of already established use of digital technologies in teaching learning and assessment and taking advantage of advice from teachers who have already implemented the proposed use.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Some specifically stated that their projects were too ambitious and thus should have been scaled back over the course of the programme. Whilst ‘innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment’ was a reasonable goal for this initiative in 2018, other goals may be more appropriate in the future. An obvious future goal might be: The implementation of a wider uptake of already established use of digital technologies in teaching learning and assessment and taking advantage of advice from teachers who have already implemented the proposed use. However, teachers are faced with a rapidly changing technological environment and such straightforward solutions are not always available. It may therefore be appropriate to focus on supporting teachers in becoming more flexible, evolving their technical and pedagogic skills as well as developing new approaches to the curriculum, through initiatives aimed at: The development of teachers’ professional skills through communities of practice in the area of using digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment, with a focus on personal reflection, and taking advantage of the advice of critical friends from partner organisations. Such an approach might call on ideas of teachers as designers and researchers, and the success of such an initiative would be measured in terms of teachers improved skills, rather than the continued use of specific technologies in the classroom. The second element that was mentioned in the call was for schools to come together and form clusters. This aspect seemed to work well where clusters collaborated and worked together towards a common goal. However, there were issues with some clusters that were created after the application process had closed and this is, perhaps, something to avoid in the future. www.h2.ie 74 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Initial organisation of the programme The responses suggested that where projects encountered problems, particularly in the later stages (i.e. Year 3 onwards), these were sometimes due to decisions made at the initial stage of setting up the Programme. For instance, the application form, the types of projects that were sought, the supports that would be provided and the deliverables that were expected from schools. Whilst many respondents were happy with the original application process, there were some who expressed a wish for a more structured approach with explicit guidance on what they were to do. There were also issues with how clusters applied for and managed their finances particularly in the face of unanticipated costs for replacement equipment and repairs. It was suggested that a two-stage application process would have helped clusters avoid these problems. Clusters might have been asked first to develop an initial idea outlining the area they wished to explore, and then those that were selected at that stage could have been provided with support and training to develop more detailed plans that were realistic in terms of time commitment, finance, and the support available in their schools. It is also likely that a three-year project duration (which ultimately was four years because of COVID-19) was too long, and that yearly funding cycles, followed by a review before further funding was released might have helped. For example, in France where they run innovation competitions with industry, they evaluate the projects at the end of Year 1 to see which ones will be funded in Year 228. A similar approach might have worked well in this Programme, particularly for projects that would have benefited from annual funding rather than receiving the bulk of their funding at the outset. Such a staged funding and review process could also have identified any projects that were encountering implementation issues early on.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf For example, in France where they run innovation competitions with industry, they evaluate the projects at the end of Year 1 to see which ones will be funded in Year 228. A similar approach might have worked well in this Programme, particularly for projects that would have benefited from annual funding rather than receiving the bulk of their funding at the outset. Such a staged funding and review process could also have identified any projects that were encountering implementation issues early on. Programme flexibility The COVID-19 pandemic had a notable impact on the project, causing disruption and leading to a shift in priorities. Whilst this could not have been foreseen, the lack of preparedness for disruption highlights the need for flexibility and adaptability in project planning and implementation. Future programmes should include contingency plans for possible school disruptions, such as future pandemics or natural disasters, as well as for potential issues with chosen technologies, including licensing, data privacy, and technical faults. Responsibilities should also be clearly defined from the outset. Some respondents noted the need for greater flexibility in amending project goals and workplans. When dealing with digital technology there is always a possibility that things will not work out as planned and future initiatives should should include contingency plans to adapt goals and timelines when unforeseen technical or organisational challenges arise. Project administrative requirements The Department provided guidance to schools in writing and at the initial launch event in UCD, on what was expected of them and of Oide TiE. The documentation, entitled Managing your 28 Édu-Up system, support for the production of digital resources for schools: https://eduscol.education.fr/1603/le- dispositif-edu www.h2.ie 75 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Project, outlined the role of the coordinator, the role of the participating schools and the reporting requirements for each project. The documentation outlined four types of reports that were expected to be completed at various intervals, as follows: 1. Termly Reports: The production of termly reports was a key deliverable from each project. These reports were expected to detail progress, or challenges encountered, in relation to the milestones and deliverables contained in the project plan required to deliver the project’s overall objectives. 2. Biannual Expenditure Reports: These reports detailed the purpose and level of expenditure, and the procurement procedures followed. Note: All quotes and invoices were to be retained on behalf of the cluster for the duration of the project. 3. Annual Reports: These were intended to be more complete than the Termly Reports and were to indicate progress achieved against the overall project objectives and outcomes over the course of the year. 4. Making project documentation available to the Working Group upon request. Clusters were also provided with templates for reporting and a note relating to financial management. Despite this guidance and direction provided by the Department and Oide TiE to schools at intervals during the programme, several respondents indicated their frustrations with the monitoring and reporting procedures that were in place during the Programme. They sometimes felt they were overly controlled, and they were tired of being asked for reports. This raises issues around the suitability of these reports and the benefits they brought to the overall project management. It also suggests that classroom teachers may not be best placed to carry out this work due to their existing workloads and their lack of experience in project management. While future programmes will require monitoring of expenditure and activity, these should not be the only kinds of reporting that clusters would need to engage in.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf They sometimes felt they were overly controlled, and they were tired of being asked for reports. This raises issues around the suitability of these reports and the benefits they brought to the overall project management. It also suggests that classroom teachers may not be best placed to carry out this work due to their existing workloads and their lack of experience in project management. While future programmes will require monitoring of expenditure and activity, these should not be the only kinds of reporting that clusters would need to engage in. Clusters were also asked to capture and demonstrate project progress, and in the process capture student role and voice. However, this element was largely missing from the submitted reports that were reviewed as part of this evaluation. While clusters were directed to some examples of such reports on Scoilnet and elsewhere29, this is an area that might usefully be further developed in any future project. The evaluation attempted to address this lack of detailed reporting by developing a series of case-studies of three clusters (see Appendix 3) and by capturing Teacher Stories (Appendix 4) and these highlight many innovative practices schools engaged in and the lessons they learned. Cluster Coordinators, despite receiving written and oral guidance, often lacked clarity regarding project management responsibilities and reporting obligations. Many struggled in this regard and any future projects should consider the role of such individuals. These issues might be resolved by providing clearer initial guidance and training for Cluster Coordinators, where their responsibilities are even more clearly defined and supported by guidelines and exemplars for the deliverables required. However, it should be noted that the Department felt they provided such clarity to all Cluster Coordinators. Furthermore, there may be occasions where a Cluster 29 Some reference sites that were shared: https://sites.google.com/a/sbclara.ie/midlands-digital-cluster/ http://westcorkrobotics.edublogs.org/ www.h2.ie 76 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Coordinator could be supported on the administrative tasks by another colleague or an outside organisation (e.g. an education centre), as often these individuals were the creator of the proposal idea and were highly competent in using digital technology, but may have lacked the necessary time and project management skills to successfully meet the procurement and reporting requirements. Sometimes there were issues with how the Department acknowledged and responded to the submission of these reports and other queries in relation to a cluster and its project. The Programme generated additional work for the Department, and they often lacked the personnel and/or time to respond to such requests in a timely manner. Any future projects should carefully consider the additional workload for the Department and if necessary, identify other agencies or organisations that could carry out this work. Funding of digital technologies To ensure effective integration of digital technology in teaching, learning and assessment significant ongoing funding is required. It should not be the role of initiatives such as this Programme to provide all the funding, but rather to provide temporary additional funding to enable new opportunities to be explored and evaluated. However, it appears that some schools perceived the Programme as an opportunity to draw down funding to fund core digital activities in their school or cluster. While the Department does provide separate digital education funding to schools, any future projects should focus on additional digital technologies for specific programme purposes and avoid funding regular school digital activities. This relates back to an earlier point regarding the difference between encouraging innovative uses of digital technology as opposed to more mainstream applications of digital technologies.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf However, it appears that some schools perceived the Programme as an opportunity to draw down funding to fund core digital activities in their school or cluster. While the Department does provide separate digital education funding to schools, any future projects should focus on additional digital technologies for specific programme purposes and avoid funding regular school digital activities. This relates back to an earlier point regarding the difference between encouraging innovative uses of digital technology as opposed to more mainstream applications of digital technologies. The results suggest that even with very willing teachers, the lack of resources, equipment (including replacement and updating), software, internet connectivity, and technical support had negative consequences for some projects. Future programmes might consider breaking funding down into two over-arching categories: • Capital Expenditure • Current Expenditure Furthermore, these might be broken down into categories, such as: • Digital equipment procurement. • Broadband Connectivity (to school and internally) to enable project activities. • Teacher Release Time (Human Resources). • Technical Support to maintain project equipment30. • Outside training/support on specialist technologies. The provision of funding to procure and install digital technologies was most welcome, clusters also needed funding to free up teachers to write reports, to meet and to plan/share their digital activities. Time is always cited as a major barrier in implementing projects in schools and this issue should be addressed more holistically in the future. 30 While this issue applied to project digital infrastructure, it is a much wider system issue that impacted on the project activities of some clusters. www.h2.ie 77 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Projects that required significant capital funding, for example purchases of €20k or over, should be carefully considered and evaluated against the proposed project deliverables. Where possible funding should be released in stages, rather than at the outset of the project, as has been suggested elsewhere. Such an approach reduces the risk of clusters drawing down funding and not completing their project activities. Ongoing support structures Clusters consistently identified a need for ongoing support in the following areas: • Pedagogical support – using technology tools with learners. • Technical support – maintenance of digital equipment. • Monitoring and Evaluation support – administrative reporting and critical friend approach. Where such supports were provided clusters reported satisfaction, but when support was limited, they faced significant challenges. Oide school support (pedagogical) The support from Oide TiE appeared to have worked very well. For some specialist training requirements extra training was procured from outside bodies, and this is reasonable, as Oide TiE cannot be expected to cover all specialist training needs. Where such gaps arise, it might be prudent to consider how the Oide TiE Advisor team can participate and benefit from such specialised training themselves. Technical support and project monitoring were outside the remit of Oide TiE and are addressed in this section. Technical support As highlighted in this report, technical support presented a significant challenge for some clusters. There was no clear mechanism for addressing the issue, and responsibility for it appeared to be undefined. Sometimes teachers took on the responsibility themselves, sometimes external private support was procured. This approach was unsustainable, highlighting the need for clear expectations and defined responsibilities. If this responsibility is to rest with schools, it should be addressed at the outset of the project, with additional funding allocated where necessary to support it. Monitoring and evaluation (administrative support and the role of critical friends) As noted above, Cluster Coordinators were expected to complete a range of reports on their project’s activities. These reports, in the main, monitored project activities and expenditure.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Sometimes teachers took on the responsibility themselves, sometimes external private support was procured. This approach was unsustainable, highlighting the need for clear expectations and defined responsibilities. If this responsibility is to rest with schools, it should be addressed at the outset of the project, with additional funding allocated where necessary to support it. Monitoring and evaluation (administrative support and the role of critical friends) As noted above, Cluster Coordinators were expected to complete a range of reports on their project’s activities. These reports, in the main, monitored project activities and expenditure. There is clearly a need for such reporting, as they may need to be shared with different units or sections within the Department, depending on their purpose and on how they are to be utilised. Future projects should consider what information should be collected, when and by whom and ensure that it is made as easy as possible for schools. Where information is incomplete or non- existent, there should be some processes in place to gather this information in order to take www.h2.ie 78 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 further action. The creation of such monitoring systems creates a workflow for the Department and additional supports should be provided to implement them effectively. In addition to the monitoring element, projects were required to propose evaluation plans in their initial proposals. Feedback on these evaluation proposals could have helped clusters to refine them and enhance their overall project plans. A number of these plans appeared somewhat unrealistic, in demanding too much in the way of data collection, and some projects proposed creating weekly evaluation reports. While self-reporting, as an approach to project evaluation, is a good idea, it appears that many clusters struggled with how best to implement such approaches, and greater guidance should be provided in the future. For instance, in previous digital innovation programmes (i.e. Schools Integration Project - SIP) clusters received training on how to conduct action research on their innovative activities31. The interruption caused by the COVID-19 pandemic led to few project level evaluation plans being implemented or reported. If such evaluation plans are required in future, all projects should complete them, and the Department should provide guidance on what should be contained in such documentation. A couple of initial proposals had included the idea of a ‘critical friend’ as an approach to obtaining critical feedback on their projects. Such an approach could have added value to the Programme, as it would have enabled clusters to engage in critical discussion with an objective third party around their project activities. However, the provision of such a service would have required additional funding, and this was not included in the programme budget. Clusters were advised that funding could not be used to procure the services of a critical friend on this occasion. Oide TiE did not see this as their role and felt it might undermine their relationship with the schools if they were to provide such critical advice. Thus, there was an expectation that projects would self-report on their own activities and decide if they were successful or not. In future it would be worth considering learning models, such as double loop learning32, where organisations modify their goals based on feedback and this could be provided by input from critical friends to the project. The funding of ‘critical friends’ to play a role in future initiatives may be a good way of ensuring some kind of external criticality, without burdening schools with labour intensive data collection for evaluation purposes.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Thus, there was an expectation that projects would self-report on their own activities and decide if they were successful or not. In future it would be worth considering learning models, such as double loop learning32, where organisations modify their goals based on feedback and this could be provided by input from critical friends to the project. The funding of ‘critical friends’ to play a role in future initiatives may be a good way of ensuring some kind of external criticality, without burdening schools with labour intensive data collection for evaluation purposes. There is a need to separate such reporting from the administrative reports that were specified in the early stages of the project. Programme dissemination Some clusters felt that what was being asked of them in terms of dissemination was unclear. They wanted clear expectations to be set out from the beginning, but other clusters asked for the ability to negotiate these, as they felt that some forms of dissemination were better suited to their project and the strengths of their team than others. Some projects had active websites or social media accounts – some detailed descriptions of this are given in the Interim Evaluation Report, however, as reported, the level of engagement with social media accounts was often low. 31 https://doras.dcu.ie/17938/ 32 For more see: https://hbr.org/1977/09/double-loop-learning-in-organizations www.h2.ie 79 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The attempt to collect the Teacher Stories at the end of the project was only partially successful. Part of this is no doubt due to the length of time since the projects finished (projects finished in June 2022 and data was collected in September 2023), but it is perhaps also due to the fact that teachers perceived this as an additional burden, not something they are keen to get involved with. Clusters seem to have been happier in sharing their projects through events such as the SEF Digital & STEM Programme meetings in Athlone in 2019 and Croke Park in 2022. Other possible approaches that might be tried in future initiatives could include someone from outside the school interviewing teachers involved in a project and writing up the story for them (maybe a role for mobile journalism as explored in one the cluster projects). The use of Oide TiE webinars and podcasts is another possible approach. Final word Overall, the Programme was deemed an overwhelmingly positive experience by the majority of those who actively participated in it. The Programme enabled schools to work together, to use digital technologies in a supported way across the curriculum. The majority of teachers who participated in the Programme continue to use these technologies after the end of the Programme and they believe it has had a lasting impact in embedding digital practices in their schools. While digital technology has been used in Irish schools for over fifty years there has been tremendous progress in terms of access to technologies in recent years and schools are on a journey of discovery to identify digital practices that work for them. This work should continue, and the lessons learned from SEF Digital & STEM should influence any future experimentation projects, big or small, to support schools in evolving and meeting the needs of society, today and into the future. www.h2.ie 80 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Appendix 1: List of SEF Digital & STEM clusters This is a listing of the clusters that participated in the Schools Excellence Fund Digital and STEM Programme.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf www.h2.ie 82 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Appendix 2: Department of Education SEF Digital & STEM documentation This section contains the guidance provided to clusters on managing their projects and the confirmation agreement they signed committing to adhere to the terms of the programme governing project management, and expenditure, and to report to the Department per the agreed schedule and as required. Managing your project The purpose of this document This document is intended to provide guidance and support to project co-ordinators in implementing their Digital Clustering Project within their cluster. The projects operate as part of The School Excellence Fund-Digital and are designed to facilitate and support innovation in the use of digital technologies in teaching, learning and assessment in primary and post-primary schools. Effective project co-ordination at local level is essential in ensuring the successful implementation of the various projects. The projects operate under the aegis of a Working Group established by the Department of Education and Skills (DES) and their implementation in schools is supported by the Professional Development Service for Teachers (PDST) and through the provision of funding, training and substitution cover to facilitate project meetings and attendance at training and other project-related events. Consideration should be given to establishing a steering group to support project management at the local level and, therefore, to ensure: • The development of a project implementation plan with clear schedules and deliverables. • Effective communication with all of the project partners and support services • Delegation of project roles and responsibilities to the participating schools • Optimum use of project resources, including compliance with purchasing procedures and guidelines. • Maintenance of records of project activities, achievements and challenges for the purpose of informing project reports. • Open and effective collaboration with the external evaluation team. The role of the Coordinator The co-ordinator plays a key role in enabling the successful implementation of the project and takes responsibility for its day-to-day operation, on behalf of the steering committee, if one is established, the co-ordinator takes responsibility for: • Managing the development and maintenance of the project schedule, by; − Adopting overall responsibility for ensuring adherence to the agreed schedule − Assigning tasks to collaborating teachers − Ensuring evaluation elements are integral to the project reporting • Maintaining clear lines of communication with the PDST, Working Group, external evaluator, and the participating schools in the cluster. • Identifying and communicating to the PDST the project’s support and CPD requirements • Developing and submitting agreed reports on project implementation in line with the requirements of the Working Group • Facilitating communication and collective decision-making within the cluster. • Organising meetings and events within the cluster. • Planning for project requirements, including training and infrastructure. www.h2.ie 83 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 • Overseeing outlay of project funds, maintaining records of same, and providing expenditure reports to the Department as required. • Ensuring compliance with the terms of the programme. • Identifying challenges to project implementation and escalating issues to the PDST if required. The role of participating schools It is envisaged that the clusters will operate in a spirit of partnership and collaboration with each participating school taking responsibility for agreed elements of the project’s implementation. Details of each school’s particular role should be agreed with the project steering group. In addition, each participating school will: • Contribute to the development of the project, including the project schedule, budget, communications plan. • Take responsibility for specific elements of the project implementation and provide reports on their activities to the project coordinator.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf The role of participating schools It is envisaged that the clusters will operate in a spirit of partnership and collaboration with each participating school taking responsibility for agreed elements of the project’s implementation. Details of each school’s particular role should be agreed with the project steering group. In addition, each participating school will: • Contribute to the development of the project, including the project schedule, budget, communications plan. • Take responsibility for specific elements of the project implementation and provide reports on their activities to the project coordinator. • Identify challenges to project implementation and escalate those requiring particular attention to the project coordinator. • Adhere to the expenditure and governance requirements of their project. • Maintain ongoing communications with the other schools in the cluster. • Participate in the CPD and training provided to support project implementation and evaluation. • Engage with external evaluator as required. Reporting requirements Each project will be required to maintain all project documentation and submit a number of reports during the project’s lifetime, these include: 1. Termly Reports: The production of termly reports is a key deliverable from each project. These reports should detail progress, or challenges encountered, in relation to the milestones and deliverables contained in the project plan required to deliver the project’s overall objectives. A reporting template is attached in the appendices. 2. Biannual Expenditure Reports: These reports will detail the purpose and level of expenditure, and the procurement procedures followed. Note: All quotes and invoices should be retained on behalf of the cluster for the duration of the project. An information note relating to financial management is attached in the appendices. 3. Annual Reports: These are intended to be more complete than the Termly Reports and will indicate progress achieved against the overall project objectives and outcomes over the course of the year. 4. The production of project documentation to the Working Group upon request. Note: Reports issuing from the clusters will be submitted to the PDST for review. A summary report will subsequently be compiled by the Working Group and submitted to the ICT Policy Unit of the Department for sign-off. The Role of the PDST The PDST will play a central role in facilitating project implementation and in supporting schools in meeting targets and overcoming challenges. Each cluster will have an identified PDST advisor who will be identified as the point of contact between the service and the cluster. It is envisaged that the clusters, through the project co-ordinator, will maintain ongoing communication with their PDST advisor throughout the project’s lifetime. The PDST, in turn, will maintain communication with the Working Group www.h2.ie 84 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 and the ICT Policy Unit of the DES and the external evaluator. In supporting project implementation, reporting and evaluation, the PDST will • Engage with clusters to identify their training or professional development needs and facilitate or directly provide the requisite training. • Provide ongoing advice in relation to project management advice and support on an ongoing and reporting. • Review project reports. • Liaise between clusters and working group regarding any challenges or issues that may arise and suggest alterations to project implementation plans to resolve any such issues. • Escalate any issues, where appropriate, to the Working Group and ICT Policy Unit for resolution. • Supported by the ICT Policy Unit of the DES, maintain overall records for the programme. • Engage with the external evaluator as required. • Produce information and reporting on the programme to the Working Group and the ICT Policy Unit of the DES as required.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf These factors are discussed under the following headings: • Students • Teachers • School leaders • Shared vision • Wider partnerships These factors are derived from the key findings of the EU report Supporting School Innovation Across Europe. Final Report to DG Education and Culture of the European Commission34 (PPMI 2018). The detailed description of these factors given in that report is reproduced below: “Our analysis of school innovation success stories reveals many entry points to the process of innovation in schools. Certain conditions must, however, be met to ensure that change is managed effectively and leads to positive outcomes: Students are at the centre of learning, and their engagement is crucial to ensure the relevance of school innovation. They can be active actors in the learning process, e.g., through self- and peer- assessment, as well as in school governance and partnerships with external stakeholders. Teachers’ preparedness, commitment and capacity to manage change are key factors in ensuring that innovations are implemented effectively. Professional development and collaboration initiatives, such as professional learning communities, teaching teams, action research projects, teamwork and formative feedback mechanisms, can help the implementation process, collectively focus on achieving success in teaching and learning, and developing innovative mindsets among teachers. School leaders are a dynamic and influential force in the school community. They can guide schools in creating a culture that initiates and supports innovation. Good leadership can ensure a supportive environment for teachers; promote collaborative practices such as peer-learning, mentoring, formative feedback, reflective and inquiry- based practices; and provide relevant opportunities for networking and professional development. Distributed leadership constitutes one of the conditions for sustainable 34 https://op.europa.eu/en/publication-detail/-/publication/2e440297-4ce9-11e8-be1d-01aa75ed71a1/language-en www.h2.ie 86 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 innovation by encouraging teachers to assume increased responsibilities, to engage in individual or collective initiatives, and to build their capacity to take on leadership roles. A shared vision and clear action plans help to structure the implementation process, ensuring the sustainability of school innovations. This begins by identifying the school’s needs, then by agreeing on the way to move forward, and extends to providing feedback and reflection throughout the process. This also helps to instil a sense of commitment and stability among school staff, students, parents and local stakeholders. Innovative schools promote an active role for students, teachers and school leaders, parents and other stakeholders, helping to build a system of horizontal accountability. Creating wider partnerships is an intrinsic feature of innovative schools. Engaging with parents can be a necessary step in overcoming their potential resistance to changes. Schools also need to draw on the various resources and opportunities provided by other schools, as well as municipalities, teacher education providers, local businesses, civil society organisations, and other local stakeholders. Recognising and supporting the horizontal connectedness of schools with their communities is a crucial step towards overcoming school isolation, facilitating different forms of learning, and bringing learning closer to real-life contexts.” www.h2.ie 87 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Case Study 1 A Cross Sectoral collaboration to promote student interest and engagement in STEM subjects Lead School: C.B.S. Thurles (65450W) School context The project involves eight schools (one post primary and seven primary) most in Tipperary, with one in Waterford. The lead school is C.B.S. Thurles (65450W). Two of the primary schools have very small numbers of pupils (37 and 57). Sixteen teachers are involved in the project and 400 pupils (from a total of 875 pupils in the primary schools). All the schools have a Catholic ethos. In one of the primary schools all subjects are taught through Irish, and in all the other schools no subjects are taught through Irish.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Thurles (65450W) School context The project involves eight schools (one post primary and seven primary) most in Tipperary, with one in Waterford. The lead school is C.B.S. Thurles (65450W). Two of the primary schools have very small numbers of pupils (37 and 57). Sixteen teachers are involved in the project and 400 pupils (from a total of 875 pupils in the primary schools). All the schools have a Catholic ethos. In one of the primary schools all subjects are taught through Irish, and in all the other schools no subjects are taught through Irish. One of the primary schools is classified as DEIS. External involvement • Education Centre Network: Kilkenny Education Centre - assistance with training (IP); Thurles Education Centre – meetings venue and collaborative space to contact other Education Centres. (3YP) • Third Level: Limerick Institute of Technology (Thurles campus) - provisions of a collaborative learning space, IT support for creating a ‘remote laboratory’, support for cluster presentations at ICT in Education Conference. (IP, 3YP) • Industry: − Lennox Educational: advice on initial sensor purchases as well as training requirements and ongoing maintenance of hardware, queries or technical issues with the sensors. − Elite Drone Services: Launch event support. (Y1_Mid_PR) − Wriggle: Apple training for staff and students as part of the overall package. (IP, Y1_Mid_PR) • Evaluation partners: None identified. Proposed project The project and its aims The Cluster website says that the project aims to increase interest and engagement in STEM subjects among students, by providing opportunities for: • Primary pupils to develop their problem-solving skills by participating in inquiry-based learning activities. • Transition Year (TY) science and mathematics students to be involved in creating tutorial videos and facilitating workshops for primary-school teachers and pupils. • Greater levels of cross-sectoral collaboration between teachers. • Embedding digital technologies in the curriculum in an innovative manner. www.h2.ie 88 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The website says the project aligns with the Digital Learning Framework, focussing on Domain 2: Learner Experiences Standard 2.4 and Domain 3: Teachers' Individual Practice Standard 3.1. The IP indicates an important role for competitions: Our Project will provide opportunities for each school to partake in STEM focused competitions during the 3 years. This will embed interest and encourage students to engage purposefully and meaningful with STEM learning activities both inside and outside the classroom. Grant allocation Y1_Mid_ER indicates a grant of €24,239.00. The IP indicated that three iPads would be allocated to each primary school and 20 to the post-primary school. Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf bits develop leadership skills. • Schools’ engaging with • Increased uptake of STEM Coding element (online subjects in senior cycle for collaboration) pupils in the project (IP) Cluster collaboration IP describes a variety of forms of collaboration including email, Twitter, Blogs, monthly meetings, and workshops. Dissemination One of the proposed outcomes is a “resource bank available through an online space”. The Cluster will participate in the Final Showcase Event and intended to participate in an ICT in Education Conference at LIT Thurles in May 2020. (IP) Evaluation The Initial Proposal (IP) presents quite ambitious plans in relation to evaluation, stating: www.h2.ie 89 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 • Our project actions will be monitored on an ongoing basis to evaluate their impact on learning, teaching and assessment practices. • The final evaluation process will involve the participating schools providing feedback on areas such as student involvement, student classroom participation, teacher confidence levels in STEM subjects etc. • We would also hope to make some evaluation of the success of this project in terms of primary students’ attainment levels in STEM related subjects through the early years of secondary school. Project progress Most innovative elements of the project The Cluster Coordinator identified the following as innovative aspects of the project: "The cross sectoral collaboration between the secondary lead school and the primary cluster schools. This collaboration has huge potential in terms of streamlining the transition for students between primary and secondary school in all subjects, not just science." "Many of the primary teachers involved in our project would admit to a certain level of unease in using the wireless technology with their students as well as a lack of in-depth knowledge of the background scientific explanations. Having a second level Physics teacher available to call to the primary schools to give a lesson or to simply explain the background theory to the teacher has helped to ease any sense of ‘lack of knowledge’ and has helped to improve teacher engagement." "The use of wireless sensor technology at primary school level is in itself an innovative approach and has definitely helped to increase student engagement in the lessons." (CCS2020) Example of a learning activity "Students used the knowledge of Lego WeDo to design, build and code a racing car, taking into account factors to improve the speed of their designs. They then used the Pasco wireless motion sensor to monitor the speed of their cars. The students then had the chance to alter their designs before finally using the motion sensor again to find the speed of their designs and to race one another. This activity involved a lot of elements, including research, design, programming, measurement, analysis, reflection and interpretation and was a great example of how the teacher used the topic of motion and speed to link two separate initiatives into one lesson." (CCS2020) Reported progress The End of Year Report (Y1_Final_PR) is positive about the achievements of the project, and highlights some specific achievements: "We are happy that year one has been a success on many fronts, most importantly in the fact that the primary school children have shown great enthusiasm and engagement with the project to date." "Very successful launch event held in October 2018 with over 400 local primary school children attending. The event was covered in local media as well as on social media." www.h2.ie 90 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Schools are actively engaged with the project and are currently working on an agreed timetable of rotation of sensors between each school."

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf "Very successful launch event held in October 2018 with over 400 local primary school children attending. The event was covered in local media as well as on social media." www.h2.ie 90 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 "Schools are actively engaged with the project and are currently working on an agreed timetable of rotation of sensors between each school." "Weekly visits by Cluster Coordinator to primary schools has been successful as a means of providing assistance/expertise to primary teachers." "Twitter account set up by Oide TiE Advisor to promote the project and the great work being done by teachers within primary classrooms." "The Cluster presented at the SEF event in Athlone IT on May 20th, 2019." The Cluster Coordinator sums up progress as follows: "Each of the seven primary schools involved in the cluster is following an agreed timetable to use one of our wireless sensors (motion, pH, voltage, current, weather station, pressure, temperature, conductivity) with their students on a monthly basis. The primary teachers conduct, record and document any practical activities undertaken and the work is recorded currently on the virtual learning platform ‘Edmodo’. Sample videos are to be recorded by the lead school as a means of empowering primary teachers and to provide assistance in terms of sensor use. The goal this year was to try to incorporate the use of the wireless sensors in a more meaningful way … Currently, the primary pupils involved are from 4th, 5th and/or 6th class. Our focus cohort are 5th class as these students will be exposed to the project for the full three-year duration." (CCS2020) "However, time is an issue: As with any school-based endeavour, time is the big issue for everybody. Time to plan the activities, time to collect the sensors, time to record and document results and findings, time to visit schools, time, time, time." (CCS2020) It is clear from both Progress Reports that many action points had not been achieved and that original timeframes had been too optimistic: "The main issue that we have. It has proved difficult to find times that all members are available for, it has also proven quite difficult to have a consistent access to our TY students due to the many demands placed on them during transition year." (Y1_Final_PR) External involvement: This took place as planned. Cluster collaboration This proceeded much as planned: Cluster coordinator has visited each school to offer assistance/expertise to the primary school teachers, on a weekly basis. Communications are ongoing through ‘posts’ on Edmodo classroom and WhatsApp messenger. Cluster coordinator will maintain contact with cluster schools with weekly visits. (Y1_Mid_PR) Dissemination: The Cluster held a launch event and participated in the SEF Digital & STEM event in Athlone in May 2019. (Y1_Final_PR). Details of online dissemination are given in Section 4.4 below. Evaluation: The evaluation activities are not yet well developed: Measure of progress to date has been observation of student engagement during school visits by Cluster Coordinator.
