Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Evaluation: The evaluation activities are not yet well developed: Measure of progress to date has been observation of student engagement during school visits by Cluster Coordinator. (Y1_Mid_PR) Budget: Almost all the funds had been spent in the first term - see Table 12. (Y1_Mid_ER) www.h2.ie 91 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Table 12: Budget expenditure for Cluster 20. Item Cost Sensors €4,090.44 iPads €17,936.98 Launch meeting €1,475.48 TOTAL EXPENDITURE €23,502.90 GRANT €24,239.00 Use of public online platforms The project has maintained a website (https://tippstem.weebly.com/) and a Twitter account (@TippSTEM) since the start of the project. The project website contains a Home page which provides an outline of the project and its aims; a Cluster Launch Day page which provides a description of launch day with photographs, and related tweets; pages for each of the eight schools – one of these (Gaelscoil Durlas) describes activities with the pH sensor, motion sensor and voltage sensor, accompanied by photographs and video (the later were produced by the Lead School). The posts present an outline of the activities and showcase what was done, but provide little detailed pedagogic or technical description, analysis of results, or critical reflection. Figure 9: Screenshot from project website for Cluster 20. As of 2 May 2020, the twitter account had 78 followers, and had published 81 tweets, the first on 2 Oct 2018, and the latest on 19 March 2020. The plot of the number of tweets per day, shows two clumps of tweets, the first around the start of the project and the launch day, and the second around a joint training day. The total engagement score (i.e., number of favourites and retweets) over the period was 220 – which is quite low. The highest engagement for a single tweet was 21 for a tweet in October 2019 announcing the launch of the Cluster website. www.h2.ie 92 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 10 8 6 4 2 0 12/08/2018 10/12/2018 09/04/2019 07/08/2019 05/12/2019 03/04/2020 Figure 10: Number of tweets per day by date for Cluster 20. Observations Highlights The DES and Oide TiE support in terms of finance for equipment and training, in the support for the Cluster Launch Day, and the ongoing support provided by the Oide TiE Advisor were clearly very valuable. The Cluster Launch Day was a major and very successful event, involving the schools (including a significant role for the Transition Year students from the post-primary school), Oide TiE, and several companies, and it made a significant and important start in addressing the aims of the project. As reported in Section 4.1, the Cluster Coordinator identified the following innovative aspects of the project: cross sectoral collaboration; support by a second level Physics teacher; use of wireless sensor technology at primary school level. He sums up his view of the project in this way: "I am happy however that the pupils involved in the project across the seven primary schools in our cluster project, are enjoying the experience and opportunity to utilise the wireless sensors in class and to design practical activities that mean something to them. Once the primary pupils are engaged in scientific enquiry, prediction and analysis, I believe that the project is a success." (CCS2020) Issues The Initial Proposal was too ambitious, and the proposed evaluation unrealistic, including an examination of impact on primary to post-primary transition, whilst not planning for an evaluation partner. With hindsight, it might have been helpful if more detailed feedback had been given to the Cluster about these aspects of the proposal before commencement.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Once the primary pupils are engaged in scientific enquiry, prediction and analysis, I believe that the project is a success." (CCS2020) Issues The Initial Proposal was too ambitious, and the proposed evaluation unrealistic, including an examination of impact on primary to post-primary transition, whilst not planning for an evaluation partner. With hindsight, it might have been helpful if more detailed feedback had been given to the Cluster about these aspects of the proposal before commencement. Despite its genuine achievements the project did not do all it had hoped, made little progress on evaluation, and found that the demand on teachers’ time was greater than expected. The evidence we have suggests that the project may have worked better in some primary schools than others. The educational gains for the post-primary partner were unclear. The Transition Year Science and Mathematics students played a useful role in the Cluster Launch Day, but further involvement seems to have met practical problems which should have been foreseen. The allocation of 20 iPads to the post-primary partner in the proposal does not therefore seem to be justified. www.h2.ie 93 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The data we have does not provide a detailed account of the collaboration that would enable replication of the practice, and it is hoped that this account will be developed later in the project. The comments with reference to Digital Learning Framework on the project website might suggest a ‘checklist’ approach, rather than a use of the DLF to examine, critique and develop current practice. Whilst the Cluster Launch Day and the Athlone event were significant dissemination activities; the website has little content and there is little engagement with the Twitter account. Lessons learned The economic value of working in a cluster in order to share access to quite expensive technology that is only used occasionally (the sensors) seems to be clear. An issue raised by this Cluster project is the potential value of cross sectoral support for professional development. Is this useful, or would professional development based in the primary schools (perhaps supported by external agencies) be more effective? What are pros and cons, and how might it be designed to achieve maximum impact? Will this collaboration have the added value of impacting on the primary/post-primary transition for these pupils? At present we are not capturing enough information to be able to answer this question, and existing internal evaluation is weak. Key factors for enabling innovative practices • Students - Some evidence of student as active actors in the learning process, but not in school governance or external partnerships. • Teachers – Professional development and collaboration is the chief focus of the project, though issues have arisen around capacity. No evidence about formative feedback by the teachers. • School leaders – No evidence presented of the role of school leaders. • Shared vision – The relationship of the project to schools’ shared vision and action plans is not presented. It is not clear how much of the vision of the project is generated by the Lead School rather than arising from an analysis of needs by the primary school partners. This may have implications for sustainability. • Wider partnerships – There was a strong involvement by some businesses in the initial stages, but it is unclear how this will continue (beyond training which forms part of the hardware acquisition package). Collaboration with other organisations seems not to have worked as planned, and there are no plans for wider community involvement (including parents).

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf It is not clear how much of the vision of the project is generated by the Lead School rather than arising from an analysis of needs by the primary school partners. This may have implications for sustainability. • Wider partnerships – There was a strong involvement by some businesses in the initial stages, but it is unclear how this will continue (beyond training which forms part of the hardware acquisition package). Collaboration with other organisations seems not to have worked as planned, and there are no plans for wider community involvement (including parents). www.h2.ie 94 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Case Study 2 Use of embedded digital video strategies to enhance teaching and learning in post-primary schools Lead School: Banagher College (76105Q) School context The project involves four post-primary schools, two in Offaly, one in Westmeath and one in Roscommon. The lead school is Banagher College, a LOETB school. The other schools are secondary Catholic schools. None of the schools teach any subjects through Irish. None are classified as DEIS. The project involves eight teachers and 200 pupils. External involvement • Education Centre Network: Athlone Education Centre - Initial cluster meeting and training sessions. (IP) • Third Level: Limerick Institute of Technology (L.I.T.) - guidance for technology and training, input from ‘The Youth Media Team’. (3YP) • Industry: RTE/RTE Digital (MoJo Training); Titanium Media (MoJo Training); Frontline Films (MoJo Training/Storyboarding); TV3 (Journalism); AIT (Digital Marketing). (IP) • Evaluation partners: None identified. Proposed project The project and its aims The project aims to use industry leading training in MoJo (mobile journalism) video content creation to enhance teaching, learning and digital literacy among educators and students in the cluster schools, while also being central to AFL (assessment for learning) and blended learning approaches in the respective schools. (IP) This would seem to break down into two different, if related, strands. Firstly, access to high level training in mobile journalism leading to the creation of numerous video artefacts, and then in later years of the project this leads on to more specialist training for the creation of more complex artefacts using a wider range of skills, techniques and equipment. Then, secondly, a wider use of these skills in video production in the wider curriculum: "It provides numerous opportunities for student collaboration. Short videos can be used to enhance the teaching experience, to develop learning concepts, to allow for student immersion, to develop problem-solving skills, to enhance computational skills, to develop social skills, to build and develop teamwork, encourage social interaction through different age groups, to “flip” learning, to provide new channels for both formative and summative assessment and much more." (IP) The IP states that project ties in with the STEM Action Plan 2017 – 2019 under the Implementation phase in providing support for schools and teachers to work together on innovative and collaborative initiatives in STEM, scoping out the partnership with STEM business and industry, and supporting effective and engaging STEM teaching. www.h2.ie 95 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The IP states that the project aligns itself with Statements of Highly Effective Practice in the Digital Learning Framework (DLF) under all four of the Teaching and Learning domains and three out of four of the Leadership and Management domains (omitting 2 – Managing the Organisation). This includes €1,800 on teacher meetings and €8,700 on training, €7,830 on 20 iPads and another €4,695 on 8 iPhones, the rest of the money is for other equipment (mostly specifically relevant to mobile journalism).

Use of public online platforms The project main website is at https://edmojoireland.wordpress.com/ and the project also uses the main social media channels of the different schools in the cluster (CCS2020). The main website was set up on 9 April 2020, and as of 2 June had four posts which are a series of short introductions to the project. We identified some related websites: the TY Edmojo Project page (https://www.bccns.ie/ty-edmojo-project/) which contains a link to the Banagher College Mojo YouTube channel (https://www.youtube.com/channel/UChHGvznqRGPPuEynHQYHLWQ/) with 36 videos added since the start of the project in October 2018 (some with several hundred www.h2.ie 98 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 views) and another 16 older videos, and the Edmojo related YouTube channel for Coláiste Mhuire (https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCnQqycsxF82aSEeMJLCMSEg) with five videos. The Twitter account @EdMojo1 “Cluster of four schools in the Midlands embarking on a Mobile Journalism journey!” had 52 followers as of 2nd May 2020 and published 24 tweets between 29 May 2018 and 19 Jan 2019. The English Department at Banagher College Twitter account (@BCCNSEnglish) had 59 MoJo related tweets between 28 Nov 2015 and 19 March 2020. Other school accounts (@CbsMullingar, @ULTIMojoCBS; @colaiste_choilm; @CChiarain) had small numbers of MoJo related tweets, and the schools’ Instagram and Facebook accounts had a small number of videos related to this project. Figure 12: Screenshot from YouTube channel for TY Edmojo Project. Observations Highlights The opportunity for students to engage with mobile journalism as they might in the wider media world, using professional equipment, benefitting from professional training and having their work published is clearly exciting and potentially a very valuable experience. The link with industry partners for training seems to have gone very well and been a highlight of the project. Banagher College has produced a large number of videos as a result and made them available on their YouTube channel. Banagher College has been involved with mobile journalism for several years, and this project enabled them to obtain additional specialist equipment, increase their own expertise through high level professional training and extend the work to a wider cluster of schools. Issues The project aim of obtaining access to high level training in mobile journalism leading to the creation of numerous video artefacts, seems to have been largely achieved. However, the project claims to have also achieved its aim of embedding these skills in video production in the wider curriculum, though this is not evidenced in the published materials. Most of the published videos are of general interest (news, school events, sports events) and journalistic in tone, www.h2.ie 99 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 seemingly somewhat cut off from the mainstream curriculum. This disjunction is illustrated by the comment that: "The students need ongoing input at all stages of production, the vast majority of which has to take place outside of regular class time."

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf However, the project claims to have also achieved its aim of embedding these skills in video production in the wider curriculum, though this is not evidenced in the published materials. Most of the published videos are of general interest (news, school events, sports events) and journalistic in tone, www.h2.ie 99 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 seemingly somewhat cut off from the mainstream curriculum. This disjunction is illustrated by the comment that: "The students need ongoing input at all stages of production, the vast majority of which has to take place outside of regular class time." (CCS2020) Whilst the Banagher College YouTube channel hosts a significant number of videos, we did not find evidence that the other schools met the targets the project had set. The engagement with social media in general (particularly Twitter) was much less than anticipated in the proposal. The present outputs fulfil the goal of demonstrating the students’ skills in creating materials, but it is important that the project finds some way of capturing the process as well as the output, in order to enable other schools to learn from the project. The existing project video of a training session simply provides a view of some classroom activities accompanied by a music, with no overview of what was happening or explanations. Lessons learned An important question for schools potentially interested in this approach, is how useful is mobile journalism in education outside the field of journalism as part of language and literacy studies? So far, this project does not seem to have demonstrated its relevance to other parts of the curriculum, and other digital storytelling approaches based on simpler technologies and production methods may well be more successful in that respect. The coordinator’s strong comments on excessive workload are an important consideration for the SEF Digital & STEM Programme. This raises the question of whether to invest in additional time to pursue ambitious goals or to set goals that are more realistic and manageable within existing time constraints. Key factors for enabling innovative practices • Students – Some evidence of student as active actors in the learning process (though difficult to be sure from existing evidence), though not in school governance or external partnerships. • Teachers – The teachers involved with the project are very committed to it, but there is a concern on whether they have the time capacity. It may be that it was because the project was not fully integrated with the curriculum that so much work was done outside class time. • School leaders – The school leaders may be pleased with the outputs of the mobile journalism in terms of publicity, but it is it is unclear how much support they provided. • Shared vision – It is unclear to what extent this project arises from a shared vision of the school’s needs, rather than reflecting the (important) concerns of one curriculum area. • Wider partnerships – The link with industrial partners through the paid training is clear, but it is not clear whether any sort of connection continued after this. Whilst parents and other stakeholders will be interested in the news outputs (and they do get a lot of views), it is unclear to what extent this manages to engage them with the innovation in the schools. www.h2.ie 100 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Case Study 3 Machines and Mechanisms Lead School: Firies N.S (15945T) School context The project involves four primary schools- all in Kerry. The lead school is Firies N.S. (15945T).

Proposed project The project and its aims The Initial Proposal sets out the project aims as: Students: • Actively engage in STEM learning – both in school and out of school (home, Liebherr, fire station (Killarney), Kerry airport, train station Killarney and/or Farranfore). • Students have an increased awareness and appreciation for the importance, value and operation of STEM. • Use STEM to open up new forms of learning and collaboration. • Children will be engineers, designers and artists. • Use ICT to support different styles of learning and children with special educational needs. • Develop 21st century skills such as problem solving, inquiry-based learning and teamwork. Observations Highlights The project has had significant impact on the teachers in developing leadership capacity and so strengthening middle management, involving teachers in developing CPD for their colleagues, and in developing collaborations within and between schools and with parents, and has established strong links with Third Level partners. The project seems to have been very effective in terms of staff development, the concept of ‘community’ plays a significant role in the accounts the Cluster provides about its work, and the collaboration between staff, external www.h2.ie 105 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 partners, Oide TiE and use of the website and social media provides a good example of connectivism learning. This in turn leads to a promise of sustainability: "We plan to continue our learning cluster into the future (after the initial three years) and work on other projects together." (CCS2020) The strong online presence also seems to reflect an active, well organised and collaborative cluster. The project website provides a positive image of the children’s activities with a wide range of digital technologies and STEM and reflects their enjoyment of what they were doing. Issues The IP was probably over optimistic with respect to what could be done, and underestimated problems over sharing equipment. Feedback from the Department at the proposal stage might have helped the Cluster plan more effectively. The reports make significant claims for impact which will need to be further evidenced later. The IP promises several things in relation to monitoring which have not yet been reported on. Whilst the Y1_Final_PR says that the schools “worked on three step process to become Digital School of Distinction”, there is no mention of the DLF in any of the project documents. Planning for wider dissemination, reporting on process as well as outcomes, capturing lessons learned by the project and providing help to other schools that might wish to do something is absent so far. Lessons learned The contribution to the development of leadership capacity, and the creation of a collaborative working relationship both across the schools and externally are the most striking aspects of this project. The word ‘community’ comes up repeatedly in the documents from this Cluster. It would be useful to make comparisons between the effectiveness of this community-oriented approach to the somewhat more top-down approaches adopted by some other Clusters. It is good that taking part in this project meant that the teachers felt valued, but this raises issues about possible negative impact on schools not selected for participation in such initiatives. Key factors for enabling innovative practices • Students – The students are active actors in the learning process and are also providing input into the planning for the initiative. • Teachers – The teachers are working collaboratively on the development of teaching approaches and providing formative feedback. • School leaders – School leaders are actively involved in the project, providing support for a school wide change. • Shared vision – The project is based on a shared vision and is part of a wider action plan for change. • Wider partnerships – The project has involved parents, Third Level institutions and industry in collaborative work in developing the project.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf • Teachers – The teachers are working collaboratively on the development of teaching approaches and providing formative feedback. • School leaders – School leaders are actively involved in the project, providing support for a school wide change. • Shared vision – The project is based on a shared vision and is part of a wider action plan for change. • Wider partnerships – The project has involved parents, Third Level institutions and industry in collaborative work in developing the project. www.h2.ie 106 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Appendix 4: SEF Digital & STEM teacher stories: Overview This section contains 12 teacher stories with each following a similar layout, listing the schools involved, an overview of the project, the project aims, how it was implemented, some reflections on how it evolved and links to additional resources. The stories cover a range of topics that include: • Computational Thinking x 2 • Cross curricular use of digital technologies in primary • Constructivist use of digital technologies • Virtual Reality technology in primary school • STEM x 2 • Creative use of digital [post-primary but no links to curriculum] • Digital Literacy • PE at primary • Robotics The majority of stories capture practice from predominantly primary school clusters and here the teachers typically adopted a cross curricular approach to the use of digital technologies. 1. Collaborative Approach to Computational & Design Thinking to Improve Primary School Problem-Solving Skills Schools Involved: • Talbot Senior National School, Clondalkin, Dublin • St. Anne's National School, Ardclough, Straffan, Co. Kildare Project Overview This project aimed to develop problem-solving skills among primary school students through the integration of computational and design thinking. It involved collaborative work within and between two schools, Talbot Senior National School and St. Anne's National School. Using Lego WeDo, Chromebooks, and iPads, students engaged in hands-on, technology-driven activities that promoted critical thinking and teamwork. The project also fostered self-reflection and peer assessment, encouraging students to evaluate their own learning processes and improve their problem-solving capabilities. Project Aims • Improve problem-solving skills among students • Use technology to design and program robots • Promote collaboration within and between schools • Encourage self-reflection and peer assessment www.h2.ie 107 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Implementation The project was focused on 4th class students in both schools and spanned three years. The primary technological tool was Lego WeDo, which allowed students to create and program robots to solve various tasks. Each school was equipped with 30 Chromebooks, and the teachers received training on using Lego WeDo for classroom workshops. The first phase involved gathering data on the students' attitudes toward problem-solving through discussions and questionnaires. This was supplemented by quantitative assessments to measure the improvement of problem-solving skills, with guidance from the Teaching for Robust Understanding (TRU) framework provided by DCU. Teachers also used a checklist to track students' problem-solving abilities during the workshops, observing aspects like reflection, question posing, and flexible thinking. Students were given roles such as Builder, Supplier, and Engineer to ensure shared responsibility during the project. A key task assigned to the two schools involved creating a canal boat and a bridge. One school was responsible for designing and programming the boat, while the other developed the bridge. The boat had to move, and the bridge had to open, all controlled by the Lego WeDo 2.0 software. The students then came together to combine their creations, showcasing their work at a Digital STEM event at Athlone Institute of Technology. In the second year, the project incorporated a digital portfolio system using the Seesaw platform.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf A key task assigned to the two schools involved creating a canal boat and a bridge. One school was responsible for designing and programming the boat, while the other developed the bridge. The boat had to move, and the bridge had to open, all controlled by the Lego WeDo 2.0 software. The students then came together to combine their creations, showcasing their work at a Digital STEM event at Athlone Institute of Technology. In the second year, the project incorporated a digital portfolio system using the Seesaw platform. Students were encouraged to upload videos, photos, and interviews, reflecting on their learning journey. The platform also facilitated peer assessment, with students recording interviews where they discussed what they enjoyed, the challenges they faced, and how they collaborated. Each school purchased additional iPads to enable this more dynamic and interactive approach to learning. Throughout the project, the teachers held regular meetings with their Oide TiE Advisor to address any challenges and share new ideas. These were crucial in maintaining the project’s progress and ensuring a collaborative atmosphere across the schools. "Cluster meetings were essential – either in person (better) or via Zoom (if location is an issue)." Teacher Reflections Teachers noted that the use of digital technology, particularly Lego WeDo, kept students highly engaged throughout the workshops. The division of roles within the student groups helped build fairness and encouraged participation from all members. The teachers found that stepping back and allowing students to explore the technology on their own often led to surprising creativity and innovation. Cluster meetings among the teachers were viewed as an essential aspect of the project, providing opportunities to refine the approach and generate new ideas. The teachers also reflected on the importance of qualitative data collection, such as student interviews and focus groups, in capturing the nuances of the students’ learning experiences. They highlighted the effectiveness of Seesaw in promoting self-reflection and peer assessment, which helped students to become more aware of their own thought processes. www.h2.ie 108 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The project faced significant challenges during the COVID-19 pandemic. The restrictions initially halted all in-person activities, and the interactive nature of the Lego sets was particularly difficult to maintain under social distancing rules. While the project resumed with some adjustments, the disruption was noted as a setback to its overall continuity. "Lego WeDo is an excellent resource for all types of learners – it kept the children engaged for the full hour of the workshop." "The children are not afraid of digital technology – sometimes it’s best to step back and let the students explore the technology themselves." Developing a Continuum of Computational Thinking and Programming Activities in a Cluster of Schools in Mayo/Sligo Schools Involved • Various schools in Mayo and Sligo, including both single-class and multi-class contexts. Project Overview The project aimed to develop a coherent and progressive approach to teaching computational thinking and programming across a cluster of schools in Mayo and Sligo. Using the Digital Learning Framework and the European Framework for the Digital Competence of Educators (DigCompEdu) as guidance, the project focused on building a shared understanding of computational thinking, integrating these concepts into the curriculum, and supporting teachers through mentoring and professional development. By pooling resources and collaborating, the schools promoted innovation and creativity in learning, teaching, and assessment, while also providing opportunities for students to engage in real-world problem- solving using digital technologies.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Using the Digital Learning Framework and the European Framework for the Digital Competence of Educators (DigCompEdu) as guidance, the project focused on building a shared understanding of computational thinking, integrating these concepts into the curriculum, and supporting teachers through mentoring and professional development. By pooling resources and collaborating, the schools promoted innovation and creativity in learning, teaching, and assessment, while also providing opportunities for students to engage in real-world problem- solving using digital technologies. Project Aims • Develop a shared understanding of computational thinking among teachers and students. • Integrate computational thinking concepts into various curricular areas in a structured and progressive manner. • Explore and implement teaching methodologies that enhance digital literacy and problem-based learning. • Provide coaching and mentoring support to teachers, as well as external resources when needed. Implementation The project began with establishing a shared understanding among teachers regarding what computational thinking entails and how it can be applied in primary schools. The schools worked together to define the core skills and concepts, developing a spiral continuum of activities that could be integrated with the primary curriculum. This ranged from using basic tools like BeeBots during early childhood learning to more advanced programming tasks with Lego WeDo and other digital platforms. Teachers worked collaboratively to create resources, lesson plans, and problem-solving tasks that would help integrate computational thinking across multiple subjects, including mathematics, literacy, and science. These resources were shared among the cluster via a Google Drive repository, which formed the basis of a whole-school plan for each participating institution. The project also involved extensive professional development for teachers, which included coaching, mentoring, and external training opportunities. Teachers were supported in embedding computational thinking and programming into their teaching practices, and the schools embraced hands-on digital learning activities that allowed students to evaluate and reflect on their experiences. www.h2.ie 110 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Throughout the project, there was an emphasis on real-world engagement, with some schools linking with industry partners and the broader community to enrich the learning experience. Students were encouraged to assess their own work and participate in peer assessments, becoming more discerning about the use of digital technologies in the process. "Many schools integrated the equipment with existing curricular areas, such as BeeBots during Aistear and Lego WeDo for Bricks Club." Figure 16: Group learning with BeeBots. Teacher Reflections Teachers noted the significant impact of the project on both teaching and learning. The collaborative approach to resource development and the shared understanding of computational thinking allowed schools to better integrate digital technologies into the curriculum. Teachers expressed that the project improved their confidence in using digital tools, enabling them to create more interactive and engaging lessons for their students. The professional development sessions were particularly valuable, providing teachers with the skills and knowledge to guide students through computational thinking activities effectively. Teachers also reflected on the challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic, which interrupted the planned face-to-face meetings and training sessions. However, the schools adapted by using digital tools like Google Workspace, email, and WhatsApp to continue collaborating and progressing the project remotely. Despite these challenges, teachers felt that the project helped them develop a stronger understanding of computational thinking and its application in the classroom. "Collaboration around a computer makes learning meaningful and supportive, enhancing creativity and the evaluative power of learners’ ideas." "Computational thinking is emerging as a key competence across all disciplines, combining problem-solving and design to create useful solutions." Digital GMP (Gortskehy-Meelickmore-Patrick) Schools Involved • Gortskehy National School • Meelickmore National School • St. Patrick’s De La Salle BNS Project Overview The Digital GMP project was a collaborative effort between three primary schools in County Mayo, aimed at enhancing digital literacy among both teachers and students. By pooling resources and sharing knowledge, the schools integrated digital technologies into various curricular areas, moving from teacher-led instruction to student-driven learning. The project fostered a digital community of practice, where teachers and students worked together on innovative digital projects, learning from each other and enhancing their digital skills in teaching, learning, and assessment. Project Aims • Move from teacher-led pedagogy to student-led knowledge creation through the use of digital tools. • Enhance digital literacy and promote innovative use of technology in teaching, learning, and assessment. • Collaborate across schools to develop and share best practices in integrating technology into the curriculum. • Foster creativity and engagement by allowing students to create their own digital content, such as presentations and projects. Implementation Each year of the project was divided into two iterations, focusing on different curricular themes. In the first year, the theme was Local History, with students researching their towns (Castlebar and Claremorris) and their schools over the past 100 years. Using tools like Google Slides, Adobe Spark, and Padlet, students created presentations and photo stories, incorporating research, interviews, and historical data. The project allowed students to collaborate in real- time, both at school and at home, using digital platforms to create and share their work. In the second year, the focus shifted to STEM, with students using Lego WeDo kits and Scratch to create robots and animations. The theme of Climate Change was explored, and students built windmills and other projects while documenting their progress using Adobe Spark and Google Slides. The third year introduced creative writing and literacy, with students creating digital storybooks using Book Creator, focusing on themes related to Mayo and local history. Collaboration was at the heart of the project, with teachers and students from all three schools working together through shared resources and platforms like Google Workspace. The schools also engaged external partners, including local historians and museums, to enhance learning experiences. "Collaboration was the main ingredient in this project. Working together to plan for the integration of technology into teaching, learning, and assessment was the key to our success." www.h2.ie 112 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Teacher Reflections Teachers reflected on the positive impact of the project in promoting collaboration and enhancing digital literacy. The shared use of digital platforms allowed schools to plan lessons, create resources, and share content seamlessly. Teachers noted that students became more engaged in their learning when they were given the opportunity to create their own content, leading to a shift from passive consumption to active knowledge creation. The project also highlighted the importance of flexibility and adaptability, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Dreams to Reality: Harnessing the Potential of the Digital World Schools Involved: • St. Patrick’s NS Loch Gowna • Breifne College, Cavan • Bruskey NS • Ballynarry NS • Lacken NS • Cavan Institute Project Overview The Dreams to Reality project was launched as part of the School Excellence Fund Digital initiative to promote digital literacy and computational thinking across both primary and secondary education. The collaborative project involved schools from primary through secondary levels, focusing on using digital tools like Lego WeDo, Minecraft, and coding to bridge the transition between primary and secondary school learning. Students engaged with technology to develop problem-solving skills, teamwork, and computational thinking, while teachers integrated modern teaching methods to support students' digital proficiency. The project encouraged peer tutoring and self-assessment to foster independent learning. Project Aims • Develop digital literacy and computational thinking from early primary education through secondary school. • Facilitate a smoother transition for students moving from primary to secondary school using technology. • Incorporate peer tutoring to enhance communication, teamwork, and problem-solving. • Track the benefits of early digital literacy on students’ progress as they advance through the educational system. Implementation The project commenced with a cluster planning phase in 2018, during which schools identified their individual strengths and expertise. Over the course of three years, students from junior infants to secondary level participated in various hands-on and digital activities aimed at developing computational thinking and digital skills. Primary students engaged with BeeBots and BlueBots, starting with simple programming tasks and advancing to more complex coding through tools like Scratch. Unplugged activities such as writing code for everyday tasks helped students understand the importance of precise instructions, which tied into digital activities like Minecraft and Lego WeDo. Senior students in primary schools were involved in creating digital projects using tools like Adobe Spark, which enhanced their presentation skills and allowed them to demonstrate their understanding of STEM topics. In the post-primary schools, the focus shifted to more advanced computational thinking exercises, including algorithm development and online coding tasks. Students explored various digital platforms like Microsoft Teams, Office 365, and coding software such as Scratch and Blockly. Peer tutoring sessions enabled older students to mentor younger ones, reinforcing the skills learned and fostering a collaborative learning environment. www.h2.ie 114 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The project also included several showcase events, where students from different schools gathered to share their work and experiences.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf In the post-primary schools, the focus shifted to more advanced computational thinking exercises, including algorithm development and online coding tasks. Students explored various digital platforms like Microsoft Teams, Office 365, and coding software such as Scratch and Blockly. Peer tutoring sessions enabled older students to mentor younger ones, reinforcing the skills learned and fostering a collaborative learning environment. www.h2.ie 114 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 The project also included several showcase events, where students from different schools gathered to share their work and experiences. One key event was the Showcase Day in 2019, where pupils from all participating schools presented their digital projects and visited Cavan Institute to see advanced digital technology in action. Another notable event was a visit to Combilift, where students observed the practical application of digital technology in industry, further emphasising the real-world relevance of their learning. "Students were excited to work with digital tools, and the collaboration between schools brought a new dynamic to their learning." Teacher Reflections Teachers involved in the project highlighted the positive impact of incorporating digital tools into the curriculum, noting how engaged and motivated the students were during the activities. They observed that the combination of unplugged and digital tasks helped students build essential problem-solving skills while maintaining enthusiasm for learning. The peer tutoring element was particularly effective in fostering leadership skills among older students, and teachers appreciated how the students took ownership of their learning through self-assessment and reflection. Teachers also mentioned the challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic, which temporarily disrupted the project. However, they acknowledged that the groundwork laid by the project in earlier years made the transition to remote learning smoother, as both students and teachers were already familiar with digital platforms such as Teams and Seesaw. "The unplugged activities were a great way to introduce students to computational thinking, and the results were evident when they transitioned to coding tasks." "The peer tutoring sessions gave older students the chance to solidify their learning, while younger students benefitted from the mentorship and guidance provided." Integration of Virtual Reality (VR) in 21st Century Teaching, Learning and Assessment Schools Involved • St. Mark’s SNS • Sacred Heart SNS • St. Thomas’ SNS • St. Anne’s Primary Project Overview This project involved the use of Virtual Reality (VR) to enhance teaching, learning, and assessment across multiple DEIS (Delivering Equality of Opportunity in Schools) schools. The aim was to provide students, especially those in disadvantaged areas, with access to immersive digital learning experiences that go beyond traditional classroom activities. The project focused on enhancing students’ proficiency in SESE (Social, Environmental and Scientific Education), SPHE (Social, Personal, and Health Education), Literacy, and Numeracy by incorporating VR into the curriculum. By exploring virtual environments, students engaged in interactive learning that was otherwise inaccessible. The initiative also promoted mindfulness and well-being activities, using VR to help alleviate stress and anxiety. Project Aims • Develop students’ proficiency in SESE, SPHE, Literacy, and Numeracy. • Use VR to provide unique learning experiences that enhance traditional teaching methods. • Incorporate mindfulness and well-being programmes into the learning process to support mental health. • Enable students to explore global environments and historical settings virtually, broadening their educational experiences beyond the local context. • Showcase student learning through digital tools such as Google Expeditions, Google Docs, and Google Slides. • Establish VR as a cost-effective, scalable tool for improving teaching, learning, and assessment in schools. Implementation The project began by integrating VR into everyday teaching activities.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf • Use VR to provide unique learning experiences that enhance traditional teaching methods. • Incorporate mindfulness and well-being programmes into the learning process to support mental health. • Enable students to explore global environments and historical settings virtually, broadening their educational experiences beyond the local context. • Showcase student learning through digital tools such as Google Expeditions, Google Docs, and Google Slides. • Establish VR as a cost-effective, scalable tool for improving teaching, learning, and assessment in schools. Implementation The project began by integrating VR into everyday teaching activities. Teachers selected virtual expeditions from Google Expeditions (later replaced by Expeditions Pro) and used them to enhance their lessons in various subject areas. Each school was equipped with VR headsets and a teacher tablet to control and guide students through immersive VR lessons. Students explored topics such as virtual archaeological digs, nature trails, and mindfulness activities. A unique feature of the project was the creation of a collaborative virtual tour of local landmarks in Tallaght. Using 360-degree cameras, teachers and students captured images and recorded information about the landmarks, creating an interactive virtual tour that could be shared across schools. The project was supported by Oide TiE which provided training and guidance to the participating schools. Regular meetings were held between the schools to share ideas, overcome challenges, and assess the project’s progress. www.h2.ie 116 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Google Workspace for Education was used to facilitate collaboration among teachers. Google Classroom, Google Drive, and shared virtual spaces allowed teachers to co-create lesson plans, share resources, and track the progress of the project. "We were fortunate to have a whole-class set of VR headsets, which made the teacher delivery of lessons more effective and exciting." Teacher Reflections Teachers reflected that VR added an exciting and engaging dimension to the learning experience, making lessons more interactive and accessible for students. The use of VR allowed students to gain insights that were previously unattainable through traditional teaching methods. Teachers appreciated the support provided by the Oide TiE and the collaborative nature of the project, which allowed them to share resources and strategies. Despite the success of the project, teachers acknowledged that the devices used for VR lessons had some limitations. Older devices tended to overheat, and the battery life and processing speed deteriorated over time. The need for updated technology was noted as a key consideration for future programmes. Teachers also emphasized the need for more VR content specifically designed for primary schools, as much of the content available during the project was limited. "Creating our own virtual tours using 360-degree cameras allowed students to take ownership of their learning, combining technology with creativity to explore their local environment in a new way." "VR expeditions brought topics to life, providing a way to engage students in immersive learning experiences that sparked curiosity and creativity." Project Links https://x.com/vrdub24 Figure 19: Lesson using VR in action. www.h2.ie 117 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 6. Machines and Mechanisms Schools Involved • Firies National School, Firies • St. Brendan’s National School, Blennerville • Murhur National School, Moyvane • Presentation Monastery N.S., Killarney Project Overview The Machines and Mechanisms project aimed to incorporate enquiry-based learning in STEM into the school curriculum. The initiative was supported by the School Excellence Fund, promoting collaboration among schools and the use of digital tools like LEGO, K’nex, and iPads. The project provided students with hands-on learning opportunities, encouraging them to work together as developers, designers, and problem-solvers. Through enquiry-based tasks, students were challenged to identify problems, brainstorm solutions, and apply critical and creative thinking.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf The initiative was supported by the School Excellence Fund, promoting collaboration among schools and the use of digital tools like LEGO, K’nex, and iPads. The project provided students with hands-on learning opportunities, encouraging them to work together as developers, designers, and problem-solvers. Through enquiry-based tasks, students were challenged to identify problems, brainstorm solutions, and apply critical and creative thinking. Teachers and students alike benefited from the collaborative approach, sharing best practices and resources across schools to build essential 21st-century skills. Project Aims • Actively engage students in enquiry-based learning to enhance problem-solving skills. • Increase student awareness and appreciation of STEM through practical, hands-on activities. • Use digital technologies to open up new forums of learning and collaboration. • Develop students' abilities to work as engineers, designers, and creators using modern tools. • Empower teachers to collaborate and share effective digital teaching practices, improving instruction and student engagement in STEM. Implementation The project began with meetings between lead cluster teachers and school management teams to align the initiative with the Department of Education's Digital Learning Framework. Teachers evaluated their current digital practices and developed a digital learning plan for each school. Digital school committees, consisting of teachers, students, and parents, were formed to guide the project and ensure effective use of resources. Resources like LEGO Education sets, K’nex, and iPads were procured and organised for use in classrooms. These tools facilitated enquiry-based learning, where students worked together to build and code mechanisms, such as cars, cranes, and cable cars. Students were assigned roles within their teams, such as builder, coder, and reporter. These roles were rotated so that all students gained a understanding of the various aspects of the projects. The progression of activities was carefully structured across different class levels. Junior and Senior Infants worked with LEGO Duplo, while older students engaged with more advanced sets like LEGO WeDo and LEGO Prime. The activities were linked to the curriculum and provided students with the opportunity to apply scientific, mathematical, and technological concepts to solve real-world problems. www.h2.ie 118 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Collaboration was key throughout the project. Schools shared resources, and teams of students worked together on tasks, documenting their learning using iPads and apps like Scratch, Seesaw, and Green Screen. Schools also partnered with external organisations, such as Liebherr and local train stations, to provide students with insights into STEM careers and real- world applications of the skills they were developing. "Children became developers, creators, and change-makers of technology through hands- on enquiry-based learning." Teacher Reflections Teachers expressed that the project had a significant positive impact on student engagement and learning. The combination of digital technology and hands-on enquiry-based activities encouraged students to think critically and work collaboratively. Teachers appreciated the opportunity to share resources and teaching strategies across schools, which enhanced their ability to integrate STEM into the curriculum. The rotating roles within student teams ensured that every child participated fully and developed a broad range of skills. Teachers also noted the importance of incorporating digital tools like Seesaw, which allowed students to document their progress and reflect on their learning. The project faced challenges due to the COVID-19 pandemic, but the strong foundation of digital literacy and collaboration enabled schools to adapt to remote learning more effectively. "Collaboration among schools and sharing resources enabled us to enhance teaching and learning in STEM." "Students explored real-world problems, using digital technologies to design solutions, which helped them develop critical thinking and creativity." Multimedia Cluster Project Schools Involved • Dublin 7 Educate Together (Lead School) • Presentation Primary School, George's Hill • St. Mary's Primary School • St. Paul's CBS, Brunswick Street • Gardiner Street Convent School Project Overview The Multimedia Cluster Project brought together five primary schools to enhance digital literacy through the use of collaborative digital technologies. The project aimed to integrate tools like Microsoft Office 365 and GSuite into the classroom, facilitating communication, creativity, and collaboration among nearly 1,400 students and 100 teachers. Pupils and teachers worked together across schools, creating digital content, sharing resources, and participating in video calls. A core goal was to use digital technologies to transform teaching, learning, and assessment practices across the participating schools, with a focus on inquiry-based learning and cross-curricular activities. Project Aims • Enhance the use of digital technologies in teaching, learning, and assessment. • Foster collaboration between teachers and students across schools. • Develop pupils' skills in using digital tools to create, share, and communicate. • Integrate technology into all curriculum areas, promoting a student-centred approach to learning. Implementation In the first year, teachers received training in using digital applications such as word processing, email, and content creation tools. Students were introduced to basic computer skills and used video calling to communicate with peers in other schools. The project encouraged an inquiry- based approach to learning, where students worked collaboratively on topics like local, national, and international issues. Each year group participated in a specific digital project: • Junior Infants learned basic computer skills. • Senior Infants created a "Growing" video project for science. • 1st Class completed a music sound recording project. • 2nd Class built a Virtual Art Gallery. • 3rd Class created a photo gallery. • 4th Class produced a video documentary. • 5th Class wrote and designed a newspaper/book. • 6th Class built websites. Despite the disruptions caused by COVID-19, the project continued into the second and third years, with students and teachers adapting to remote learning. Teachers collaborated via shared online spaces like Google Drive, and students used Seesaw for creating ePortfolios that tracked their learning progress over time. Some classes also used Scratch to create animations and games linked to their projects, which served as assessment tools. www.h2.ie 120 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 Applications such as Seesaw, Google Drive, and Microsoft Office 365 enabled students to create digital content and share their work with peers, teachers, and parents. Teachers used these tools to assess student progress, give feedback, and plan cross-curricular lessons that integrated digital technologies into all subject areas. Video calls, digital portfolios, and multimedia projects helped build a sense of community among students, despite the physical distance between schools. "Using technology in the classroom enabled our students to connect and work together, even when they couldn’t be physically present in the same room." Teacher Reflections Teachers reported that the project had a transformative impact on both teaching and learning. The use of technology encouraged greater collaboration among students and helped them develop skills in problem-solving, communication, and creativity.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Video calls, digital portfolios, and multimedia projects helped build a sense of community among students, despite the physical distance between schools. "Using technology in the classroom enabled our students to connect and work together, even when they couldn’t be physically present in the same room." Teacher Reflections Teachers reported that the project had a transformative impact on both teaching and learning. The use of technology encouraged greater collaboration among students and helped them develop skills in problem-solving, communication, and creativity. Teachers also observed improvements in student behaviour, self-confidence, and social skills, particularly when working on group projects. The continuous professional development (CPD) workshops helped teachers develop new digital skills, which they applied to their classroom practices. Challenges included the limited availability of devices due to COVID-19 restrictions, as well as the need to train new staff on the project's tools and processes. Despite these obstacles, teachers were able to integrate digital technology into their classrooms effectively, making learning more engaging for students. "A reduction in misbehaviour and an increase in pupils’ self-confidence were clear outcomes from using digital tools to enhance collaboration and communication." "Through the project, pupils learned to create digital content, which not only helped them develop IT skills but also fostered a love of learning and exploration." North-East STEM Champions Schools Involved • St. Mary’s Parish Primary School, Drogheda • Aston Village Educate Together National School, Drogheda • Scoil na gCailíní, Castleblayney Project Overview The North-East STEM Champions project brought together three primary schools to promote STEM education through the creation of a professional learning community of teachers and students. The project emphasised active collaboration and knowledge-sharing between educators, encouraging innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning, and assessment. By integrating hands-on STEM activities into the curriculum, students engaged in inquiry-based learning, developing key skills in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. Local businesses and STEM experts were also invited to participate, offering students real-world insights into STEM careers and inspiring them to pursue STEM-related fields. Project Aims • Promote the innovative use of digital technologies in teaching, learning, and assessment. • Build a collaborative community of teachers to share STEM lessons and best practices. • Engage students in hands-on, inquiry-based STEM lessons to develop skills for the modern workplace. • Foster connections with local industries and STEM experts to inspire students, particularly girls, to explore STEM careers. Implementation The project was coordinated across all three schools through regular meetings to assess progress and plan future activities. Teachers participated in continuous professional development (CPD) to enhance their skills in delivering STEM education. Oide TiE provided training on digital tools such as SeeSaw for recording learning and LEGO WeDo to link coding with science and construction. The schools engaged students in a variety of STEM activities, including coding with Scratch, using Bee-Bots for literacy and numeracy, and building robots with LEGO WeDo. Students participated in national STEM competitions, such as the Intel Mini Scientist, ESB Science Blast, and VEX Robotics. These activities helped students develop problem-solving and IT skills, while also sparking their interest in STEM subjects. A key element of the project was the involvement of local businesses and STEM experts, who visited schools to provide role models for students. By showcasing the real-world applications of STEM, the project aimed to inspire students, particularly girls, to see the relevance of STEM in their daily lives and believe in their ability to succeed in these fields.

STEAM Hub and Centre of Technology Excellence Schools Involved • St. Brendan’s Community School • St. Brendan’s Boys School • Mercy Primary School • Scoil Náisiúnta Osmann • Crinkhill National School • Gaelscoil na Laochra Project Overview The STEAM Hub and Centre of Technology Excellence project, based at St. Brendan’s Community School, aimed to establish a central hub for digital learning and STEAM activities, benefiting both primary and secondary schools in the surrounding community. The project introduced state-of-the-art technology, including Surface Go devices and Micro tools, and developed interactive learning environments such as the Mini DreamSpace 2, located at the school and in Birr Castle Museum. Through professional development provided by Microsoft, educators were equipped to integrate digital tools into their teaching, fostering creativity and critical thinking among students. The project also encouraged collaboration between schools, local community resources, and industry partners to deliver a dynamic and future-oriented learning experience. Project Aims • Provide students with access to digital technology to enhance their learning experiences in STEAM subjects. • Create a hub for STEAM education to serve local primary and secondary schools, fostering collaboration and innovation. • Offer professional development to teachers, enabling them to integrate digital tools into their teaching practices. • Promote partnerships with local institutions such as Birr Castle Museum to extend learning opportunities beyond the classroom. Implementation The project began by providing participating schools with Surface Go devices and Micro technology, enabling students to engage in hands-on STEAM activities. Teachers received professional development through Microsoft to ensure the effective use of these digital tools in the classroom.

Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Project Links https://stbrendanscsbirr.ie/Birr-STEM-Cluster/Index.html https://www.birrcastle.com www.h2.ie 125 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 10. Technology to Collaborate, Create, and Innovate Schools Involved • Cork Educate Together Secondary School • Firhouse Educate Together Secondary School • Malahide Portmarnock Educate Together Secondary School • Clonturk Community College Project Overview The "Technology to Collaborate, Create, and Innovate" project focused on establishing a Maker Space in each participating Educate Together secondary school. The project, developed in partnership with Make Create Innovate and Oide TiE, aimed to support student learning in physical computing through the use of digital and creative technologies. By fostering a constructivist and inquiry-based learning environment, the project encouraged students to take ownership of their learning, work collaboratively, and explore innovative solutions using technologies such as Raspberry Pi, Arduino, and 3D printers. The Maker Spaces provided an interactive platform for cross-curricular activities, encouraging students to integrate various subjects like science, history, geography, and digital literacy into their projects. This hands-on approach facilitated the development of problem-solving, critical thinking, and digital literacy skills, with students creating projects that spanned multiple disciplines. Project Aims • Establish sustainable Maker Spaces in each participating school. • Train staff to use Maker Space technologies and develop cross-curricular activities. • Empower students to deliver workshops and mentor younger peers in using the technologies. • Foster collaboration between schools, enabling the exchange of skills and ideas among teachers and students. Implementation Junior Cycle students were introduced to the Maker Space through dedicated workshops held throughout the school year. Each class participated in three two-hour workshops, during which they worked in groups to explore themes such as mapping Medieval Cork, soil moisture sensors, and 3D printing. Students researched the use of various technologies, such as Raspberry Pi, micro:bits, and 3D printers, and applied this knowledge to design and build projects. Collaboration was a key feature of the project, with students working together in groups, reflecting on their work, and discussing how the projects integrated multiple subjects. Teachers from different subject areas collaborated to develop interdisciplinary lessons that made use of the Maker Spaces, enriching the learning experience. Students were encouraged to continue using the Maker Spaces during lunch breaks, fostering independent exploration and innovation. Role of Technology Technology played a central role in this project, with the Maker Spaces equipped with tools like microcontrollers, 3D printers, and programming languages such as Scratch, Python, and Arduino. Students used these technologies to prototype designs, program interactive projects, www.h2.ie 126 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 and create solutions to real-world problems. Projects ranged from creating soil moisture sensors to designing renewable energy systems. The Internet of Things (IoT) was also explored, with students developing projects that connected sensors and actuators to the internet. The Maker Spaces democratized access to advanced technologies, providing students with the opportunity to experiment with tools that were previously out of reach due to cost. This hands- on learning environment encouraged students to become creators, not just consumers, of technology. "Maker Spaces provide an opportunity for students to experiment with technology, taking ownership of their learning while developing practical and creative skills." Figure 25: Students create an interactive map of Cork. Teacher Reflections Teachers noted that the project had a significant impact on student engagement and learning. The hands-on nature of the Maker Spaces allowed students to explore and experiment with technologies in a way that deepened their understanding of various subjects. The collaborative aspect of the project fostered a sense of ownership among students, as they were able to see the tangible results of their work.

Using Digital Mobile Technologies for Teaching, Learning, and Assessment of Fundamental Movement Skills in Physical Education Schools Involved • St. Colman’s NS, Derrinabroc • Scoil Íosa, Carracastle NS • St. Nathy’s College, Ballaghaderreen • Scoil Mhuire, Brusna NS Project Overview This project aimed to enhance the teaching and learning of Physical Education (PE) across four primary schools and one secondary school by integrating digital mobile technologies. The initiative focused on using constructivist pedagogies, allowing students to engage in peer assessment and feedback while developing fundamental movement skills (FMS). The use of digital portfolios and mobile devices enabled students to record, assess, and reflect on their progress, fostering a sense of ownership over their learning. Teachers also collaborated to improve their own digital competencies and share best practices within the cluster. Project Aims • Improve the teaching and learning of PE through the use of digital technologies. • Develop students’ fundamental movement skills using video recording and peer assessment. • Create digital portfolios to track students’ progress over time. • Facilitate professional development for teachers in using digital tools in PE and beyond. • Strengthen the connection between primary and secondary schools by promoting collaboration. Implementation The project was organised with support from Oide TiE, which provided training in the use of iPads, Google Workspace, and Seesaw for creating digital portfolios. Teachers used the "Move Well, Move Often" booklet and website to introduce students to fundamental movement skills. During PE lessons, students recorded their performances using mobile devices and assessed their peers based on key skill objectives highlighted in videos. Digital resources were shared through apps like Seesaw at the primary level and Showbie at the secondary level. Students compiled videos of their progress into ePortfolios, which allowed both students and teachers to monitor development from the exploration to the mastering stage. This approach aligned with the proposed Physical Education Leaving Certificate subject, helping secondary students develop assessment skills that could be applied at higher levels. Cluster meetings were held to share lesson videos, ideas, and progress, enabling ongoing reflection and evaluation of the project. Schools collaborated closely, sharing resources and digital content to create a cohesive learning experience across different age groups. "Integrating technology into PE has allowed us to marry activity with modern digital tools, producing really worthwhile outcomes."

Recording and reviewing videos of FMS allowed students to observe their own performance and provide constructive feedback to peers. This visual approach to learning helped students understand their strengths and areas for improvement more clearly, making the learning process more interactive and engaging. The project also improved teachers' confidence in using digital tools, thanks to the ongoing professional development and collaboration with colleagues. Teachers felt that digital portfolios were an invaluable resource, particularly during the COVID-19 pandemic, when remote learning required new ways of assessing students' progress. However, challenges included the initial learning curve for teachers unfamiliar with digital technologies and the logistical difficulties of recording and editing videos, particularly in large classes. Despite these challenges, the benefits of integrating digital tools into PE were clear, with both students and teachers expressing a greater enjoyment and engagement in the subject. West Cork Robotics Schools Involved • Togher National School, Dunmanway, Co. Cork • Drinagh National School, Co. Cork • Cappabue National School, Kealkil, Co. Cork Project Overview West Cork Robotics was a collaborative initiative between three primary schools in West Cork, in partnership with the School of Computer Science and Information Technology at University College Cork (UCC). The project aimed to explore and demonstrate the innovative use of robotics and coding in enhancing teaching, learning, and assessment. The project aimed to improve digital skills for both teachers and students by integrating digital technologies into the Irish Primary Curriculum. Students engaged in hands-on activities focused on creativity, collaboration, and computational thinking. Project Aims • Provide opportunities for active learning, allowing students to explore mathematical and spatial concepts using digital technologies. • Foster collaboration and teamwork through group-based learning activities involving coding and robotics. • Develop computational thinking and problem-solving skills in students through coding languages and robotics. • Enhance digital literacy and encourage creativity and innovation by allowing students to design, build, and test solutions to various challenges. • Demonstrate effective use of digital technologies in teaching and learning, sharing best practices with other schools. Implementation In the first year, the project introduced high-level block-based programming languages such as Scratch and Scratch Junior to students across all participating schools. These tools allowed students to create interactive stories, games, and animations, enhancing their computational thinking and problem-solving skills. Younger students also worked with BeeBots, simple programmable robots designed for early learners, to explore sequencing, directional skills, and teamwork. The second year of the project expanded to more advanced robotic kits such as LEGO WeDo 2.0, Photon Robots, and Spike Prime, providing students aged 7-11 with the opportunity to build and program robots.

Title: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf
Content: SEF_Digital_and_STEM_Final_Evaluation_Report_Sept_2025.pdf Implementation In the first year, the project introduced high-level block-based programming languages such as Scratch and Scratch Junior to students across all participating schools. These tools allowed students to create interactive stories, games, and animations, enhancing their computational thinking and problem-solving skills. Younger students also worked with BeeBots, simple programmable robots designed for early learners, to explore sequencing, directional skills, and teamwork. The second year of the project expanded to more advanced robotic kits such as LEGO WeDo 2.0, Photon Robots, and Spike Prime, providing students aged 7-11 with the opportunity to build and program robots. These activities helped students develop a deeper understanding of programming concepts like loops, sensors, and event sequencing, while encouraging creativity through the design and testing of their robots. Collaboration was a key element, with students working in small groups to complete tasks. As the project progressed, students also engaged with more complex technologies, including the LEGO EV3, VEX IQ robotics kits, and 3D printing with Tinkercad. These tools were introduced to help students develop critical thinking, design thinking, and technical skills. Cross-curricular integration was encouraged, connecting robotics and coding activities with subjects like mathematics, science, and engineering. The project concluded with a showcase event where www.h2.ie 130 SEF Digital Final Evaluation Report 2025 students presented their projects and shared their learning experiences with the school community. "Block-based programming allows young students to learn the fundamentals of coding and computational thinking without worrying about syntax errors or complex code." Figure 27: Programming a Mindstorms Robot. Teacher Reflections Teachers reflected that the project significantly enhanced student engagement, fostering creativity, innovation, and collaboration. The introduction of block-based programming languages like Scratch allowed younger students to develop a strong foundation in coding, while older students thrived in using more advanced robotic kits to solve complex challenges. Teachers also highlighted the importance of professional development, as many of the technologies were new to them. Training and support from UCC and external facilitators were essential for building confidence in integrating robotics and coding into the curriculum. Teachers also noted the long-term impact of the project, with students demonstrating greater interest in STEM subjects and continuing to apply computational thinking in their daily learning. The COVID-19 pandemic presented challenges, limiting in-person workshops and group activities. However, the schools adapted by focusing on remote collaboration and virtual learning. Teachers expressed hope that future face-to-face events and showcase opportunities could resume. "These tools help students develop computational thinking, critical thinking, design thinking, and problem-solving skills." "Robotics and coding enhanced student engagement, encouraged creativity and innovation, and fostered collaboration and teamwork."
